## CHAPTER 5: “Lines That Don’t Lie”

Tiffani’s logbook gained weight in her hand without gaining pages.

She kept it dry anyway. Habit. Oilcloth wrap, tie twice, tuck into the inner pocket of her field satchel. Even with Soulpulse dust in the air, even with mortar grit everywhere, even with the city’s breath turning coarse after the Piercing, paper still mattered. Paper held numbers. Numbers held memory without needing permission.

The Kiln Corridor ran crooked through the Glass Quarter, ovens on one side, cracked stone on the other. Firehouse vents still exhaled when they could. Some were dead. Some were choked. The ones that worked made the corridor warmer than the lanes that fed into it, and warmth made people gather. Warmth made it harder to keep clear passages. Warmth made a load path more complicated.

Tiffani stopped at the first hold site of Tide 2, Kindling. A small archway that had already been shored twice. The crew had used timber braces and iron straps. The straps were too thin and too long; the timber was warped. The arch carried weight from a broken upper walk and redirected it into a corner abutment that had lost its footing when the ground rotated during the Piercing.

She put her palm on the stone and did not pretend it was calm.

Her fingers felt micro-vibration, a fine pulse that traveled through her wrist and up into her forearm. She steadied herself with the other hand on the strap and counted the cadence. Not a sound. A repeatable rhythm. Repeatable meant real.

She opened the logbook to the current section and wrote:

**Hold Site 1 — Kiln Corridor / West bend / Oven 14 vent line**  
**Structure:** arch + corner abutment  
**Material:** stone + mortar, iron strap add-on  
**State:** creep present, low amplitude, constant  
**Human traffic:** high, uncontrolled  
**Response:** additional brace recommended; traffic control required  
**Note:** baseline vibration increased by one subdivision since Tide 1, Quench

She underlined **traffic control** twice. Not because it would happen, but because she wanted it documented that it should have happened.

She moved down the corridor in measured Strides, stopping at each shift in the paving where the stone had split. She marked deviations on her corridor map: hairline fissures, buckled joints, slumped grout. She checked the same points she had checked during the first Tides after the Piercing, and she compared. Some cracks were stable. Some had widened by less than a finger’s breadth. Two had opened enough for grit to pour through when she tapped them with her knuckle.

When she reached the second hold site, she did not have to ask whose work it was.

The copper-gold stain was faint but visible in the stone. Not a dye. Not paint. A residue in the surface where heat and light had passed through fractured material and left the cracks cleaner than the surrounding dust. Tiffani knelt, wiped away grit with the side of her hand, and traced the path with her fingertip.

Stress lines. Real ones. The route was not random. The glow had flowed along the fracture network that carried load.

She wrote again.

**Hold Site 2 — Kiln Corridor / Junction to Underface stair**  
**Structure:** stair throat + supporting lintel  
**State pre-hold:** recorded sag by two subdivisions, Tide 1 late Quench  
**State post-hold:** stable within half subdivision, Tide 2 Kindling  
**Indicator:** copper-gold residue along primary fractures  
**Hypothesis:** activation follows immediate need, follows load path, prefers existing fracture routes

She paused with the pen hovering.

Hypothesis needed testing. Hypothesis needed failures documented as carefully as successes.

That was the point of the logbook. Not praise. Not story. Pattern.

A shout from deeper in the corridor pulled her head up. A crew dragged a brace into position. Someone argued with a woman carrying a bundled child. The child did not cry. The woman’s face was too empty for crying.

Tiffani closed the logbook and moved.

The next hold site was not in the corridor itself but in a side lane where a glass furnace had collapsed. The oven’s dome had cracked and dropped inward, spilling ash and broken brick. The roof beams above had bowed. The beam ends had torn into their sockets. A crew had propped them with scavenged columns and wedges. The wedges were too dry. Dry wedges slipped.

She examined the beam ends and the sockets. The stone around the sockets had sheared, and the shear line had the same faint copper-gold residue as the lintel site.

Two points of residue now. Two points meant the thing existed beyond a single event. Two points did not mean enough.

Tiffani’s hand shook once while she drew the shear line in the logbook. She braced her wrist on the page, stabilized, and continued.

She did not call it noise now.

—

By the time Tide 2 reached Pouring, she had six hold sites mapped and three confirmed sites with copper-gold residue. She had two recorded failures where the residue was absent and the structure continued to creep despite shoring.

Failures mattered. Failures defined the boundary.

She chose an observation position above the Saint Merro junction approaches, where the main walk sloped down toward an intersection of arches, columns, and ramps that carried people between the Crown’s inner districts and the Glass Quarter camps. The junction had become a throat. Everything moved through it.

Tiffani set up her glass-sight on a stable parapet. The lens had survived because she had wrapped it in wool and stored it in a padded case during the Piercing. The lens was not perfect anymore; a hairline crack ran along one edge, but the crack did not distort within the central field. Good enough for measuring.

She aligned the sight with a known stone mark on the far arch and checked baseline. The arch carried slightly low. The deviation was within one subdivision, but the direction was consistent with the creep she had logged at other sites.

She wrote: **Junction arch A — low by 0.8 subdivision; trend stable**.

Then she waited.

Waiting was not passive. Waiting was data collection with legs still.

When the shoring crew set a brace and the arch answered with a small shift, she recorded the shift. When a line of refugees pushed through and the load increased, she recorded the increased vibration. When an aftershock ran under the stone and the arch responded with a groan that made the lens tremble, she recorded that too.

The man she was tracking arrived from the Underface stair at the junction’s left side.

Jhace Torrin moved with the gait of someone who had spent most of his life under stone. He watched the joints first, then the crowd, then the shoring. He spoke to the crew chief with minimal words. He carried a hammer but did not swing it. His right hand held the handle too tightly, and his fingers did not settle into a stable grip.

His tremor showed even at distance. Small. Persistent. Not a shaking fit. A baseline instability that affected fine motor control. Tiffani had seen it in Tide 1. It was worse now.

He set his palm against the arch base. He did not close his hand around anything. Skin to stone. Contact. Intent. Need.

The copper-gold light did not flare theatrically. It moved.

Tiffani watched it through the glass-sight as it ran along existing fractures and into a joint that had begun to separate. The arch shifted upward by half a subdivision. Then it held.

Jhace pulled his hand away quickly, as if the stone had burned. His shoulders tightened. He hid the tremor by clenching his hands into fists and putting them against his thighs. The veins in his forearms stood out darker than they should, with faint lines that followed no normal pattern. Copper-gold lines under skin.

Cost. Visible. Physical.

Tiffani recorded:

**Hold Event — Junction arch A**  
**Trigger context:** crowd load + aftershock + audible joint movement  
**Method:** palm contact at arch base; no tool grip during activation  
**Effect:** immediate stabilization + lift of 0.5 subdivision  
**Residue:** visible line along fracture network; copper-gold  
**User condition:** tremor increased post-use; vein-lines visible; grip avoidance observed

She did not write his name in the logbook. Not yet. Names invited narrative. Narrative invited distortion. She wrote **User A** and left it that way.

Then the Resonance Regulation Office arrived.

They came in pairs, wearing clean coats that did not match the dust in the air. The coats were heavy cloth, the cut formal, the collars stiff. Their boots were polished. Their faces carried the calm of people who believed paper could still control stone.

Tiffani recognized the insignia stitched into the collar seam. A simple mark, newly standardized: intersecting arcs around a central dot. Resonance. Regulation. The office that had been a committee during the first Tide after the Piercing was learning to walk.

They did not approach the shoring crew first. They climbed to the parapet where Tiffani stood.

One of them was older, with a ledger tucked under his arm. The other was younger and watched the crowd more than he watched Tiffani.

“Observer,” the older one said. He did not ask if she was. He told her what he saw. “We were told you’ve been tracking the new phenomenon.”

Tiffani did not answer immediately. She closed the logbook, secured the tie, and set the glass-sight cap in place. She did not move away from the parapet.

“You were told correctly,” she said.

He smiled as if the correctness belonged to him. “The Resonance Regulation Office is consolidating reports. We can provide paper, ink, guards for your work sites. We can provide access to restricted zones. In return, we require your records. All of them.”

The younger one shifted his stance. He watched Jhace below with interest that did not register as concern.

Tiffani kept her voice even. “My records are not for trade.”

“They are not trade,” the older man said. “They are public safety. This phenomenon is dangerous. It must be tracked by an authorized body.”

“It is being tracked,” Tiffani said. “By me. With methods that do not depend on your authorization.”

The older man’s smile thinned. “You misunderstand. We are not requesting. We are instructing. We have authority from the Crown’s administrative chambers to collect all resonance-related observations.”

“Authority does not stabilize arches,” Tiffani said. “Braces do. Correct marking does. Competent hands do. Your papers do not hold weight.”

The younger one’s eyes narrowed. “You sound uncooperative.”

“I sound accurate,” Tiffani said.

The older man leaned forward slightly. “You will be required to register your involvement and the names of any resonance users you observe.”

Tiffani looked down at the junction. Jhace had stepped away from the arch and was speaking to a crew member. His hands were still clenched. He was trying to keep them steady by force.

“No,” she said.

The older man blinked once. “No?”

“No,” Tiffani repeated. “I will not hand over records. I will not provide names. I will not allow you to turn a survival mechanism into a ledger of control.”

The older man’s face hardened. “That mechanism is unstable. It must be regulated.”

“Then regulate your own response,” Tiffani said. “Assign traffic control. Provide sound timber. Stop moving people through failing arches without supervision.”

The younger one’s jaw tightened. “You will regret this. The Office will remember who obstructs.”

Tiffani’s pulse picked up. Not fear. Yet. Anticipation.

“I will remember,” she said, “who tried to take data by threat.”

The older man lifted his ledger as if it were a weapon. “We can compel.”

“You can attempt,” Tiffani said.

They left without taking the logbook, but they left with a look that promised return.

Tiffani did not feel triumphant. She felt the burden of attention shift toward her. Attention changed outcomes. Outcomes changed costs.

She wrote a brief note after they were gone:

**RRO approach — Tide 2 Pouring**  
**Offer:** resources with control strings; demand names + full records  
**Response:** refused  
**Risk:** escalation likely; concealment of User A identity required

—

The second pressure arrived before Tide 3 reached Kindling.

It came with water.

Not rain. Water in barrels, hauled by teams who still had access to functioning mains and reservoirs. Water meant power in the Glass Quarter. Water meant control over kiln operations, over refugee cooking fires, over mortar mixing, over basic survival.

A Water-Guild factor walked with the barrels. He wore a coat that had been cleaned recently. His hands were not callused in a way that suggested shoring work. He carried a small seal stamp on a chain at his belt.

Tellis.

Tiffani recognized him from council meetings before the Piercing, when the Glass Quarter argued for repair budgets and the Water-Guild argued for tariff adjustments. He had always spoken politely. Politeness did not make an offer benign.

He climbed to the parapet without asking permission and glanced at the glass-sight as if it were an amusing tool.

“Observer,” he said. “You’re out here watching the arches settle.”

“I’m measuring,” Tiffani said.

Tellis smiled. “Measuring, watching. Both require distance. I respect it.”

He nodded toward the junction. “The Crown will decide what gets rebuilt. The Guilds will decide what gets supplied. The Glass Quarter is not winning that argument.”

“Noted,” Tiffani said.

Tellis leaned his elbows on the parapet, careful not to dirty his sleeves. “You have influence right now. Not formal. Practical. People listen when you point at a crack and say it will fail. People move when you tell them to move.”

Tiffani waited.

“I can make that influence profitable,” Tellis said. “You could advise the Water-Guild’s retreat plan. There are districts that should be abandoned. There are routes that should be redirected. There are assets that should be salvaged before the rubble gets claimed by other hands.”

“Your retreat plan,” Tiffani said, “requires calling occupied structures unsalvageable.”

“It requires realism,” Tellis said. “The Piercing changed the city. The Glass Quarter was fragile before. Now it will bleed resources. Better to reallocate. Better to move the ovens that can move and let the rest fall.”

“People live in those ovens now,” Tiffani said.

Tellis’s smile did not change. “People live in many unsuitable places. The Guild does not control that.”

He shifted closer. His voice lowered slightly. “I’m not asking you to lie. I’m asking you to choose which truths matter. If you declare certain arches beyond repair, traffic routes shift. Supplies shift. That shift can be directed. We can protect the routes that serve production.”

Tiffani looked at him directly. “You want my measurements as a tool for profit.”

Tellis lifted his hands in a gesture that implied innocence. “Profit funds rebuilding.”

“Profit funds consolidation,” Tiffani said. “Rebuilding requires allocation by need.”

Tellis’s eyes sharpened. “Need does not keep guild halls standing. The Crown will not feed everyone. We will.”

“You will feed those who serve you,” Tiffani said.

Tellis’s smile thinned. “You’re making an enemy out of someone offering stability.”

“I am refusing,” Tiffani said. “Stability cannot be bought by abandoning the unstable.”

Tellis straightened. “You’re sentimental.”

“I’m methodical,” Tiffani said. “My method includes human load.”

Tellis glanced down at the junction again, at Jhace moving through the crowd with tremoring hands. “That man is a liability. He will get someone killed. Better to let the Office take him.”

Tiffani’s throat tightened. Not fear. Anger classified as tension.

“I will not trade him to you,” she said, surprised by the clarity of the sentence even as she spoke it.

Tellis’s gaze flicked back to her. “Trade him?”

“Trade the data,” Tiffani corrected. “Trade the name. Trade the work. No.”

Tellis’s face cooled. “You will regret being alone.”

Tiffani watched him leave. The barrels went with him, down the parapet steps, toward the crowd that would follow water the way fire follows air.

Pressure from the Office. Pressure from the Guilds. Different angles. Same intent.

Control.

She wrote:

**Guild approach — Tide 3 Kindling**  
**Offer:** profitable retreat advisory; redirect supply routes via structural condemnation  
**Response:** refused  
**Risk:** Water-Guild obstruction likely; political pressure rising

Her hand shook again, stronger than before. Not because of the Guild. Because she had spoken his relevance aloud.

Him.

User A.

Jhace Torrin.

She should not know his name, but she did. Names traveled in crisis even when she tried to keep them out of paper. A crew member had shouted it once. Someone had cursed it. Someone had said it in gratitude, softly, after a wall held.

Names were unavoidable. The question was what they did to the work.

—

Marisse found her at the third hold site of Tide 3, Pouring.

Marisse did not move with the careless speed of people who believed the world would be stable again. She walked as if each Stride needed confirmation. She scanned cracks in stone automatically, her gaze never resting.

Her face was thinner than before the Piercing. The vigil had eaten her.

“You’ve been recording,” Marisse said. Not a question.

“Yes,” Tiffani said.

Marisse crouched beside the corner abutment and touched the stone lightly with two fingers. “This joint is worse than your last mark.”

Tiffani checked the mark. Marisse was correct. The separation had widened by a fraction. Enough to matter.

“I missed that change,” Tiffani said.

Marisse’s jaw tightened. “That is how it starts.”

Tiffani did not ask what Marisse meant. She already knew.

The pre-Piercing catastrophe had been in a different district. A kiln hall collapse blamed on material fatigue. Twenty-three people had died under stone and brick. Marisse had been pulled out alive. She had named the sound before the collapse. She had said it was not noise. The investigation had said she was anxious. Tiffani had accepted the investigation’s comfort because comfort was easier than doubt.

Noise had not killed Marisse. Marisse had survived. Noise had killed twenty-three others. Or the choice to call signal noise had.

Tiffani’s pulse picked up again. Not fear. Guilt classified as pressure.

Marisse looked at her. “You’re doing the thing again.”

“The thing,” Tiffani said, “is measuring.”

“The thing is staying on the edge,” Marisse said. “Watching someone else take the weight because you can count it from here.”

Tiffani’s fingers tightened on her logbook. “Counting it from here is how we learn what it is.”

Marisse’s eyes flicked to the copper-gold residue line on the stone. “We learned enough to know it costs him.”

“Yes,” Tiffani said. “That is in the record.”

Marisse stood. “Record does not stop him.”

Tiffani’s mouth went dry. Metallic taste. Early warning.

“He is moving from hold site to hold site,” Marisse continued. “He does not rest. He does not eat when the crews offer. He does not stop when his hands fail. He is becoming a single point of failure.”

Tiffani swallowed. She did not deny it because denial was not accurate.

Marisse’s voice lowered. “You remember the twenty-three.”

Tiffani’s throat tightened. “Yes.”

“You remember what it did to you to be wrong,” Marisse said. “And you remember what it did to me to be right and unheard.”

Tiffani looked down at her logbook. Lines. Numbers. Residue marks. Tremor amplitude estimates.

She looked at Jhace in the distance, moving through dust with hands he tried to control by force.

“I’m not wrong this time,” Tiffani said.

Marisse’s face did not soften. “Then stop behaving as if you can fix it by watching.”

Tiffani’s mind ran through objections. Risks. Institutional attention. The Office. The Guilds. The danger of getting close to a phenomenon the Crown wanted to own.

Then her mind ran through the other list.

Cost. Tremor. Vein-lines. Increasing frequency of activation. Increasing duration of recovery. The pattern she had logged from Tide 1 to Tide 3 did not curve gently. It bent.

Isolation accelerated decline. A single person trying to hold a city failed.

Tiffani’s fingers stopped shaking. That was the moment she recognized her decision had already been made. Her body had accepted it before her mind gave it words.

“Where is his forge?” she asked.

Marisse’s eyes narrowed. “You do not know?”

“I know roughly,” Tiffani said. “I want the door.”

Marisse pointed toward the Underface stair throat, past the kiln corridor bend. “Down two levels. Old ironwork bay. He keeps it hot even now.”

Tiffani nodded once. “Thank you.”

Marisse’s hand caught her wrist. Grip firm, not gentle. “Do not go in and talk. Do not go in and plead. Go in with proof. He will hear proof.”

Tiffani looked at her. “I will.”

Marisse released her. “And if you call it noise again—”

“I won’t,” Tiffani said.

Marisse turned back to the abutment and put her fingers against the joint, listening through touch.

Tiffani opened her logbook and wrote a final line at the bottom of the current page:

**Decision — Engage. Anchor the work to method before User A becomes the next collapse.**

She tied the oilcloth wrap around the book, tightened it, and stood.

The sky seam cut the distance above Saint Merro as a pale line that did not match the rest of the sky. It was there even when she did not look at it. It did not need her attention to exist.

Tiffani adjusted the strap of her satchel, turned toward the Underface stair, and began walking toward Jhace Torrin’s forge with the logbook pressed against her ribs.

---

## CHAPTER 6: “Weight That Won’t Yield”

Jhace felt the junction fail before he saw it.

The stone under his boots changed tone. Not sound. Weight. A shift in the way vibration traveled through the paving, a deepening pulse that did not match the crowd’s footfall. The junction always carried too much. Since the Piercing, it carried too much without its old margins.

A runner grabbed his sleeve near the Underface stair throat. “Torrin. Arch A. It’s moving.”

Jhace didn’t ask which arch. There were only so many that mattered at the junction, and this one mattered. He pushed through bodies, kept his shoulder low, and moved toward the main intersection where arches met ramps and the crowd funneled into a single passage.

He saw it then. The arch’s crown had dropped. Not far. Less than a hand’s breadth. Enough to change the load path. Enough to pull the abutments outward and make every crack in the joints relevant.

A crew had already thrown up timber shoring. Two vertical posts, one diagonal brace, wedges driven hard. It was the right shape for the wrong situation. The timber bowed under compressive load it wasn’t sized for. The wedges had begun to spit out dust.

“Back,” Jhace said to the closest cluster of refugees.

No one moved. They were cold. They were tired. They were trapped between a failing arch and the open lane where aftershocks still ran under the stone.

Jhace lifted his hammer and pointed at the base of the arch. “That joint goes, this whole throat goes. Move.”

A woman with a boy on her hip stared at him. Her eyes were dry and fixed. The boy’s face was turned inward, pressed to her shoulder.

Jhace swallowed and stepped past her, closer to the shoring crew.

“Who’s lead?” he asked.

A man with mortar on his forearms nodded. “Me. We’re set. We’ll add another brace.”

“Your braces are too long,” Jhace said. “They’ll bow and kick out. You need shorter spans under the crown and a clamp at the base.”

“We don’t have clamps,” the man said.

Jhace looked around. Salvage piles, scattered iron straps, broken brackets, bent nails that wouldn’t hold in soft stone. Too much damage. Too little useful material.

The arch answered with another small drop. Dust shed from the mortar seam. The crowd made a sound that wasn’t words.

Traditional shoring was not going to outrun the failure.

Jhace’s hands shook. The tremor never stopped now. It lived in his wrists. It lived in the muscles that used to obey without question. When he tried to grip the hammer tighter, the tremor sharpened, and the handle jittered in his palm.

He set the hammer down on a flat stone block because he did not trust his grip.

The runner was still there, breathing hard. “Do the thing,” the runner said. “Do the glow.”

Jhace’s mouth tightened. He hated that the city had already made it into a request. He hated that it had become an expectation.

He stepped toward the arch base and reached out.

“Wait.”

The voice cut through the junction noise with precision, not volume.

Jhace turned his head.

Tiffani stood on the near ramp, a satchel slung tight, a stick of charcoal in her hand. Dust streaked her coat. Her hair was tied back hard. Her eyes were on the arch, not on him.

She moved down the ramp with measured Strides, stopped near the base, and knelt without hesitation. She pressed the charcoal against the stone and drew a line that followed a visible crack. Then another, intersecting. Then a short mark at a joint that had begun to separate.

“Primary fracture network,” she said, and her words were clipped in a way that meant she was naming, not performing. “The load is not centered. It is traveling along this route.”

Jhace stared at the marks. They were accurate. They matched what his fingers had already begun to sense in the stone, but he hadn’t put it into lines yet.

“Torrin,” she said, still not looking at him. “If you spread across the whole base, you waste. You need to target the joint that’s opening. Here.”

She tapped the charcoal tip on the joint. The sound was small. The meaning was not.

Jhace’s heart hammered once, hard enough to make his hands shake more.

He did not want her here. He did not want a watcher. He did not want anyone close enough to turn his failure into someone else’s data.

But the arch dropped again. Dust fell in a thicker curtain, and someone screamed.

Jhace stepped in beside her.

“Keep people clear under the crown,” Tiffani said to the crew chief. “Two Strides back. Now.”

The crew chief blinked, then shouted. “Back! Back from the arch!”

Bodies shifted. Not enough. But some.

Tiffani looked up at Jhace. “Palm contact. On my mark. You will push into this joint, not into the entire arch.”

“How do you know what I—” Jhace started.

“Because I’ve been recording what happens,” she said. “And because stone does not hide where it breaks.”

Jhace swallowed the argument. He didn’t have time for pride.

“Mark,” he said.

Tiffani placed her left hand flat against the stone just below the opening joint. Her right hand held the charcoal, poised above the crack network.

“Not there,” she said, shifting her palm half a hand-width. “Here. Direct contact with the joint interface. You need the shortest path.”

Jhace forced his right hand to steady and set it over her hand, skin to skin and skin to stone. The contact was wrong in a way that had nothing to do with propriety. It was wrong because it made the work shared. Jhace had been doing this alone.

The arch gave a hard shudder. An aftershock ran under the junction. The timber brace bowed. The wedge spit out grit.

Jhace’s stomach clenched. The crowd’s sound rose. His mind flashed the memory he never invited—bodies, a collapsed span, the count that lived behind his ribs.

Protect. Hold.

He pushed the intent into his palm.

Heat flared, immediate, sharp. Copper-gold light spread under his skin and into the stone where his hand met the joint. It did not flood everywhere. It followed the line Tiffani had marked, sliding into the crack network that carried load.

The arch’s vibration changed tone again. The deep pulse smoothed into something more uniform. The crown stopped dropping.

Tiffani’s charcoal moved fast. She traced the glow’s route as it traveled, adding marks that anticipated the next stress shift. “Here,” she said. “It’s pulling into the left abutment. Push there next if it slips.”

Jhace’s teeth clenched. The tremor fought him. The heat in his palm intensified, and the copper-gold lines climbed higher in his forearm than they had during Tide 1. His fingers cramped. His grip wanted to fail.

He shifted his left hand to the point she indicated, never fully releasing contact. He kept his right hand pressed. He pushed again.

The glow answered.

The joint tightened. Not healed. Not repaired. Stabilized. Held in a new alignment that resisted the load that had been tearing it open.

The crowd flowed under the arch in a sudden surge as people realized it was not collapsing in this instant. The shoring crew shouted and reset braces tighter, working in the margin Jhace and Tiffani had created.

“Hold,” the crew chief yelled.

Jhace’s knees threatened to buckle. He kept them straight by force.

The arch did not move again.

Tiffani lifted her hand from the stone first, pulling away cleanly. Jhace’s hand stayed a fraction longer because he did not trust the stability without him. Then he forced himself to release.

The copper-gold glow faded from the stone surface, leaving residue lines that traced the fractures in a map that made his stomach turn. It was too clear. Too honest. It showed every weakness.

Jhace stepped back. His right hand shook hard enough that he could not close his fingers. He tried anyway. The tremor spiked and ran up into his elbow. The copper-gold vein-lines on his forearm were brighter, more extensive, and they did not fade when he looked away.

He swallowed bile that rose from his gut and tasted iron at the back of his tongue. Not blood. Not yet. Just strain.

Tiffani watched his hands without flinching. Her gaze moved over the vein-lines with the same expression she used on cracks in stone.

“Your tremor is worse than Tide 1,” she said.

Jhace’s jaw tightened. “I know.”

“You’re using too broad a push when you act alone,” she said. “You waste contact area. You increase cost. I’ve seen it.”

“Seen it,” Jhace repeated, sharp.

Tiffani did not blink. “Recorded it.”

“Stop,” Jhace said. He did not know what he meant by it. Stop watching. Stop writing. Stop making his failure into a graph.

Tiffani’s voice stayed even. “If you want to keep holding structures, you will need to stop pretending you can do it without method.”

Jhace’s hands shook harder. He shoved them into his pockets, useless gesture because the tremor kept going.

“You came to my forge,” he said, because that was what this was now. He could feel the boundary shift. “You’re here.”

“I’m here,” Tiffani said. “Because your cost curve is not stable. Because the city is asking you to be a brace made of flesh.”

Jhace stared at her. He wanted to tell her she didn’t understand. He wanted to tell her to leave. He wanted to tell her that he had no choice.

Instead, he heard himself say, “You marked the right joint.”

Tiffani nodded once. “Stone answers when you ask the right question.”

It was not comfort. It was competence.

Jhace looked back at the arch. The shoring crew had reset wedges and added a clamp scavenged from a broken stair railing. The clamp sat crooked but held. The crowd had thinned. People moved through the junction in smaller clusters now, less frantic.

The work had succeeded. People were alive because it had.

Jhace’s tremor did not care.

He turned back to Tiffani. “If you’re going to keep doing this,” he said, and the sentence surprised him because it assumed continuation, “you need to be where the failure is. Not on a parapet.”

“I agree,” she said.

Jhace’s throat tightened. He could feel the cost in his bones, the way his muscles refused fine control. He could also feel something else: the relief of not being the only one reading the stone.

He nodded toward the Underface stair throat. “My forge is down there.”

Tiffani adjusted her satchel strap. “I know.”

Jhace started walking, and he did not tell her to follow. He did not need to. Her footfalls came behind him with measured Strides, steady cadence, close enough that he could hear the change when she stepped on cracked stone and corrected her weight.

The junction held.

And for the first time since the Piercing, Jhace felt a different kind of margin open in the work: not more power, not less risk, but shared load.

---

## CHAPTER 7: “Quiet Calculations”

Tiffani spread her maps across the observatory data chamber table and did not let herself look at the sky seam through the high slit windows.

Looking did not change the seam. Recording did.

The chamber smelled of dust and lens oil. The floor was stone. The table had been reinforced with iron straps after the Piercing because even the observatory had shifted. The straps were new enough that they shone in places where hands had touched them repeatedly.

Pella sat across from her with his own ledger open. He had ink on his fingers and a fatigue line at the corner of his mouth that had deepened over the last few Tides.

“You’re back early,” he said.

“I came to compile,” Tiffani said.

“You came to obsess,” Pella said, but his tone held no mockery. He watched her place the logbook at the center of the table with care, as if it were fragile glass.

Tiffani unwrapped the oilcloth and opened to the first page after the Piercing. She laid a straightedge along the margin and began transferring points to the larger grid map pinned to a board beside the table.

Each point represented a hold event. Each mark carried a coded set of notes: structure type, activation trigger context, effect magnitude, and observed cost indicators.

She did not write Jhace’s name on the public grid. She used **User A**. She used the same code she had used since Tide 1. The code mattered because codes could be defended if the Office pushed.

Pella tapped his pen against his own ledger. “How many now?”

Tiffani counted without pausing her work. “Seventeen logged across five hold sites. Nine direct stabilizations. Three partial. Five failures under non-critical conditions.”

Pella exhaled slowly. “That is more than the council understands.”

“The council understands panic,” Tiffani said. “Panic is not the same as comprehension.”

She drew the curve line on a secondary sheet: a simple axis grid she had made for herself. Horizontal: sequence of activations. Vertical: observed cost severity, converted into subdivisions based on tremor amplitude and vein-line persistence.

The first activation—the forced one during the Piercing—had been the baseline event. Cost was high but contextual. Everything was high during Striking. She had treated it as an outlier until she had more points.

Now she had points.

Tremor baseline at rest: increased by a measurable amount after each use. Vein-lines: appearing for longer durations post-use, then persisting faintly even without use. Recovery time: longer. Grip capacity: reduced. Adjacent symptoms: nausea, gait compensation, increased reliance on palm contact rather than tool grip.

Tiffani plotted the points.

The line did not rise steadily.

It rose, then rose faster.

She stopped with the pen hovering, stared at the curve, and checked her conversions. She recalculated two points, then three. She adjusted one value down by half a subdivision to account for observational uncertainty. The curve still bent.

Not linear.

Compounding.

“Pella,” she said.

He looked up. “That tone means you found something.”

“I found the thing I did not want,” Tiffani said.

Pella stood and came around the table, leaning over her chart. He did not touch the paper. He respected boundaries of work.

He traced the curve with his eyes. “This is not straight.”

“No,” Tiffani said. “It accelerates.”

Pella’s brow furrowed. “You’re saying each use costs more than the last by more than a constant amount.”

“Yes,” Tiffani said. “The cost is not additive. It multiplies.”

Pella stared at the curve longer. “That means a few more uses and he loses function entirely.”

Tiffani’s fingers tightened around the pen. “He is already losing function.”

Pella’s gaze lifted toward the maps. The hold sites marked in charcoal and ink clustered around the junction throats and the refugee corridors. “And the city will keep asking.”

“Yes,” Tiffani said.

She took a breath and turned to a second sheet where she had begun a projection. She had avoided it until now because projection invited fear. Now projection invited planning.

If the curve held, and if activation frequency continued at the current rate, the next threshold—what she had begun to call **Second** for her own internal classification—approached soon. Not a named stage. Not a doctrine. A practical boundary where physical function failed enough that holding anything became impossible.

She wrote: **Second threshold proximity — high**.

Pella’s voice lowered. “What do you do with this?”

Tiffani did not answer immediately. She looked down at her logbook again, at the entries that included the RRO approach and Tellis’s offer, at the note she had written after talking to Marisse.

Engage. Anchor the work.

She had engaged. She had been at the junction. She had watched Jhace’s hands shake harder than before. She had watched the vein-lines persist after the glow had faded from the stone.

She had seen the curve in his body without needing the chart.

Now she had proof.

“Method,” Tiffani said.

Pella blinked. “Method?”

“He does not target efficiently when alone,” Tiffani said. “When he targets with marks, he spends less contact area. The glow follows stress lines more directly. The effect is cleaner. The cost may reduce.”

“That’s a hope,” Pella said.

“It is a hypothesis,” Tiffani corrected. “Hypotheses can be tested.”

Pella sat back down slowly. “And if the hypothesis fails?”

Tiffani’s mouth went dry again. Metallic taste.

“Then the curve still exists,” she said. “And the city still asks. And the Office still watches. And the Guilds still offer retreat plans. And a single man becomes a choke point.”

Pella’s eyes held concern now, not as a performance but as a calculation of risk. “You could hand this to the Office and let them control him.”

Tiffani’s pulse jumped. Anger classified as heat in the face.

“No,” she said.

“They would lock him behind a ledger,” Pella said. “At least he would be alive.”

“At least he would be owned,” Tiffani said. “And the work would become a tool for policy instead of survival.”

Pella’s gaze flicked to the sky slit window. The seam was visible if he shifted his chair slightly. He did not look long. “We don’t have the luxury of ideals.”

Tiffani tapped the curve line with the end of her pen. “This is not an ideal. This is a structure failing. When a structure fails, you shore it properly. You do not hand it to a committee and hope it stops failing.”

Pella’s mouth tightened. “He will hate you for telling him.”

“He already hates being watched,” Tiffani said. “I’m not going to persuade him with sympathy. I will show him the curve.”

Pella leaned forward. “And if he refuses?”

Tiffani looked at the curve again. Then at the hold site map. Then at the list of refugees moving through the junction throats.

“If he refuses,” she said, “I keep showing up with marks. I keep reducing waste. I keep logging cost. I keep building proof until the city’s survival method does not rely on a single set of shaking hands.”

Pella’s expression shifted. “That is not a small plan.”

“No,” Tiffani said.

Pella was quiet for a long moment. Then he gave a short, tired exhale that might have been a laugh in a different life. “People who notice patterns get tied to impossible mentors.”

Tiffani’s mouth twitched in something close to a smile. “Watching is easier if you don’t fall over.”

Pella’s eyes softened slightly. “Be careful.”

Tiffani closed the logbook and tied the oilcloth wrap. “I am careful. That is why I’m going.”

Pella’s brow rose. “Now?”

Tiffani stood and slid the logbook into her satchel. “Now. Before the next aftershock turns this curve into a body.”

She left the data chamber and moved through the observatory corridors that had once felt permanent. Now she checked the joints as she walked. She counted the subdivisions of sag in the stair throat. She noted the new iron straps. She measured even in transit because transit had become part of the hazard.

Outside, Saint Merro carried itself with visible strain. Dust hung in pockets between buildings. Soulpulse residue glinted faintly on a few repaired seams where the light had traveled and left evidence. People moved with loaded bundles. Shoring crews worked without pause. The Glass Quarter camps pressed against every warm vent and every stable wall.

Tiffani crossed the Kiln Corridor again, past Oven 14’s vent line, past the shored arch she had logged in Tide 2, Kindling. She watched the brace hold. She watched the crowd flow. She recorded nothing because she had no new data there today.

Her destination was not a hold site.

Her destination was a forge under stone, where heat could be controlled even when the world above could not.

She descended the Underface stair throat two levels, following the route Marisse had given her. The air grew warmer. The stone underfoot grew more consistent. The noise of the crowd dulled, replaced by the lower resonance of tunnels and ironwork.

She reached a door reinforced with an iron crossbar. Heat seeped through the cracks around it. Not uncontrolled heat. Managed heat. Forge heat.

Tiffani adjusted her satchel strap, placed her palm briefly against the door to feel the vibration behind it, and waited one breath for her pulse to settle.

Not fear. Determination.

She lifted her hand and knocked.

[ASSISTANT]
## INTERLUDE I: The Registry  
*Tide 3, Quench — Crown-side annex, Saint Merro*

The annex used to be a storage hall for broken panes and miscut stone. It still smelled like old lime and powdered glass, only now the shelves held ledgers instead of scrap. Lanterns burned low along the walls, their light caught and scattered by dust that never settled. Every surface had a thin grit on it—proof the Piercing had reached even here.

The older man set his ledger down with care, as if impact alone could crack policy.

He did not wear a clean coat anymore. The coat had been clean at the first forming of the Office, when authority still pretended it was above the city’s dirt. Now the collar was gray with stone dust, and the cuffs bore ink smears from too many revised orders.

He opened the ledger to a page marked with a strip of red cloth and pressed his thumb to the margin, holding it flat.

“Read it again,” he said.

Across the table, the younger man did not sit. He paced in short, tight Strides that never took him far enough to feel like escape. His eyes kept sliding toward the slit window at the far end of the annex, where the sky seam could be seen if you leaned the wrong way and lifted your chin.

He stopped pacing long enough to pick up a bundle of reports tied with twine.

“Hold event at the junction throat,” he said, scanning the top sheet. “Witnesses say the stone brightened. Copper-gold. Then the arch lifted.”

“Names?” the older man asked.

“Refugees are inconsistent,” the younger said. “Crews disagree. One says Torrin. Another says ‘the Underface hand.’ Another says ‘the shaking one.’”

The older man’s mouth tightened. “That’s a name.”

“It’s a likely name,” the younger corrected.

“Likely becomes sufficient when roofs fall,” the older man said.

He turned a page and ran a finger down a column of marks. Each mark was a pinprick of ink beside a place name, an arc, a throat, a stairwell, a retaining wall.

Seventeen marks, if the reports were honest.

Honesty in crisis was always suspect. But repetition was harder to fake.

“The phenomenon is spreading,” the older man said. “Or we are finally seeing it.”

The younger man looked back down at his bundle. “Or more people are trying it.”

“Or more people are being forced into it,” the older man said. “Because our shoring crews are exhausted and our stockpiles are empty and the city keeps insisting it can stand on will.”

He tapped the ledger with the pad of his finger. “We need a registry.”

The younger man’s gaze snapped up. “We need braces.”

“We need both,” the older man said. “Braces for stone. Braces for behavior.”

The younger man made a sound that was not agreement. “A registry won’t hold a wall.”

“No,” the older man said. “But it will tell us which wall is held by a hand instead of timber.”

“That hand is what keeps people alive,” the younger man said, and the sentence surprised him enough that he paused afterward as if he had heard someone else speak through his mouth.

The older man watched him. “And hands can be guided.”

The younger man’s jaw flexed. “And chained.”

The older man did not deny it. He turned another page.

“Bring him in,” he said, as if the sentence had already been written and he was simply reading it aloud.

The younger man’s eyes flicked to the slit window. The seam was a pale line, too straight to be a cloud and too present to be ignored. It made every argument feel smaller.

“We don’t know what it does to them,” the younger man said. “The cost. The strain. The tremor—”

“We know enough,” the older man said. “We have three reports of post-use collapse. We have one report of seizures. We have one report of blood from the nose. We have six mentions of ‘vein-lines’ that weren’t there before.”

He leaned forward. “If it kills him in the street, the phenomenon doesn’t disappear. It becomes myth. Then it becomes hunger. Then you have people burning their own insides out trying to imitate a thing they don’t understand.”

The younger man swallowed. He did not look away. “So you lock him in a room and call it safety.”

“So we keep him alive,” the older man said. “And we keep the city from tearing itself apart chasing an answer.”

The younger man turned the top report over and scanned the back. “There’s an observer. Observatory. She’s collecting data.”

The older man’s eyes narrowed. “The one who refused us.”

“Yes,” the younger man said. “Tiffani.”

The older man held still, then wrote the name on a separate slip of paper. One name. A small, sharp thing.

“Find her,” he said.

The younger man hesitated. “She’s not using it. She’s measuring.”

“She’s adjacent,” the older man said. “Adjacency becomes influence. Influence becomes obstruction.”

The younger man’s mouth tightened again. “We could recruit her.”

The older man’s pen paused. “Or we could remove her.”

The younger man’s stomach turned, and he hated that his first thought was not refusal but calculation—how hard removal would be, how much risk it would add, how many eyes would notice.

The older man set the pen down. “We will attempt recruitment first,” he said, as if granting mercy rather than choosing strategy. “Because we need her records.”

“And if she refuses again?”

“Then we do not leave her free to hand the city an unregulated method,” the older man said. “Nor do we leave her free to sell the method to a guild.”

As if summoned by the word, the door at the annex’s far side opened.

A man stepped in carrying a seal stamp on a chain at his belt. His coat was clean enough to look insulting in this room. Two porters followed him, hauling a barrel between them on a wooden skid.

The barrel sloshed when they set it down.

Water.

The younger man’s throat tightened with an instinctive, ugly envy. He had been drinking recycled runoff for three Tides.

“Registrar,” the clean-coated man said, inclining his head at the older man. “You sent for me.”

The older man did not stand. “Factor Tellis.”

Tellis’s eyes flicked over the ledgers and the stacks of reports. “You’re busy.”

“Everyone is busy,” the older man said. “Few are useful.”

Tellis smiled as if the insult were part of the ritual. “And you believe the Water-Guild can be useful.”

“I believe you can be supplied,” the older man said, nodding toward the barrel. “Which makes you mobile. Mobility makes you capable of shaping outcomes.”

Tellis rested a hand on the barrel’s rim. “The Guild shapes outcomes already. Water goes where it is paid to go.”

The older man’s gaze did not change. “This is a Crown annex.”

Tellis’s smile thinned by a hair. “Then water goes where it is ordered to go.”

The younger man watched the exchange, hearing the lie in both statements and recognizing it as the city’s last shared language.

Tellis glanced at the younger man, then at the reports. “You’ve been hearing rumors.”

“We’ve been logging events,” the older man said.

“Ah,” Tellis said. “Logging. That implies a future where paper still matters.”

“It will,” the older man said. “If the city still stands.”

Tellis leaned forward slightly, lowering his voice as if the dust might listen. “Your Office is new. Your authority is still wet. You need allies.”

The older man’s pen lifted again. “We need compliance.”

“Call it what you like,” Tellis said. “I call it coordination.”

He tapped the barrel. “You want the resonance user. The one who holds arches with his palms.”

The younger man went still.

Tellis saw it and smiled wider, pleased to be correct. “Everyone wants him,” Tellis said. “Because everyone is afraid.”

“We want him alive,” the older man said.

Tellis’s eyes glittered. “Alive is negotiable.”

The older man’s voice hardened. “Alive is not negotiable.”

Tellis lifted his hands in a placating gesture. “Of course. Of course. I’m simply saying—if you do not secure him, a guild will. If not ours, then another. And guild hands are not gentle.”

The younger man spoke before he could stop himself. “How do you even plan to secure him? He appears in crowds. He vanishes into Underface. He doesn’t wear a sign.”

Tellis’s gaze slid to him. “Doesn’t he?”

The older man’s eyes narrowed. “Speak clearly, Factor.”

Tellis reached into his coat and produced a small wrapped bundle. He set it on the table between the ledgers and unwrapped it with careful fingers.

Inside lay a shard of glass.

Not ordinary kiln glass. This piece had a pale filament through it, a straight white thread caught in the material as if the sky seam itself had been pulled into a pane and cooled there. The shard hummed faintly—not loud, not audible in the way a voice was, but perceptible in the teeth.

The younger man’s skin prickled.

Tellis watched their reaction with quiet satisfaction. “Recovered from a fall site,” he said. “Aftershocks sometimes shake loose fragments from the higher tiers. This landed near an oven yard. When we brought it close to a certain… residue line…”

He trailed off, letting the implication do the work.

The older man’s hand did not reach for the shard. He stared at it, then at Tellis. “You tested it on someone.”

Tellis did not deny it. “A worker. Minor. Not your Torrin. He tried to brace a lintel. His hands shook afterward so badly he couldn’t drink without spilling. We brought the shard near him and it sang.”

“Sang,” the younger man repeated, disgust and fascination braided together.

Tellis shrugged. “Hummed. Vibrated. Responded. Choose your word.”

The older man’s voice stayed flat. “Where is the worker now?”

Tellis’s smile returned, smooth and practiced. “He is under Guild care. We fed him. We gave him water. We gave him a warm place to lie down.”

“You detained him,” the older man said.

“We protected him,” Tellis corrected. “From the crowd. From your Office. From other guilds. From himself.”

The younger man’s fists clenched at his sides. The shard’s hum seemed to creep deeper into his bones the longer it sat exposed.

The older man finally reached out—not to touch the shard, but to pull a cloth over it again, muting its hum to a faint pressure in the air.

“You brought this as leverage,” the older man said.

“I brought this as proof,” Tellis said. “And an offer.”

The older man’s eyes held him. “Speak.”

Tellis rested his hands on the table edge, leaning in as if sharing a secret. “We can help you find Torrin. The Underface workers gossip. The shoring crews talk. The camps keep track of the ones who save them.”

“And you want what,” the older man asked.

Tellis’s voice warmed, persuasive. “You want a registry. I want a map.”

The older man’s pen hovered. “Explain.”

Tellis spread his fingers, palms down. “Districts will be abandoned. You know this. Braces are finite. Timbers are finite. Hands are finite. Water is finite.” His eyes flicked to the barrel again. “If the Crown wants to keep production alive, it must decide which districts receive reinforcement and which districts are allowed to crumble.”

The younger man’s stomach sank. He had heard the argument in murmurs, in alley curses, in quiet conversations that ended when someone stepped too close.

Tellis continued. “The Water-Guild can supply the reinforced districts. In return, we need authorization to withdraw from certain zones without being accused of hoarding. We need protection for our routes. We need a stamped mandate.”

The older man’s gaze stayed cold. “You want permission to abandon people.”

Tellis’s smile did not falter. “I want permission to save what can be saved.”

The younger man could not hold his tongue. “And you want Torrin as a tool to justify your choices.”

Tellis turned his head toward him, expression mild. “A tool? No. A symbol. Symbols move crowds faster than wedges.”

The older man’s pen touched paper. He wrote a single word, slow: **BARGAIN.**

Then he looked up. “We do not bargain with human beings,” he said.

Tellis’s eyes sharpened. “You already do. You just call it ‘allocation.’”

Silence filled the annex, heavy as wet stone.

At the far end of the room, behind a partial screen of hanging cloth, someone made a small sound.

A cough.

It was not the dry cough of dust. It was wet. It carried a metallic edge.

The younger man’s head snapped toward the sound.

He had noticed the screen earlier and dismissed it as storage covering.

Now it moved.

A figure shifted behind it, and a hand appeared—thin, shaking, the fingers curled as if they could not fully open. Copper-gold vein-lines ran under the skin like a map of fracture routes.

The hand tried to grasp the cloth, failed, then pressed the palm against the stone floor to steady itself.

The cough came again.

Tellis did not look guilty. He looked satisfied, as if revealing this piece was the final step in closing a deal.

“You brought him here,” the older man said quietly.

Tellis nodded. “I brought you your proof.”

The younger man took a step forward before he knew he was moving. The captive’s eyes appeared through a gap in the cloth—bloodshot, fever-bright, focused with the desperate clarity of someone in pain.

The captive whispered, voice hoarse. “Don’t… make me… do it again.”

The sentence landed in the younger man’s chest like a weight.

The older man stood.

He moved around the table and drew the screen aside with one hand, exposing the captive fully.

A worker, young but already worn, sat slumped against the stone wall. His wrists were wrapped in stained bandage. His forearms tremored constantly. The copper-gold lines under his skin pulsed faintly in the lantern light, as if remembering heat.

His lips were cracked. A smear of dried blood marked one nostril.

The older man crouched, eyes scanning. “How many activations,” he asked.

The captive’s mouth worked. “Three,” he whispered. “Maybe. I don’t know. It… happens when it’s falling.”

“And after,” the older man said.

The captive swallowed hard. “After I can’t… hold anything. Can’t eat. Can’t sleep. My teeth—” He broke off, coughing again, shoulders shaking.

The older man’s face did not soften, but something in his posture changed. Not pity. Responsibility, or the appearance of it.

He stood and faced Tellis. “You will leave him here.”

Tellis raised his brows. “He’s under Guild care—”

“He is under Crown annex jurisdiction now,” the older man said. “If you want your mandate, you will not contest it.”

Tellis’s smile returned, sharp. “And the mandate?”

The older man held his gaze. “You will submit your district maps. You will submit your water route lists. You will submit your fall-site recovery logs. Including where you found that shard.”

Tellis’s eyes narrowed. “That is a lot to ask.”

“It is less than you would lose if I accuse you publicly of detaining a resonance-afflicted worker for leverage,” the older man said.

Tellis’s jaw tightened, then eased. “Fine,” he said. “Coordination.”

The older man turned to the younger man. “Send word. We begin sweeps at Underface access points. We prioritize forge bays. We look for copper-gold residue. We look for tremor. We look for vein-lines.”

The younger man hesitated. “And the observer? Tiffani?”

The older man’s gaze hardened. “Watch her. Do not touch her yet. If she goes to Torrin, we follow. If she recruits him, we take both.”

The younger man’s stomach twisted. “Alive,” he said, because he needed to hear the word.

“Alive,” the older man confirmed.

Behind them, the captive’s shaking hand pressed harder into the stone as if he could anchor himself there, as if contact with something solid might keep him from sliding into the same compounding curve that Tiffani had drawn in ink.

Tellis stepped back toward the door, satisfied, his clean coat barely stirring dust. “Saint Merro loves its ledgers,” he said lightly. “Just remember: ledgers don’t drink.”

When the door closed behind him, the annex felt colder.

The older man looked down at the cloth-wrapped shard on the table, then at the captive trembling against the wall, then at the ledger open to its column of ink marks.

“Record this,” he said to the younger man. “New classification. Resonance-afflicted. Cost visible. Prognosis uncertain.”

The younger man picked up his pen.

His hand shook—not from Soulpulse, not from copper-gold lines, but from the quiet realization that the registry they were building was not a net to catch danger.

It was a net to catch people.

And the seam in the sky did not care what they called it.

- **Jhace Torrin** (target of the registry sweep)  
- **Tiffani** (the observatory observer they want to watch/track)

---

## INTERLUDE I: Corlexi — System vs Chaos  
*Tide ~6–7 — Crown administrative chambers, Saint Merro*

Corlexi could not stop seeing the joints.

Even when he stared at a blank wall, his eyes found the seam where stone met stone, the slight lip where mortar had shrunk, the hairline crack that ran like a thin breath between blocks. The Piercing had changed the city’s posture, and the city’s posture had changed his mind. He no longer saw “a room.” He saw load.

He stood in the Crown’s administrative chambers with a bundle of petitions pressed to his ribs and tried to keep his hands steady. The chamber was built for authority: tall pillars, polished stone, iron braces disguised as ornament. It had been designed to make people feel small enough to obey.

Now Corlexi felt the opposite.

The chamber was a structure. Structures could fail. That meant structures could be corrected. That meant authority was a matter of bracing, not blood.

The air tasted of powdered glass and damp ink. Lantern light struck dust motes and made them sparkle like grit in a crucible. Somewhere above, beyond the slit windows, the sky seam cut the day into two truths, a pale line that refused to behave like air.

Corlexi kept his gaze off it. Looking at the seam made his skull hum.

He moved to the petition table and laid his bundle down in a perfect square, edges aligned with the table’s iron inlay. Alignment mattered. It made the breath inside him slow.

A clerk to his left was speaking too quickly, voice thin with exhaustion. “If we don’t record the incidents, we can’t allocate the brace crews—”

A different clerk snapped back, “We can’t allocate what we don’t have!”

Their words came in ragged bursts, but Corlexi heard the pattern underneath: pressure rising, resources thinning, people trying to turn chaos into columns of ink.

A scribe dropped a ledger. The thud echoed through the chamber floor, and Corlexi flinched as if the sound had struck his teeth. The echo came back wrong—its return skewed, the way a hammer ring goes sour when the anvil has hairline fractures.

He looked down.

One of the floor stones at the chamber’s center had shifted. A fraction. Enough to change the return of sound.

No one else noticed. No one else cared. They were drowning in paper.

Corlexi crouched without asking permission, pressed two fingers to the stone seam, and felt the vibration.

Not an aftershock. Not a crowd tremor. A slow creep under constant load. The chamber was settling, and the settlement was uneven.

He straightened, breath shallow. The sensation in his fingers made his mind fill with lines—angles, braces, counterforces—an entire invisible scaffolding overlaying the room.

He blinked hard to clear it.

It did not clear.

The chamber’s pillars became vectors. The benches became weights. The people became moving loads, their steps shifting stresses through the stone the way water shifts through a cracked channel.

Corlexi’s throat tightened. Sensory overload pressed against his skull. Too many lines. Too many forces. Too many failures waiting to happen.

He focused on one thing: the table’s iron inlay. Straight. Reliable. A simple rectangle in a world that had stopped behaving.

A shadow fell across the table.

Corlexi looked up and saw Corvin.

Corvin’s coat was dusted like everyone else’s, but his posture was unbowed. He carried a roll of diagrams under one arm and a narrow ledger in his hand, the ledger’s spine newly reinforced with iron. Corvin’s gaze swept the chamber the way Corlexi’s did—seeking joints, seeking weak points—but Corvin’s eyes did not flinch from the pressure. He seemed to feed on it.

Corvin placed his diagrams on the table and unrolled them with practiced control.

Lines filled the parchment: district arcs, tunnel maps, brace allocations, chokepoints marked in thick ink. It was the city reduced to geometry.

Corlexi’s pulse steadied. Geometry was honest.

“We need a registry,” Corvin said, voice calm enough to quiet the clerks near him without demanding silence.

One of the clerks rubbed at their face with ink-stained hands. “We’re already drowning.”

“We’re drowning because you’re counting wrong,” Corvin said. “You’re counting damages. You need to count causes.”

A second clerk frowned. “The Piercing is the cause.”

Corvin’s mouth twitched, not quite a smile. “The Piercing is the event. The cause is what people do in response.”

He tapped the narrow ledger. “We have reports of copper-gold stabilization. Contact-based. Conditional. Cost visible. Untrained use is spreading through the camps.”

Corlexi’s stomach tightened. Copper-gold. The light that crawled through stone when desperation turned into something else.

Corvin continued, “If we do not record who can do it, where it happens, and what triggers it, we will lose control of the phenomenon. Guilds will buy it. Crowds will worship it. Desperate hands will burn themselves out trying to copy it.”

A clerk whispered, “It saves lives.”

Corvin’s gaze sharpened. “So does a brace. So does traffic control. So does obedience. Saving lives is not a license to become ungovernable.”

Corlexi’s fingers curled against the table edge. He could almost see the shape of Corvin’s plan as a structure: a net of ledgers and checkpoints and stamped permissions, laid over the city like an iron cage meant to keep the walls from falling outward.

A cage also held people.

Corlexi’s head began to hum again, the hum rising toward pain. The chamber’s lines multiplied, each word from Corvin adding another brace in Corlexi’s mind until the room felt webbed with invisible iron.

He breathed through his nose and tried to anchor to the table’s rectangle.

Corvin turned slightly, and his gaze landed on Corlexi—not as a person, but as a component.

“You,” Corvin said. “You’ve been quiet.”

Corlexi’s mouth was dry. “I’m a clerk,” he said, because that was the safest label. “Quiet is my function.”

Corvin studied him for a beat. “Quiet clerks see more than loud ones. What did you just check?”

Corlexi realized his fingers still carried stone dust from where he had touched the floor seam. He brushed it off too quickly, betraying himself.

“The floor,” Corlexi said.

Corvin’s eyes flicked down, then back up. “And?”

“It’s creeping,” Corlexi said before he could stop. “Uneven settlement. The center stone is shifting. The echo return is wrong.”

The nearest clerks stared at him as if he had spoken another language.

Corvin did not look surprised. He looked pleased.

“Good,” Corvin said. “You can hear structure.”

Corlexi’s pulse spiked. Hearing structure was not a promotion. It was a hook.

Corvin rolled one of the diagrams closer to him. “Then help us stabilize the system.”

System.

The word landed in Corlexi’s bones like a hammer blow. The city had always been a system. The Piercing had turned it into a stress test. Now people like Corvin were building new systems out of fear and ink and hunger for control.

Corlexi’s vision flickered.

For an instant, he saw the chamber as a perfect lattice—pillars, beams, ledgers, bodies—each part assigned, each load path controlled, each chaotic motion pinned into place. In that lattice, the seam in the sky was not a wound.

It was a line of instruction.

A rule cut into the world: **shape it, or it breaks.**

The vision vanished, leaving behind the hum and a thin ache behind his eyes.

Corlexi swallowed. He did not understand what had just happened. He did not understand why geometry now felt like prophecy.

But he understood one thing with brutal clarity:

If chaos continued unchecked, the city would fall.

And if the new systems were built wrong, the city would stand—just long enough to become a cage.

Corlexi placed his palm flat on the diagram paper, grounding himself in its inked angles.

“Tell me what you’re building,” he said.

Corvin’s smile finally arrived, small and sharp. “A structure that can’t be argued with.”

Outside the slit windows, the sky seam held its straightness without apology, and Corlexi felt—deep in the part of himself that answered to line and load—that soon the city would be forced to choose which kind of holding it wanted.

Stone held by hands.

Or people held by rules.
