from docx import Document
from docx.shared import Pt, Inches
from docx.enum.text import WD_ALIGN_PARAGRAPH
from docx.oxml.ns import qn
from docx.shared import RGBColor
from pathlib import Path
import re

doc = Document()

# Basic style
style = doc.styles['Normal']
style.font.name = 'Times New Roman'
style._element.rPr.rFonts.set(qn('w:eastAsia'), 'Times New Roman')
style.font.size = Pt(11)

def add_title(text):
    p = doc.add_paragraph()
    run = p.add_run(text)
    run.bold = True
    run.font.size = Pt(18)
    p.alignment = WD_ALIGN_PARAGRAPH.CENTER

def add_subtitle(text):
    p = doc.add_paragraph()
    run = p.add_run(text)
    run.italic = True
    run.font.size = Pt(12)
    p.alignment = WD_ALIGN_PARAGRAPH.CENTER

def add_heading(text, level=1):
    doc.add_heading(text, level=level)

def add_block(text):
    # split by blank lines to paragraphs
    for para in text.strip().split("\n\n"):
        p = doc.add_paragraph(para.strip())

# ---------- Manuscript Front Matter ----------
add_title("N01 — Glass Under Load")
add_subtitle("Confluence Chronicles — Novella 01 Manuscript")
doc.add_page_break()

# ---------- Chapter 1 ----------
add_heading("CHAPTER 1: Something Wrong", level=1)
ch1 = r"""
Jhace Torrin started each Tide the same way: hand to pipe, boot to stone, ear to the heat-grid’s thrum.

Underface air carried heat even when the upper tiers went cool. It carried soot, damp, and the sharp tang of old metal. The main line beside the inspection walk ran steady, warm enough to fog his breath at the edge of the vent slits. Jhace pressed his palm to the iron and counted the vibration through his wrist.

Stable. No chatter. No stutter.

He moved along the line in measured Strides. The walk was narrow and slick with condensation. Each step landed on stone set in a staggered pattern to spread load across the tunnel floor. Bram Kethis had taught him to trust staggered patterns and distrust long straight seams. Straight seams invited clean breaks.

The first junction box sat where the line split toward the kiln galleries. Jhace crouched and ran his fingers along the box’s edge. The seam at the hinge carried a fine grit. New grit.

He scraped a grain onto his thumb and rubbed it. The grain was not ash. It was sharp, glassy.

He looked up at the overhead brace. The brace plate had shifted half a nail’s breadth. Not enough to alarm the crews upstairs. Enough to change load on the tunnel arch.

Jhace stood and listened again. The tunnel held a hum, low and constant, but a second tone rode it now. Finer. Higher. A thin vibration that did not match the heat-grid’s usual pattern.

He pressed two fingers to the arch stone. The tone traveled through the stone and into his bones.

Wrong.

He moved faster, not running. Running added load. Running made boots slip. He took longer Strides, letting the tunnel guide his feet. The second junction box was warmer than it should have been, and the warmth arrived unevenly. Heat shifted in pulses, not flow.

Jhace knelt and checked the bracket clamps. The top clamp was tight. The bottom clamp had loosened a fraction. The bolt still held, but the iron strap under it carried a slight bend that had not been there two Tides earlier.

Bram’s voice lived in his head: Check the bend before you tighten the bolt. Tightening a bent strap snaps it. Snapped straps kill.

Jhace didn’t tighten. He marked the strap with chalk, a short line to show drift, and moved on.

The deeper he went, the more the second tone thickened. It was not loud. It was persistent. It had the same feel as a wire under tension, vibrating from a touch you couldn’t see. Jhace stopped at a vent slit and held his hand near the opening.

Cold air passed over his knuckles. Not normal Underface cold. This cold carried a dry bite. It carried no damp. It carried the edge of high stone and open distance.

He drew his hand back.

A tremor ran through the floor. Small. The tunnel responded with a soft grinding sound where stone joints shifted against each other. Jhace widened his stance and let the vibration pass through his legs. Aftershocks had been common since the Piercing rumors started in the upper tiers. Small shudders, then calm.

This tremor had a different cadence. It was not a wave. It was a tilt.

Jhace felt his balance shift left, then right, then settle somewhere that was not center. The tunnel did not collapse. The pipes did not burst. But the load in the arch changed. The stone felt heavier, then lighter, then heavy again.

He pressed his palm to the wall and waited until his breath steadied.

Bram would want to know. Bram would want the grid shut down before it tore itself apart. Bram would also tell him not to dramatize.

Jhace wiped sweat from his forehead with his sleeve, leaving a streak of soot on his skin. He turned back toward the forge bay, where Bram kept the main tools and the salvage clamps.

The second tone followed him, present even when he moved away from the junction boxes. It sat in the tunnel air. It sat behind his teeth.

Jhace climbed the short ladder into the forge bay and pushed the iron grate open.

Heat hit his face. Forge heat, steady and controlled. The smell of coal and oil and iron filings took over from Underface damp.

Bram stood at the anvil block, hammer in hand, shaping a bracket with the calm patience of someone who had learned not to waste strikes. He glanced up once, eyes narrowing at Jhace’s pace.

“You move like something slipped,” Bram said.

“Something shifted,” Jhace said. He kept his voice flat. Panic didn’t help. “Heat-grid tone is wrong. Junction box one has new grit. Brace plate drifted.”

Bram set the hammer down. The metal thud sounded clean. “Show.”

Jhace led him to the access grate and down the ladder. Bram followed without hurry, but his hands flexed once as if preparing for work.

At the first junction box, Jhace pointed to the grit and the drift mark. Bram crouched, touched the seam, and held still. He listened through his fingertips, not his ears.

Bram’s mouth tightened. “That’s not ash.”

“No,” Jhace said.

Bram stood and pressed his palm to the arch stone where Jhace had felt the second tone.

The older man’s eyes narrowed further. He kept his face controlled, but his shoulders tightened.

“It’s there,” Bram said.

Jhace exhaled. Confirmation mattered. Confirmation meant he wasn’t inventing.

Bram moved to the vent slit and held his hand near the airflow. He drew it back fast and wiped his palm on his apron.

“Cold bite,” Bram said.

Jhace nodded. “Dry.”

Bram stared down the tunnel. The second tone thickened, then thinned, then thickened again, matching nothing Jhace could explain with pipe flow.

“Shut down the side line,” Bram said. “Not the main. Side. Slow the pull.”

Jhace hesitated. “That means the kiln galleries run cooler.”

Bram’s eyes snapped to him. “Cooler is better than split.”

Jhace moved to the valve wheel and set both hands on it. He turned slow, feeling the resistance change. The wheel vibrated under his grip, a fine tremor that carried through his forearms. He closed the side line to half, then a little more, stopping before the wheel seated hard.

The second tone did not vanish.

Bram watched the valve and the tunnel wall. “Not from flow,” he said.

Jhace swallowed. “Then what?”

Bram’s gaze stayed on the stone. “Something under the stone. Something above it. Doesn’t matter. We brace what we can. We keep people out of the junction throat. We wait for the next tremor and we see what it does.”

Waiting never sat well in Jhace’s ribs.

The floor gave another small tilt. Jhace felt his balance slide, then catch. Bram’s hand went to the wall without thinking.

The tilt eased.

Jhace looked at Bram. Bram looked back.

“Up,” Bram said. “Get word to the crews. Keep the kiln corridors clear. No crowding under arches.”

Jhace climbed the ladder, heat rising with him, the second tone still behind his teeth. He stepped into the forge bay and grabbed his coat from the hook by the door.

Bram called after him, voice hard. “And keep your hands to yourself. Whatever this is, it’s not for testing.”

Jhace paused at the threshold, feeling the Underface hum through the soles of his boots, and nodded once.

He went out into Saint Merro’s lower lanes with a wrong vibration in his bones and a sense that the next tremor would not be small.
"""
add_block(ch1)
doc.add_page_break()

# ---------- Chapter 2 ----------
add_heading("CHAPTER 2: Glass That Listens", level=1)
ch2 = r"""
Pella hated heights and loved instruments. The observatory demanded both.

He stood on the platform rim where Saint Merro’s upper stone dropped away into open air, and he kept his hands on the scope stand until his knuckles went pale. The stand was bolted into a reinforced base block. Pella checked the bolts by touch. Tight. No drift since last Tide’s inspection.

Marisse paced behind him in short Strides that never formed a pattern. Her eyes did not rest. They scanned the parapet seams, the braces at the platform corners, the tower’s iron ties. She had no instrument. She did not need one. She carried vigilance in her shoulders and in the way she kept her weight centered over her feet.

“You’re late,” Marisse said.

Pella adjusted the scope angle by a fraction. “The lens had grit. I cleaned it.”

“You clean everything,” Marisse said.

“I prefer clean data,” Pella said.

Marisse’s mouth tightened. “Clean data didn’t stop the kiln hall fall.”

Pella did not answer. He did not know how to answer without choosing the wrong words. He adjusted the scope again and checked baseline alignment against the far marker stone on the next tower.

Baseline held. That meant the stand had not shifted, and the platform had not settled more than expected.

Pella lifted his eyes past the scope and looked at the sky.

A thin white line cut across it.

He had seen the line earlier in the Tide, faint enough to dismiss as haze. Now it held straightness. It did not drift. It did not soften. It did not match any known weather sheet.

He lowered his gaze to the scope lens and tried to turn the line into measured angles.

The lens edges showed distortion. Fine, subtle warping of air. The marker stone beyond the lens did not sit exactly where it should have. Pella checked the scope’s internal reference marks. He adjusted. The marker stone stayed wrong.

He swallowed. Instrument error was always the first suspect. He checked the scope again. He checked the stand bolts. He checked the bubble glass.

The bubble was centered.

The marker stone remained off by a fraction.

Pella wrote the deviation on his slate. He did not interpret yet. He only recorded.

Marisse stepped to the parapet and stared at the sky seam, eyes narrowed until the skin around them creased.

“You taste it,” she said.

Pella blinked. “Taste what?”

Marisse’s lips pressed together. “The air changed. Dry bite.”

Pella tasted. He found it after a breath, the edge of dryness that did not belong at this height. It made his tongue feel coated. It made his throat tighten.

He wrote: **Air bite present. Non-humid.**

Marisse’s voice stayed low. “This is signal.”

Pella’s hand paused. The word struck him with weight. Signal meant real. Signal meant the world had changed in a way that didn’t care about his fear of height.

He forced the pen to move again. “Describe.”

Marisse exhaled through her nose. “The platform feels wrong. It’s holding, but the hold feels temporary.”

Pella looked down at the stone under his boots. He pressed the sole of his boot harder into it, seeking vibration. The platform carried a faint hum from the city’s heat mains below, but beneath that hum ran another pulse.

A slow tilt.

The platform shifted under him. Small. Enough to make his stomach lurch.

Pella grabbed the scope stand and widened his stance. Marisse’s hand snapped to the parapet, fingers spread.

The tilt eased, then returned in the opposite direction, then settled off-center.

The white line in the sky seemed sharper when Pella looked up again. His eyes watered from wind and from something else, pressure behind his eyes that made focusing a strain.

Pella wrote: **Tremor event. Tilt component. Baseline offset persists.**

Below the platform, the city’s sound rose. Shouts. Running feet. A crack that carried through stone and iron alike.

Marisse’s head snapped toward the sound. “That’s ground-level failure,” she said.

Pella forced himself to keep the scope steady. He turned the lens toward the horizon where the sea line used to blur into haze. The white seam continued beyond the city’s edge, a cut through the air that extended farther than he could track with the instrument.

He wrote the angle and the direction.

Another crack sounded, closer. Dust rose from a far lane in a pale plume. People began to move, first as clusters, then as streams.

Marisse’s breathing quickened. She did not call it fear. Her posture did. “This is the moment,” she said.

Pella’s throat tightened. “What moment?”

Marisse looked at him. Her eyes held the same intensity Pella had seen in survivors who refused to die quietly. “The moment when everyone calls it noise because they can’t carry signal.”

Pella stared at his slate. The numbers held. The deviations repeated. The tilt event had direction. The air bite was consistent.

He closed his fingers around the slate’s edge until his knuckles hurt. “It’s not noise,” he said.

Marisse nodded once. “Then move.”

They left the platform with the scope stand locked, the lens capped, the baseline recorded. As they descended the stair throat, the city’s screams grew louder. Another tremor ran through the stair. The handrail vibrated under Pella’s grip.

The white seam remained above the slit windows, visible even in fragments.

Pella did not look away. He had written it down. Now the city would answer whether it wanted to listen.
"""
add_block(ch2)
doc.add_page_break()

# ---------- Chapter 3 ----------
add_heading("CHAPTER 3: The Sky Breaks", level=1)
ch3 = r"""
The Underface tunnel moved before Jhace could decide how to brace.

It began with a sharp jerk in the floor and a sound that came from stone joints grinding. The tunnel arch shed grit. The main line shuddered under his palm. Jhace widened his stance, but the tilt threw him off-center and the tunnel’s vibration changed pitch.

A support bracket ahead snapped loose. Iron rang against stone. A hanging pipe strap swung and struck the wall, sending a metallic chatter down the line.

Bram shouted from behind him, “Out of the throat!”

Jhace moved. He took two Strides forward toward the nearest cross-bay where the tunnel widened and the arch carried more evenly. The floor shifted again, and a section of wall at the bay mouth cracked open.

Chloen was there, crouched near the wall with a bundle of scrap cloth in her arms. She had been sent down by someone who thought Underface was safe. Her eyes went wide when the wall cracked.

“Move,” Jhace said.

She tried. The floor tilted. She slipped. The wall section heaved and dropped, trapping her lower leg under a broken slab.

Chloen screamed once, then clamped her teeth shut, face tight with effort.

Jhace dropped beside her and grabbed the slab edge. It was heavy. His fingers slipped on wet stone and grit.

Blood spread fast from beneath the slab, dark and warm.

Jhace’s throat tightened. The sight landed behind his ribs as a familiar weight. He did not let his mind name it. Names stole time.

He looked at the wall. The crack line spread. The arch above the bay mouth carried wrong now. Load shifted into a thin strip of stone that could not hold long under repeated tilt.

Bram’s boots thundered closer. “Leave it,” Bram shouted. “She’s pinned. We can’t fight the wall.”

Jhace heard Bram. He also heard the sound beneath it: the wall seam widening with each small tremor pulse.

Chloen’s breath came in short, sharp pulls. “It hurts,” she said.

Jhace set his palm on the slab edge and felt the vibration through it. The stone was not stable. It wanted to settle deeper. If it settled, it would crush her leg fully.

He tried to wedge his hammer head under the slab. The tremor in the floor made his hand slip. The hammer skittered away.

Chloen’s fingers clenched in cloth. Her knuckles whitened. She stared at Jhace with an expression that held no bargaining. Only need.

Jhace pressed both hands to the slab and pushed.

His muscles strained. The slab did not move.

A tremor hit again, harder. The slab shifted a fraction downward. Chloen’s cry tore out of her, raw.

Jhace’s mind flashed the old count behind his ribs. Twenty-three names. Twenty-three bodies. He saw dust and blood and iron, and his hands remembered pulling until nails tore.

He could not add another.

He slapped his right palm flat against the slab and held it there. Skin to stone. Contact.

He pushed intent through the contact, not words, not prayer. A single command shaped by fear and protection: hold.

Heat burned into his palm. Copper-gold light threaded under his skin and into the slab seam. The glow followed the fracture line, tracing stress paths in the stone. The slab’s downward drift stopped.

Jhace’s left hand shoved into the gap his right had stabilized. He levered with his forearm, using the moment. The slab lifted a fraction. Enough.

Bram was there now. Bram jammed a wedge into the gap and drove it with two hammer strikes. The wedge seated hard. The slab stayed raised.

Chloen sucked in air and tried to pull her leg free. The gap was not wide enough.

Jhace kept his palm pressed. Heat climbed into his wrist. The copper-gold glow brightened and then steadied, as if it had found the line it wanted.

“Move,” Jhace said to Chloen, voice tight.

She dragged her leg. The trapped foot twisted free with a wet sound that made Jhace’s stomach turn. Blood smeared the stone. Chloen collapsed into Jhace’s shoulder, shaking.

Jhace pulled his palm away from the slab.

The glow faded from the stone, leaving faint residue along the fracture line. The residue shimmered and then dulled into a copper-gold stain.

Jhace’s right hand shook. The tremor was not from effort alone. It ran deeper, a vibration in his tendons that did not obey breath. Copper-gold vein-lines stood out along his forearm, branching under skin.

Bram stared at his arm. Bram’s face stayed hard, but his eyes tightened.

Chloen coughed, a wet sound, then went still. Jhace looked down and saw that the wound in her lower leg, the ragged gash that should have bled longer, had tightened at the edges. The blood slowed. The skin looked wrong, smoothed in places where it should have torn.

Jhace’s mouth went dry.

Bram’s voice came low. “Don’t touch her again.”

Jhace swallowed. “I didn’t.”

The tunnel tilted again. The bay mouth arch groaned. The wedge Bram had set bit deeper. The slab shifted sideways. The wall crack widened. The bay itself started to fail.

Bram grabbed Chloen under the arms and hauled her up. “Out,” Bram said. “Now.”

Jhace’s right hand shook too hard to close fully. He forced his fingers around his hammer haft anyway. He picked it up and followed Bram toward the ladder throat that led up to street level.

The ladder shook under their boots as they climbed. Dust poured down from above. Someone screamed at the surface. The city had become a chain of failures.

At the top, the grate opened to light that looked wrong. The sky carried a straight white seam overhead, pale and fixed. Dust hung in the air and caught the light, turning the street into a haze of grit and glare.

A building support across the lane had already collapsed. People ran around the rubble, slipping and shoving. A shoring crew tried to set braces in a doorway while the lintel above them cracked.

Jhace stepped into the street and felt the ground settle under his boots in a slow, uneven motion. The tremor in his right hand continued, stubborn and visible.

A woman in a dust-streaked coat stood on a low wall with a glass-sight tube braced against her shoulder. She turned her head and locked eyes on Jhace’s shaking hand.

She climbed down in two Strides, moving with control even as the street moved under her.

“You,” she said, voice sharp and steady. “You held stone with skin contact.”

Jhace’s throat tightened. He looked at Bram. Bram’s jaw set.

The woman stepped closer. Her gaze traced the copper-gold vein-lines up Jhace’s forearm. “There is residue,” she said. “And cost.”

Jhace stared at her. “Who are you?”

She did not answer with her name. She answered with method.

“I measure,” she said. “And your method just changed my baselines.”
"""
add_block(ch3)
doc.add_page_break()

# ---------- Chapter 4 ----------
add_heading("CHAPTER 4: After the Shudder", level=1)
ch4 = r"""
Jhace woke on forge stone with his right hand still shaking.

He had fallen asleep sitting up, back against the wall. His coat was folded under his head. Bram’s boot toe rested near his ribs in a posture that meant Bram had not left him alone.

The forge air was warm. Coal smell lingered. The sound of the city above came down in dull waves—shouts, hammers, distant cracks.

Jhace lifted his right hand and watched his fingers tremble. The tremor was fine, constant. It did not stop when he clenched. Clenching made it worse.

He sat up and pressed his palm to the forge wall. The stone was steady. His hand was not.

Bram’s voice came from the bench. “You awake.”

Jhace looked over. Bram sat with a mug in his hand, shoulders hunched, eyes half-lidded. “You shook through sleep,” Bram said. “That’s new.”

Jhace swallowed. “It’ll pass.”

Bram grunted. “It might. It might not.”

Chloen lay on a pallet near the heat-grid vent, wrapped in cloth. Her breathing was shallow but regular. Her lower leg was bound. The wound looked cleaner than it should have. The skin edges had tightened. The gash had no rot smell.

Jhace stared at it until his stomach clenched.

A knock hit the forge door.

Bram stood and opened it a fraction. The woman from the street stepped in, dust still on her coat. She carried a satchel and a logbook wrapped in oilcloth.

“Tiffani,” Bram said, naming her without warmth.

Jhace’s eyes narrowed. He caught Bram’s tone and filed it away: Bram knew her already. That meant she had history in Saint Merro’s measurements.

Tiffani stepped inside and shut the door. She looked at Jhace’s hands first, then at Chloen, then at Bram.

“Your tremor persisted,” she said to Jhace.

Jhace didn’t answer. He didn’t like being evaluated.

Tiffani moved to Chloen’s pallet and crouched. She did not touch the girl. She watched the wound binding and the skin edges. Her eyes tracked in small precise movements.

“This is not normal closure,” she said.

Bram’s voice stayed flat. “Nothing is normal.”

Tiffani looked up. “It matters whether this is a side-effect of the hold.”

Jhace’s throat tightened. “I didn’t do anything to her except get her out.”

Tiffani’s gaze returned to his hands. “Except put copper-gold through stone while she was in contact range.”

Jhace felt heat rise in his face. Anger, shame, fatigue, all mixed. “I didn’t choose it,” he said.

Tiffani opened her logbook and wrote without looking down for long. “Forced activation,” she said, then wrote more. “Condition: immediate protective need. Method: skin contact at fracture. Effect: temporary stabilization. Cost: tremor and vein-lines visible.”

Jhace’s jaw tightened. “Stop writing.”

Tiffani’s pen paused. “No.”

Bram’s hand lifted slightly, a warning to Jhace not to snap. Bram’s caution was practical. The city had too many enemies now. Starting a fight in a forge did not help anyone.

Tiffani closed the book, tied the oilcloth wrap, and set it back in her satchel. “I’m not here to claim you,” she said. “I’m here because this is a new structural variable. It will kill people if it’s misunderstood.”

Jhace stared at her. The truth of the sentence did not make him like her.

Bram said, “Talk fast.”

Tiffani nodded once. “The hold fails when it isn’t real need,” she said. “I’ve seen two attempts by others. They pressed palms to cracks in calm conditions. No glow. Then their hands shook anyway.”

Jhace’s eyes narrowed. “So it punishes waste.”

“Yes,” Tiffani said. “Conditional activation. It requires a true trigger.” fileciteturn24file3

Bram’s mouth tightened. “Good. That means fools can’t farm it.”

Tiffani’s gaze sharpened. “Fools will still try.”

Jhace flexed his fingers. The tremor did not ease. “What do you want from me?” he asked.

Tiffani spoke with the same clipped clarity she used on measurements. “I want to observe another activation under controlled conditions. Not staged. Not manufactured. A real failure point. I want to know whether targeting the contact point changes cost.” fileciteturn24file3

Jhace laughed once, short. “You want to reduce my cost.”

“I want to reduce loss,” she said.

Bram leaned against the anvil block. “And what do you offer?”

Tiffani met his gaze. “Maps. Baselines. A list of choke points where the city will fail first.”

Bram’s eyes narrowed. “You offer knowledge.”

“I offer accuracy,” Tiffani corrected. “Accuracy keeps people alive.”

Jhace’s right hand shook harder. He shoved it into his pocket. “I’m not your instrument.”

Tiffani did not flinch. “Then stop acting alone.”

The words struck Jhace’s pride and his fear at the same time. He stepped forward, then stopped. Bram’s presence at his side held him in place.

Before Jhace could answer, a runner slammed into the forge door hard enough to rattle the latch. Bram opened it and caught the runner’s shoulder before he fell in.

“West throat,” the runner gasped. “Triage wall. It’s moving.”

Bram looked at Jhace. Jhace felt the decision lock in his ribs before he chose it with words.

He grabbed his hammer. “Show,” he said.

They moved through lanes where dust hung in pockets and people shouted warnings that arrived too late. The triage wall at the west throat carried hundreds behind it—refugees clustered under patched roofs, a line of wounded waiting for space inside the relief hall.

The wall’s base had cracked. The crack line widened with each small tremor. A brace crew hammered wedges, but the wedges kept spitting out grit.

Jhace stepped to the base seam and pressed his palm toward it.

Nothing happened.

No heat. No glow. Only the shame of emptiness and a small spike of tremor that made his fingers shake harder.

Tiffani’s voice cut in, sharp. “It is not in immediate failure. You are trying too early.”

Jhace jerked his hand back. Anger hit hard. “It is moving.”

“It is moving slowly,” Tiffani said. “The trigger hasn’t crossed threshold. You waste.”

Bram’s hand clamped Jhace’s shoulder once. “Listen,” Bram said.

Jhace’s jaw clenched. He watched the crack. He listened to the stone groan. He waited, hating the wait.

A tremor hit. The wall shifted hard. The crack opened wider in one jump. A section of plaster fell. Someone screamed behind the wall.

Now the need arrived, real and immediate.

Jhace slapped his palm to the crack seam.

Heat surged into his skin. Copper-gold light ran into the fracture network, tightening the base seam. The wall’s forward drift stopped long enough for the brace crew to drive wedges in a new pattern.

Jhace pulled away.

His hand shook so hard he could not grip his hammer for three breaths. His fingers refused to close fully. The tremor climbed into his wrist and stayed there.

The wall held. People stayed behind it, alive and angry and frightened.

Jhace stared at his hand, breath ragged.

Tiffani watched him. Her face stayed controlled, but her eyes held something sharp: confirmation. Data becoming real.

She opened her logbook and wrote one line only, fast and decisive.

Jhace could not read it. He didn’t need to. He felt the conclusion in his bones:

The wall held.

His grip did not.
"""
add_block(ch4)
doc.add_page_break()

# ---------- Chapter 5-12 + Interludes + Epilogue (revised from earlier drafts; minimal similes) ----------
add_heading("CHAPTER 5: Lines That Don’t Lie", level=1)
ch5 = r"""
Tiffani’s logbook gained weight in her hand without gaining pages.

She kept it dry anyway. Oilcloth wrap, tie twice, tuck into the inner pocket of her field satchel. Even with Soulpulse dust in the air, even with mortar grit everywhere, even with the city’s breath turning coarse after the Piercing, paper still mattered. Paper held numbers. Numbers held memory without needing permission.

The Kiln Corridor ran crooked through the Glass Quarter, ovens on one side, cracked stone on the other. Firehouse vents still exhaled when they could. Some were dead. Some were choked. The ones that worked made the corridor warmer than the lanes that fed into it, and warmth made people gather. Warmth made it harder to keep clear passages. Warmth made a load path more complicated.

Tiffani stopped at the first hold site of Tide 2, Kindling. A small archway that had already been shored twice. The crew had used timber braces and iron straps. The straps were too thin and too long; the timber was warped. The arch carried weight from a broken upper walk and redirected it into a corner abutment that had lost footing when the ground rotated during the Piercing.

She put her palm on the stone and did not pretend it was calm.

Her fingers felt micro-vibration, a fine pulse that traveled through her wrist and up into her forearm. She steadied herself with the other hand on the strap and counted the cadence. Not a sound. A repeatable rhythm. Repeatable meant real.

She opened the logbook to the current section and wrote:

Hold Site 1 — Kiln Corridor / West bend / Oven 14 vent line  
Structure: arch + corner abutment  
Material: stone + mortar, iron strap add-on  
State: creep present, low amplitude, constant  
Human traffic: high, uncontrolled  
Response: additional brace recommended; traffic control required  
Note: baseline vibration increased by one subdivision since Tide 1, Quench

She underlined traffic control twice. Not because it would happen, but because she wanted it documented that it should have happened.

She moved down the corridor in measured Strides, stopping at each shift in the paving where the stone had split. She marked deviations on her corridor map: hairline fissures, buckled joints, slumped grout. She checked the same points she had checked during the first Tides after the Piercing, and she compared. Some cracks were stable. Some had widened by less than a finger’s breadth. Two had opened enough for grit to pour through when she tapped them with her knuckle.

When she reached the second hold site, she did not have to ask whose work it was.

The copper-gold stain was faint but visible in the stone. Not a dye. Not paint. A residue in the surface where heat and light had passed through fractured material and left the cracks cleaner than the surrounding dust. Tiffani knelt, wiped away grit with the side of her hand, and traced the path with her fingertip.

Stress lines. Real ones. The route was not random. The glow had flowed along the fracture network that carried load.

She wrote again.

Hold Site 2 — Kiln Corridor / Junction to Underface stair  
Structure: stair throat + supporting lintel  
State pre-hold: recorded sag by two subdivisions, Tide 1 late Quench  
State post-hold: stable within half subdivision, Tide 2 Kindling  
Indicator: copper-gold residue along primary fractures  
Hypothesis: activation follows immediate need, follows load path, prefers existing fracture routes

Two points of residue now. Two points meant the thing existed beyond a single event. Two points did not mean enough.

A shout from deeper in the corridor pulled her head up. A crew dragged a brace into position. Someone argued with a woman carrying a bundled child. The child did not cry. The woman’s face was too empty for crying.

Tiffani closed the logbook and moved.

The next hold site was in a side lane where a glass furnace had collapsed. The oven’s dome had cracked and dropped inward, spilling ash and broken brick. The roof beams above had bowed. The beam ends had torn into their sockets. A crew had propped them with scavenged columns and wedges. The wedges were too dry. Dry wedges slipped.

She examined the beam ends and the sockets. The stone around the sockets had sheared, and the shear line had the same faint copper-gold residue as the lintel site.

Three points of residue. Pattern emerging. Not proven. Not safe.

By the time Tide 2 reached Pouring, she had six hold sites mapped and three confirmed sites with copper-gold residue. She had two recorded failures where the residue was absent and the structure continued to creep despite shoring.

Failures mattered. Failures defined the boundary.

She chose an observation position above the Saint Merro junction approaches, where the main walk sloped down toward an intersection of arches, columns, and ramps that carried people between the Crown’s inner districts and the Glass Quarter camps. The junction had become a throat. Everything moved through it.

Tiffani set up her glass-sight on a stable parapet. The lens had survived because she had wrapped it in wool and stored it in a padded case during the Piercing. The lens was not perfect anymore; a hairline crack ran along one edge, but the crack did not distort within the central field. It would still measure.

She aligned the sight with a known stone mark on the far arch and checked baseline. The arch carried slightly low. The deviation was within one subdivision, but the direction was consistent with the creep she had logged at other sites.

Then she waited.

Waiting was not passive. Waiting was data collection with legs still.

When the shoring crew set a brace and the arch answered with a small shift, she recorded the shift. When a line of refugees pushed through and the load increased, she recorded the increased vibration. When an aftershock ran under the stone and the arch responded with a groan that made the lens tremble, she recorded that too.

The man she was tracking arrived from the Underface stair at the junction’s left side.

Jhace Torrin moved with the gait of someone who had spent most of his life under stone. He watched the joints first, then the crowd, then the shoring. He spoke to the crew chief with minimal words. He carried a hammer but did not swing it. His right hand held the handle too tightly, and his fingers did not settle into a stable grip.

His tremor showed even at distance. Small. Persistent. Not a fit. A baseline instability that affected fine motor control. Tiffani had seen it in Tide 1. It was worse now.

He set his palm against the arch base. Skin to stone. Contact. Intent. Need.

Copper-gold light moved through the fracture network and into a joint that had begun to separate. The arch shifted upward by half a subdivision. Then it held.

Jhace pulled his hand away quickly. His shoulders tightened. He hid the tremor by clenching his hands into fists and putting them against his thighs. The veins in his forearms stood out darker than they should, with faint lines that followed no normal pattern. Copper-gold lines under skin.

Cost. Visible. Physical.

Tiffani wrote:

Hold Event — Junction arch A  
Trigger context: crowd load + aftershock + audible joint movement  
Method: palm contact at arch base  
Effect: immediate stabilization + lift of 0.5 subdivision  
Residue: visible line along fracture network; copper-gold  
User condition: tremor increased post-use; vein-lines visible; grip avoidance observed

She did not write his name in the logbook. Not yet. Names invited narrative. Narrative invited distortion. She wrote User A and left it there.

Then the Resonance Regulation Office arrived.

They came in pairs, wearing coats that were too clean for the dust. Their boots were polished. Their faces carried the calm of people who believed paper could still control stone.

They did not approach the shoring crew first. They climbed to the parapet where Tiffani stood.

One of them was older, with a ledger tucked under his arm. The other was younger and watched the crowd more than he watched Tiffani.

“Observer,” the older one said. “We were told you’ve been tracking the new phenomenon.”

“You were told correctly,” Tiffani said.

“The Office is consolidating reports,” he said. “We can provide paper, ink, guards for your work sites. We can provide access to restricted zones. In return, we require your records. All of them.”

“My records are not for trade,” Tiffani said.

“They are public safety,” the older man said. “This phenomenon is dangerous. It must be tracked by an authorized body.”

“It is being tracked,” Tiffani said. “By me. With methods that do not depend on your authorization.”

The older man’s calm tightened. “We are instructing. We have authority from the Crown’s administrative chambers to collect all resonance-related observations.”

“Authority does not stabilize arches,” Tiffani said. “Braces do. Correct marking does. Competent hands do.”

The younger one shifted his stance. “You sound uncooperative.”

“I sound accurate,” Tiffani said.

The older man leaned forward slightly. “You will be required to register your involvement and the names of any resonance users you observe.”

Tiffani looked down at the junction. Jhace had stepped away from the arch and was speaking to a crew member. His hands were still clenched. He was trying to keep them steady by force.

“No,” she said.

The older man blinked once. “No?”

“No,” Tiffani repeated. “I will not hand over records. I will not provide names. I will not allow you to turn a survival mechanism into a ledger of control.”

The older man’s face hardened. “Then you obstruct.”

“I refuse,” Tiffani said. “You can attempt to compel.”

They left without taking the logbook, but they left with a look that promised return.

Tiffani did not feel triumphant. She felt the burden of attention shift toward her. Attention changed outcomes. Outcomes changed costs.

She wrote a brief note after they were gone:

RRO approach — Tide 2 Pouring  
Offer: resources with control strings; demand names + full records  
Response: refused  
Risk: escalation likely; concealment of User A identity required

The second pressure arrived before Tide 3 reached Kindling.

It came with water.

Not rain. Barrels, hauled by teams who still had access to functioning mains and reservoirs. Water meant power in the Glass Quarter. Water meant control over kiln operations, over refugee cooking fires, over mortar mixing.

A Water-Guild factor walked with the barrels. He wore a coat that had been cleaned recently. His hands were not callused in a way that suggested shoring work. He carried a small seal stamp on a chain at his belt.

Tellis.

Tiffani recognized him from council meetings before the Piercing, when the Glass Quarter argued for repair budgets and the Water-Guild argued for tariff adjustments. He spoke politely. Politeness did not make an offer benign.

He climbed to the parapet without asking permission and glanced at the glass-sight.

“Observer,” he said. “You’re watching the arches settle.”

“I’m measuring,” Tiffani said.

Tellis nodded toward the junction. “The Crown will decide what gets rebuilt. The Guilds will decide what gets supplied. The Glass Quarter is not winning that argument.”

“Noted,” Tiffani said.

Tellis leaned his elbows on the parapet, careful not to dirty his sleeves. “You have influence. People listen when you point at a crack and say it will fail. People move when you tell them to move.”

Tiffani waited.

“I can make that influence profitable,” Tellis said. “Advise the Water-Guild’s retreat plan. There are districts that should be abandoned. Routes that should be redirected. Assets that should be salvaged.”

“Your retreat plan requires calling occupied structures unsalvageable,” Tiffani said.

“It requires realism,” Tellis said. “The Piercing changed the city. The Glass Quarter was fragile before. Now it will bleed resources.”

“People live in those ovens now,” Tiffani said.

Tellis’s smile held. “People live in many unsuitable places.”

“You want my measurements as a tool for profit,” Tiffani said.

“Profit funds rebuilding,” Tellis said.

“Profit funds consolidation,” Tiffani said.

Tellis’s gaze sharpened. “You’re sentimental.”

“I’m methodical,” Tiffani said. “My method includes human load.”

Tellis glanced down at the junction again. “That man is a liability. Better to let the Office take him.”

Tiffani’s throat tightened. “I will not trade data to you.”

Tellis’s face cooled. “You will regret being alone.”

She wrote after he left:

Guild approach — Tide 3 Kindling  
Offer: profitable retreat advisory; redirect supply routes via structural condemnation  
Response: refused  
Risk: Water-Guild obstruction likely; political pressure rising

Marisse found her at the third hold site of Tide 3, Pouring.

Marisse walked as if each Stride needed confirmation. She scanned cracks in stone automatically, her gaze never resting.

“You’ve been recording,” Marisse said.

“Yes,” Tiffani said.

Marisse crouched beside the corner abutment and touched the stone lightly with two fingers. “This joint is worse than your last mark.”

Tiffani checked the mark. Marisse was correct. The separation had widened by a fraction. Enough to matter.

“I missed that change,” Tiffani said.

Marisse’s jaw tightened. “That is how it starts.”

Tiffani did not deny. She already knew what Marisse meant.

Marisse looked at her. “You’re doing the thing again.”

“The thing is measuring,” Tiffani said.

“The thing is staying on the edge,” Marisse said. “Watching someone else take the weight because you can count it from here.”

Tiffani’s fingers tightened on her logbook. “Counting it from here is how we learn what it is.”

Marisse’s eyes flicked to the copper-gold residue line on the stone. “We learned enough to know it costs him.”

“Yes,” Tiffani said. “That is in the record.”

Marisse stood. “Record does not stop him.”

“He is moving from hold site to hold site,” Marisse continued. “He does not rest. He does not eat when the crews offer. He does not stop when his hands fail. He is becoming a single point of failure.”

Tiffani did not deny it. Denial would be inaccurate.

Marisse’s voice lowered. “You remember the twenty-three.”

Tiffani’s throat tightened. “Yes.”

“You remember what it did to you to be wrong,” Marisse said. “And you remember what it did to me to be right and unheard.”

Tiffani looked down at her logbook. Lines. Numbers. Residue marks. Tremor amplitude estimates.

She looked at Jhace in the distance, moving through dust with hands he tried to control by force.

“I’m not wrong this time,” Tiffani said.

Marisse’s face did not soften. “Then stop behaving as if you can fix it by watching.”

Tiffani’s mind ran through risks: institutional attention, Office, Guild, the danger of getting close to a method the Crown wanted to own.

Then her mind ran the other list: tremor, vein-lines, increasing frequency of activation, increasing duration of recovery. The pattern did not curve gently. It bent.

Isolation accelerated decline. A single person trying to hold a city failed.

“Where is his forge?” Tiffani asked.

Marisse pointed toward the Underface stair throat. “Down two levels. Old ironwork bay. He keeps it hot.”

Tiffani nodded once. “Thank you.”

Marisse caught her wrist. “Do not go in and talk. Go in with proof. He will hear proof.”

“I will,” Tiffani said.

She wrote one line and closed the logbook:

Decision — Engage. Anchor the work to method before User A becomes the next collapse.

She turned toward the Underface stair and began walking toward Jhace Torrin’s forge with the logbook pressed against her ribs.

# Chapter 6, 7, interlude I, chapters 8-10, interlude II, chapters 11-12, epilogue

add_heading("CHAPTER 6: Weight That Won’t Yield", level=1)
add_block(r"""
Jhace felt the junction fail before he saw it.

The stone under his boots changed tone. Not sound. Weight. A shift in the way vibration traveled through the paving, a deepening pulse that did not match the crowd’s footfall. The junction always carried too much. Since the Piercing, it carried too much without its old margins.

A runner grabbed his sleeve near the Underface stair throat. “Torrin. Arch A. It’s moving.”

Jhace didn’t ask which arch. There were only so many that mattered at the junction. He pushed through bodies, kept his shoulder low, and moved toward the main intersection where arches met ramps and the crowd funneled into a single passage.

He saw it then. The arch’s crown had dropped. Not far. Less than a hand’s breadth. Enough to change the load path. Enough to pull the abutments outward and make every crack in the joints relevant.

A crew had already thrown up timber shoring. Two vertical posts, one diagonal brace, wedges driven hard. It was the right shape for the wrong situation. The timber bowed under compressive load it wasn’t sized for. The wedges had begun to spit out dust.

“Back,” Jhace said to the closest cluster of refugees.

No one moved. They were cold. They were tired. They were trapped between a failing arch and the open lane where aftershocks still ran under the stone.

Jhace lifted his hammer and pointed at the base of the arch. “That joint goes, this whole throat goes. Move.”

A woman with a boy on her hip stared at him. Her eyes were dry and fixed. The boy’s face was turned inward, pressed to her shoulder.

Jhace swallowed and stepped past her, closer to the shoring crew.

“Who’s lead?” he asked.

A man with mortar on his forearms nodded. “Me. We’re set. We’ll add another brace.”

“Your braces are too long,” Jhace said. “They’ll bow and kick out. You need shorter spans under the crown and a clamp at the base.”

“We don’t have clamps,” the man said.

Jhace looked around. Salvage piles, scattered iron straps, broken brackets, bent nails that wouldn’t hold in soft stone. Too much damage. Too little useful material.

The arch answered with another small drop. Dust shed from the mortar seam. The crowd made a sound that wasn’t words.

Traditional shoring was not going to outrun the failure.

Jhace’s hands shook. The tremor never stopped now. It lived in his wrists. When he tried to grip the hammer tighter, the tremor sharpened, and the handle jittered in his palm.

He set the hammer down on a flat stone block because he did not trust his grip.

A runner said, “Do the glow.”

Jhace’s mouth tightened. The city had already made it into a request. An expectation.

He stepped toward the arch base and reached out.

“Wait.”

The voice cut through the junction noise with precision.

Jhace turned his head.

Tiffani stood on the near ramp, a satchel slung tight, a stick of charcoal in her hand. Dust streaked her coat. Her eyes were on the arch, not on him.

She moved down the ramp with measured Strides, stopped near the base, and knelt. She pressed the charcoal against the stone and drew a line that followed a visible crack. Then another, intersecting. Then a short mark at a joint that had begun to separate.

“Primary fracture network,” she said. “The load is not centered. It is traveling along this route.”

Jhace stared at the marks. They were accurate. They matched what his fingers had already begun to sense in the stone.

“Torrin,” she said. “If you spread across the whole base, you waste. You need to target the joint that’s opening. Here.”

She tapped the charcoal tip on the joint.

Jhace’s heart hammered once. His hands shook more.

He did not want her here. He did not want a watcher. He did not want anyone close enough to see him fail.

The arch dropped again. Dust fell in a thicker curtain, and someone screamed.

Jhace stepped in beside her.

“Keep people clear under the crown,” Tiffani said to the crew chief. “Two Strides back. Now.”

Bodies shifted. Not enough. Some.

Tiffani looked up at Jhace. “Palm contact. On my mark. You will push into this joint, not into the entire arch.”

“How do you know—” Jhace started.

“I’ve been recording,” she said. “Stone shows where it breaks.”

“Mark,” Jhace said.

Tiffani placed her left hand flat against the stone just below the opening joint. “Not there,” she said, shifting her palm half a hand-width. “Here. Direct contact with the joint interface.”

Jhace forced his right hand to steady and set it over hers, skin to skin and skin to stone.

An aftershock ran under the junction. The timber brace bowed. The wedge spit out grit.

Protect. Hold.

Jhace pushed intent into his palm.

Heat flared, sharp. Copper-gold light spread under his skin and into the stone where his hand met the joint. It did not flood everywhere. It followed the line Tiffani had marked, sliding into the crack network that carried load.

The arch’s vibration changed. The deep pulse smoothed. The crown stopped dropping.

Tiffani’s charcoal moved fast. She traced the glow’s route as it traveled, adding marks that anticipated the next stress shift. “Here,” she said. “It’s pulling into the left abutment. Push there next if it slips.”

Jhace’s teeth clenched. The tremor fought him. Heat intensified, and copper-gold lines climbed higher in his forearm than they had during Tide 1. His fingers cramped.

He shifted his left hand to the point she indicated. He pushed again.

The glow answered.

The joint tightened. Not healed. Stabilized. Held in a new alignment that resisted the load that had been tearing it open.

The crowd flowed under the arch in a sudden surge. The shoring crew shouted and reset braces tighter, working in the margin Jhace and Tiffani had created.

Jhace’s knees threatened to buckle. He kept them straight by force.

The arch did not move again.

Tiffani lifted her hand from the stone first. Jhace kept his palm a fraction longer, then forced himself to release.

The glow faded from the stone surface, leaving residue lines that traced the fractures.

Jhace stepped back. His right hand shook hard enough that he could not close his fingers. He tried. The tremor spiked and ran up into his elbow. The copper-gold vein-lines on his forearm were brighter and more extensive.

He swallowed bile and tasted iron at the back of his tongue.

Tiffani watched his hands without flinching. “Your tremor is worse than Tide 1,” she said.

Jhace’s jaw tightened. “I know.”

“You use too broad a push when you act alone,” she said. “You waste contact area. You increase cost. I’ve seen it.”

“Seen it,” Jhace repeated, sharp.

“Recorded it,” Tiffani said.

“Stop,” Jhace said.

Tiffani’s voice stayed even. “If you want to keep holding structures, you will need to stop pretending you can do it without method.”

Jhace shoved his hands into his pockets. The tremor kept going.

Tiffani watched him, then nodded toward the Underface stair throat. “Your forge is down there.”

Jhace started walking. He did not tell her to follow. Her footfalls came behind him with measured Strides.

The junction held.

For the first time since the Piercing, Jhace felt a margin open in the work: not more power, not less risk, but shared load.
""")
doc.add_page_break()

add_heading("CHAPTER 7: Quiet Calculations", level=1)
add_block(r"""
Tiffani spread her maps across the observatory data chamber table and did not let herself look at the sky seam through the high slit windows.

Looking did not change the seam. Recording did.

The chamber smelled of dust and lens oil. The floor was stone. The table had been reinforced with iron straps after the Piercing because even the observatory had shifted. The straps were new enough that they shone in places where hands had touched them repeatedly.

Pella sat across from her with his own ledger open. He had ink on his fingers and a fatigue line at the corner of his mouth that had deepened over the last few Tides.

“You’re back early,” he said.

“I came to compile,” Tiffani said.

“You came to count,” Pella said.

Tiffani unwrapped the oilcloth and opened to the first page after the Piercing. She laid a straightedge along the margin and began transferring points to the larger grid map pinned to a board beside the table.

Each point represented a hold event. Each mark carried a coded set of notes: structure type, activation trigger context, effect magnitude, and observed cost indicators.

She did not write Jhace’s name on the public grid. She used User A.

Pella tapped his pen against his own ledger. “How many now?”

“Seventeen logged across five hold sites,” Tiffani said. “Nine direct stabilizations. Three partial. Five failures under non-critical conditions.”

Pella exhaled slowly. “That is more than the council understands.”

“The council understands panic,” Tiffani said.

She drew the curve line on a secondary sheet: a simple axis grid. Horizontal: sequence of activations. Vertical: observed cost severity, converted into subdivisions based on tremor amplitude and vein-line persistence.

The first activation had been the baseline event. She had treated it as an outlier until she had more points.

Now she had points.

Tremor baseline at rest: increased after each use. Vein-lines: appearing for longer durations post-use, then persisting faintly even without use. Recovery time: longer. Grip capacity: reduced. Adjacent symptoms: nausea, gait compensation, increased reliance on palm contact rather than tool grip.

Tiffani plotted the points.

The line did not rise steadily.

It rose, then rose faster.

She stopped with the pen hovering and checked her conversions. She recalculated. She adjusted one value down by half a subdivision to account for observational uncertainty. The curve still bent.

Not linear.

Compounding.

“Pella,” she said.

He looked up. “That tone means you found something.”

“I found the thing I did not want,” Tiffani said.

Pella leaned over her chart. “This is not straight.”

“No,” Tiffani said. “It accelerates.”

Pella stared at the curve. “Each use costs more than the last.”

“Yes,” Tiffani said. “The cost is not additive. It multiplies.” fileciteturn24file3

Pella’s voice lowered. “What do you do with this?”

Tiffani looked at the hold site map, at the choke points marked in charcoal and ink, at the note she had written after talking to Marisse.

Engage. Anchor the work.

She had engaged. She had been at the junction. She had watched Jhace’s tremor worsen. She had watched the vein-lines persist.

Now she had proof.

“Method,” Tiffani said.

Pella blinked. “Method?”

“He does not target efficiently when alone,” Tiffani said. “When he targets with marks, he spends less contact area. The effect is cleaner. The cost reduces.”

“That is a hypothesis,” Pella said.

“It repeats,” Tiffani said. “When it repeats, it is real.”

Pella sat back. “And if it stops repeating?”

Tiffani’s mouth went dry.

“Then the curve still exists,” she said. “And the city still asks.”

Pella glanced toward the slit window. The seam was visible if he shifted his chair. He did not look long. “You could hand this to the Office.”

“No,” Tiffani said.

“They would lock him behind a ledger,” Pella said. “He would be alive.”

“He would be owned,” Tiffani said.

Pella’s gaze held her. “Be careful.”

Tiffani closed the logbook and tied the oilcloth wrap. “I am careful. That is why I’m going.”

She left the data chamber and moved through the observatory corridors that had once felt permanent. Now she checked the joints as she walked. She counted the subdivisions of sag in the stair throat. She noted new iron straps.

Outside, Saint Merro carried itself with visible strain. Dust hung in pockets between buildings. Soulpulse residue glinted faintly on repaired seams where the light had traveled and left evidence. People moved with loaded bundles. Shoring crews worked without pause.

Tiffani crossed the Kiln Corridor again, past Oven 14’s vent line, past the shored arch she had logged earlier. She watched the brace hold. She watched the crowd flow.

Her destination was not a hold site.

Her destination was a forge under stone.

She descended the Underface stair throat two levels. The air grew warmer. The noise of the crowd dulled, replaced by the lower resonance of tunnels and ironwork.

She reached a door reinforced with an iron crossbar. Heat seeped through the cracks around it. Managed heat. Forge heat.

Tiffani adjusted her satchel strap, placed her palm briefly against the door to feel the vibration behind it, then lifted her hand and knocked.
""")
doc.add_page_break()

add_heading("INTERLUDE I: Corlexi — System vs Chaos", level=1)
add_block(r"""
Corlexi entered the Crown’s record tier and felt the pressure of too many edges.

The corridor was straight. The walls were plumb. The floor was laid in repeating squares. The air carried voices and paper-dust and wet ink. Sound arrived in stacked layers, petitions read aloud over one another, stamps striking, boots scuffing in uneven cadence. Corlexi’s skull held it all and did not filter.

He pressed two fingers to the inside of his wrist and counted pulses until the count steadied. The overload sat behind his eyes.

A clerk brushed past with a bundle of forms. The bundle’s corners were misaligned by less than a span. The misalignment pulled at Corlexi’s attention until he turned and watched the clerk disappear through a door marked with fresh paint.

RESONANCE REPORTS.

New letters. New authority. New boxes for unknown things.

Corlexi moved deeper. He passed tables where recorders sat shoulder to shoulder, hunched over slate and paper, each one tracing lines meant to become law. The tables were set in parallel. The stools were not. One stool leg was short. A recorder braced a knee against it. The knee tremor transferred into the table. The tremor transferred into ink lines.

Corlexi’s teeth tightened. He stepped to the stool, tapped the short leg with his toe, then slid a thin wedge beneath it. He pressed down until the wobble ceased.

The recorder looked up, startled.

“Continue,” Corlexi said.

The recorder continued, hand steadier. The ink line became straight again.

At the central desk, an overseer argued with two guild men. A third figure stood behind the overseer, quieter, cleaner. He held a ledger that was new enough to still smell of glue. He watched without moving his mouth.

A runner arrived and laid a pouch on the desk. Copper-gold dust sifted from the pouch’s seam when it hit the wood. A few grains hovered longer than they should have, then settled along the desk’s scratches, following grooves made by years of writing.

Corlexi stared at the grains until his vision sharpened.

The grains followed stress lines.

He reached forward. His fingertip touched the desk’s surface near the dust. The wood felt warm from constant hands, but the warmth did not match the building’s baseline. A pulse ran through Corlexi’s finger and into the wood.

Not a flare. An alignment. The desk’s tiny cracks no longer drew his eye. Their edges seated closer. The dust grains shifted, then stopped.

The runner gasped. The overseer went still. The quiet policy man’s gaze snapped to Corlexi’s hand.

Corlexi pulled his finger back. His skin remained unmarked. No tremor followed. No heat climbed his forearm. The act left no visible invoice on him, only a sensation of rightness settling behind his ribs.

The policy man said, softly, “Do it again.”

Corlexi looked at him. “No.”

The overseer blinked. “You can’t refuse. This is Crown work.”

Corlexi scanned the room. Tables. Doors. People waiting with papers that represented food, shelter, timber allocations, burial permissions. The system was trying to form around a force it did not understand. It would squeeze until the force fit. It would squeeze the people inside it.

Corlexi spoke without raising his voice. “You are building a cage. You are calling it order.”

The policy man’s jaw tightened. “Order keeps the city from falling.”

Corlexi looked toward the windows. Through the glass, the sky seam cut pale light into unequal halves. The seam was straight. The world beneath it was not.

Order could not be declared. It had to be seated under load.

Corlexi stepped away from the desk. The dust grains remained where they had settled. Pens resumed their scratch. Arguments resumed in lower register.

Corlexi walked toward the door marked RESONANCE REPORTS and stopped before crossing its threshold. He put his palm against the doorframe stone. The stone carried a fine vibration, constant, present since the Piercing. The vibration entered his bones.

He did not have language for what was forming. He knew the shape: lists becoming mandates, mandates becoming enforcement, enforcement becoming a new load-bearing column in the city’s politics.

A column built too quickly failed first.

Corlexi lifted his hand from the stone and left the record tier without looking back, carrying a single conclusion:

If they forced the world into their rectangles, the world would break their rectangles.
""")
doc.add_page_break()

add_heading("CHAPTER 8: Proof and Resistance", level=1)
add_block(r"""
Jhace kept his hands busy because stillness made the tremor feel louder.

Forge heat settled into a steady breath. The bellows line held. The quench trough sat half-full, water dulled by ash and grit. Jhace reshaped three brackets since Kindling and each came out with the same faint warp—small, tolerable, proof that his grip was no longer stable.

He set a rivet on the anvil block, aligned it by touch, and brought the hammer down.

The hammer head struck true. His wrist did not.

The rivet skittered off the block and clicked against stone.

He bent, retrieved it, and tried again with slower motion. The ache in his forearm flared, a deep pull along copper-gold vein-lines that never fully faded.

A footstep sounded at the forge mouth. Measured Stride. No drag.

Tiffani stepped inside and shut the door without letting it slam. She carried her logbook tucked under one arm and a chalk cord in the other hand.

Bram sat on the bench near the heat-grid line and watched both of them with the same expression he used for a pipe joint that might fail.

“You’re early,” Bram said.

“Tide doesn’t wait for courtesy,” Tiffani said.

Jhace wiped his palms on his apron. “What do you want?”

“I want you to stop wasting your cost,” she said.

Jhace’s jaw tightened. “I don’t waste anything.”

Tiffani’s gaze moved to his hands. “You drop rivets.”

Bram made a short sound.

Tiffani set her logbook on the worktable, opened it, and turned it so Jhace could see the columns.

“Here,” she said. “Saint Merro junction. Tremor climbed into your elbow. You lost fine control for most of a Tide.”

Jhace did not deny it.

Tiffani turned a page. “Next stabilization, with chalk-line targeting. Tremor stayed in your wrist. Vein-lines remained visible but did not spread beyond mid-forearm. Recovery within a span.”

Jhace stared at the numbers she’d written in the margin. He understood pattern even when the conversion system was not his.

Bram leaned forward. “That repeats?”

“It repeats,” Tiffani said.

Jhace felt heat rise. “You’re trying to manage me.”

“I’m trying to keep you from breaking,” she said.

“Same thing,” Jhace said.

Tiffani’s eyes narrowed. “No. Management is control for the controller. This has an external reference: you keep living.”

Jhace stared at her, then at the logbook. The truth remained.

“Show,” he said.

Tiffani’s shoulders eased by a fraction. “We go to a site. Real risk. Real need.”

Bram stood. “And I go too.”

Jhace started to object.

Bram held up a hand. “No arguing. We keep it simple.”

They walked through Saint Merro’s lower streets where ash still settled in corners and the sky seam cut a straight pale line overhead. The city had a new sound: constant small creaks, the rasp of grit under boots, the irregular thrum of aftershocks.

Tiffani led them to a covered corridor connecting two refugee blocks. The roof was patched with salvaged sheet and glass panels, mismatched. Supports were timber braces married to stone posts that were not designed for wood.

The brace nearest the midpoint bowed a hair under load.

Jhace felt it before he saw it. The vibration in the floor changed—uneven. A small drift that suggested a joint losing its seat.

“If that post slips,” Jhace said, “it will pull the roof.”

“It will,” Tiffani said. “This corridor is the only path between camps without crossing open rubble.”

Bram nodded once. “Real need.”

Tiffani knelt near the post and ran chalk cord from post to brace. She snapped it and traced the stress line she could see with trained eyes.

“Contact here,” she said. “On the seam.”

Jhace pressed his palm to the seam between post and brace. Stone cool. Wood dry. The joint between them wrong.

He felt the corridor’s load through the seam. People passing. A plank shifting. Weight that should have traveled down a clean path wandering into a weak brace.

He waited for the surge.

“Not yet,” Tiffani said.

Jhace’s jaw clenched. “It is failing.”

“It is drifting,” she said. “Wait for the trigger.”

A shoring worker stepped under the midpoint with a plank on his shoulder. The brace groaned. The post slipped a fraction. The corridor roof crackled.

Protective need hit Jhace’s chest. Copper-gold heat flooded his palm.

The glow ran into the seam in narrow channels, tracing the stress line Tiffani had marked. Jhace felt the flow anchor into the joint’s weakest point, then spread outward in a lattice that followed load.

The brace stopped bowing. The post stopped slipping. The roof’s crackle ceased.

Jhace pulled his hand back.

The tremor in his wrist rose, then held. It did not climb into his forearm. His fingers stayed able to curl.

Tiffani stepped close, eyes on his vein-lines. “Visible,” she said. “Contained.”

Bram exhaled. “Cleaner,” he said.

Jhace flexed his fingers. The motion came without a drop.

“That was smaller,” Jhace said.

“It was targeted,” Tiffani replied. “Your force did not have to fill the whole structure. It filled the failure path.”

Jhace looked at her. “You made me wait.”

“I prevented waste,” Tiffani said. “Conditional activation punishes early pushes.” fileciteturn24file3

Bram stepped between them half a stride. “Proof is proof,” he said. “Quit sparring.”

Jhace swallowed. “Next crisis,” he said. “You mark. I hold. We see if it repeats.”

Tiffani closed her logbook. “It will repeat,” she said. “If you let it.”
""")

add_heading("CHAPTER 9: Ash and Claims", level=1)
add_block(r"""
The Glass Quarter had a smell that never fully left: ash, wet grit, old heat trapped in brick.

Now the camp sat in that smell. Tents and patched canopies filled the spaces where ovens once stood. Broken panes were stacked into crude windbreaks. People moved through the lanes in slow Strides, careful with weight because the ground still shifted in aftershocks that made debris talk.

Jhace hated camps. They were structure without design. Load without plan. Failure waiting for the wrong footstep.

Tiffani moved beside him, eyes tracking support posts, brace angles, rope ties cutting into fabric. Her logbook was strapped tight against her ribs.

They reached a distribution line where water cups were being filled from a barrel. A child at the front of the line coughed into her sleeve. The cough sounded wet.

Tellis stood near the barrel with two of his people, clean coats that looked wrong in this place. His gaze swept the line as if it were inventory.

Corvin stood with him, coat dusted, posture composed. He held a rolled map and a narrow ledger.

Jhace stopped. His shoulders tightened. Tiffani’s breathing changed, subtle.

Tellis smiled. “Master Torrin. You keep moving. That makes you hard to supply.”

Jhace’s voice came flat. “I don’t want supply.”

Corvin stepped forward, not smiling. “Then you will keep bleeding the city of your function. That is waste.”

Tiffani’s eyes narrowed. “You speak in claims,” she said.

Corvin looked at her. “And you speak in measurements. We both believe patterns matter.”

Jhace did not like Corvin’s “we.”

Tellis gestured toward the camp. “This is what uncoordinated help looks like. Ash and hunger. People sleeping under glass scrap.”

A brace crew shouted nearby as a post slipped. The post was caught, re-seated.

Corvin lifted his map. “We can stabilize this district. We can bring timber. Iron. Skilled crews. We can redirect water routes. We can enforce load limits.”

“And you,” Tellis added, “can stop crawling through rubble for free.”

Jhace’s jaw set. “There is a string.”

Corvin unrolled his map on two crates bridged by a plank. “Registration,” he said. “Scheduled deployment. A team assigned to you. You operate under authority. You report incidents. You stop acting alone.”

Tellis leaned in. “And water for your forge. Fuel. Metal stock. Tools replaced when they break.”

Jhace felt the offer’s weight. Order. Resources. The ability to stop choosing who got saved by who reached them first.

Tiffani spoke, quieter, for Jhace alone. “We consult.”

Jhace nodded once.

They stepped behind a stack of broken panes that blocked direct sight.

Tiffani turned to him. “What do you want?” she asked.

Jhace stared at the ground. “I want them to stop,” he said.

“Who,” Tiffani said.

“Everything,” he answered.

Tiffani waited.

Jhace forced the rest out. “I want to work. I want my forge. I want quiet. I don’t want a leash.”

Tiffani’s jaw tightened. “You will get one if you accept their supply.”

Jhace looked at her. “And you?”

“I want you alive,” Tiffani said. “I want your cost below collapse. I want the method to stay ours, not theirs.”

Jhace swallowed. “We refuse,” he said.

Tiffani nodded once. “Together.”

They walked back to the makeshift table.

“I’m not registering,” Jhace said.

Tellis’s smile faltered by a fraction. Corvin’s expression did not change.

“We will still stabilize sites,” Jhace continued. “We will not report to you. We will not be assigned.”

Corvin’s voice stayed calm. “Then you choose chaos.”

Jhace held his gaze. “I choose to keep my hands mine.”

Tellis spread his hands. “We’re offering to protect them.”

Tiffani stepped forward half a stride. “Protection that requires obedience is control. We decline.”

Corvin rolled his map back up. “Results without structure fail,” he said. “You will be blamed when it breaks.”

Tellis’s eyes narrowed. “And you will need water.”

Jhace picked up his hammer. The tremor in his fingers made the haft buzz against his palm.

“We’ll take our chances,” he said.

They turned away. Corvin’s voice carried toward the crowd. “Remember this. When the next collapse comes, ask who refused order.”

Jhace kept walking. Ash clung to his boots. Tiffani stayed beside him.

Enemies had been named. Lines had been drawn. Lines were easier than drift.
""")
