add_heading("CHAPTER 10: Near Collapse", level=1)
add_block(r"""
The main support failed with a sound that did not belong to stone.

It began as a low grind through the pavement under Jhace’s boots, then a sharp crack that ran up the nearest column. Dust burst from a joint high overhead. A brace plate popped free and clattered down the steps.

People screamed. The scream was not one voice. It was a crowd becoming panic.

Jhace saw the structure in pieces at once: the viaduct span carrying evacuees, the column cluster holding the upper terrace, the abutment where old mortar had been patched twice since the Piercing.

A load path had shifted. The shift was running.

Tiffani was already moving. Chalk in hand. Eyes tracking column faces, catching stress lines that appeared as fine dust fractures.

“Clear the run!” she shouted to the nearest shoring crew. “Off the center lane.”

Her voice carried instruction, not pleading.

Jhace’s hands were shaking at rest. He ran to the first column, pressed his palm to the stone where the crack had opened, and waited for trigger.

Trigger arrived. The viaduct above shifted downward by a fraction. People stumbled. A child slid toward the edge. A hand grabbed the child and missed.

Protective need hit Jhace’s chest hard enough to snap breath.

Copper-gold heat flooded his palm. The glow ran into the crack seam the way Tiffani’s method demanded: narrow, seam-first, following the failure path.

The joint held long enough for the span to settle.

“Next,” Tiffani said, close.

Jhace released. His wrist tremor flared, then steadied. He could still close his fingers.

Tiffani snapped chalk cord across the next joint. “Here,” she said. “Bed seam. It is sliding.”

Mark. Contact. Hold. Release. Move.

They worked in rhythm.

The structure kept failing anyway.

A secondary crack opened along the abutment. Dust poured out steady. The viaduct’s vibration deepened.

“That is too much mass,” Jhace said.

“The upper terrace is moving,” Tiffani said. “If it drops, it takes the viaduct.”

Scale was wrong. Too many people. Too much stone. Too little time.

Jhace stabilized a joint. Then another. Each time the glow ran clean. Each time he released, his tremor climbed a notch.

Tiffani stopped writing. The logbook stayed strapped to her ribs. Data could wait. Bodies could not.

A shout rose from above. “It’s dropping!”

Jhace looked up and saw the terrace edge shift downward by a span.

Mass above was coming.

He ran to the main column cluster and set both palms onto the stone.

Trigger arrived immediate and absolute. Hundreds of lives in the load above.

Copper-gold surged into both arms. The flow ran deeper than before, spreading into the column cluster’s internal lattice. Jhace felt stress lines through his bones: tension, shear, compression biting down. Sensation came too fast.

He pushed harder.

The terrace stopped dropping. For a breath. Then it tried again.

Jhace’s teeth clenched. Tremor turned into full-arm shake under the glow. Vein-lines brightened and climbed past mid-forearm toward elbow.

A thin smear of blood appeared at one nostril.

Tiffani stepped close. She saw the spread beyond her earlier ranges. She scanned the structure: terrace edge, viaduct lane, abutment seam. Shoring crews rushed toward Jhace with a timber brace. Their steps added load to the zone he held.

“Stop!” Tiffani shouted. “Hold position. Do not add mass to the column bed.”

They froze.

“Brace the side run,” Tiffani said, pointing with chalk. “If the side run fails, the crowd crushes the center lane. Move.”

They moved.

Tiffani grabbed Jhace’s wrist. Heat burned her palm through his skin.

“Release,” she said.

Jhace’s jaw locked. “No.”

The terrace creaked again.

Tiffani’s voice stayed steady. “If you hold until you drop, it drops anyway. Everyone dies. You included.”

Jhace kept holding.

Tiffani stepped in front of him, blocking his line to the terrace. “Look at me,” she said.

Jhace’s eyes snapped to hers, unfocused at first, then catching. The glow flickered under his skin.

“You will release on my count,” Tiffani said. “You will not decide alone.”

“One,” she said.

Jhace did not release.

Tiffani forced his palm to break contact by a fraction. The surge stuttered. Jhace gasped, raw. His knees almost buckled.

“Two,” Tiffani said.

Jhace slammed his palm back to the stone. Copper-gold flared again, thinner. The terrace creak eased.

“Three,” Tiffani said.

Jhace released again, shaking hard. Breath ragged. Eyes watering. Sensory overload pressed close.

Tiffani snapped chalk cord across a lower bed seam and shouted, “Brace here. Drive wedges. Seat the plate.”

Mallets thudded. Wedges bit. The brace took load. The terrace steadied enough that Jhace did not have to hold the whole mass alone.

“Off,” Tiffani said. “Full release.”

Jhace pulled both palms away. The glow faded. The terrace held on the new braces, groaning but not dropping.

Jhace swayed. Legs nearly gave.

Tiffani caught him under the arm and dragged him two Strides back from the column bed. His skin was hot. His tremor was full-body.

People streamed past them, still evacuating, still alive.

“What were you doing?” Tiffani said, low.

“Holding,” Jhace said.

“That was not holding,” she said. “That was spending yourself past collapse.”

Jhace’s eyes flashed. “Don’t touch me.”

“I will,” Tiffani said. “If you cannot stop yourself, I will stop you.”

“You don’t own me,” Jhace said.

“I don’t want to own you,” Tiffani said. “I want you alive.”

He wiped his nose and stared at the smear of red on his hand.

“You made me let go,” he said.

“Yes,” Tiffani said. “Because you will not die under a column to satisfy your compulsion.”

Jhace’s rage had nowhere clean to go. Tiffani’s certainty did not bend.

Behind them, the main support still groaned, but it held.

They turned back to the work in dust and noise, working together with silent fury between them.

Neither let go of the other.
""")
doc.add_page_break()

add_heading("INTERLUDE II: Mobel — Purity Rising", level=1)
add_block(r"""
Mobel trusted pain.

Pain stripped error from the body. It drew attention to the exact place where failure began.

He knelt at the salt pans where stone trays stepped down toward the sea. The Piercing’s far tremor had shifted the basins. The seams opened. Brine leaked into soil and left crusted white lines that spread in branching paths.

Impurity traveled through openings.

Mobel pressed his thumb into a crack until the sharp edge bit skin. He welcomed the bite. Salt air carried rot from a fish pile too long in heat and smoke from a distant cooking fire.

He stood and walked the pan edges, bare soles careful on broken stone. He carried a wood wedge and a small hammer. He set the wedge into a seam and tapped it in with controlled strikes. The wedge seated. The seam narrowed. The leak slowed.

Behind him, voices rose.

Two men dragged a third across grit and dropped him near the pans. The man convulsed. Foam gathered at his lips. His hands clawed at the ground, nails split, fingers shaking in short pulses.

Mobel watched for pattern. Tremor, cough, breath hitch. Heat in skin. The man’s eyes rolled, then locked on Mobel with a pleading stare.

“He touched the gold-dust,” someone said.

“He tried to hold a wall with his hands,” another said.

Mobel stepped forward. The crowd backed a half stride, making space without being asked.

He crouched beside the convulsing man. He did not touch him yet. He looked at the man’s palms. The skin split along the same lines brine split stone. Fine copper-gold grains clung to sweat at the wrist and the crease of the thumb. The grains shimmered when Mobel shifted his angle to the sun.

The man rasped, “Help.”

Mobel asked, “What did you take in?”

“I breathed it,” the man said. “I thought it would make me strong.”

Strength without method produced waste. Waste produced collapse.

Mobel placed his palm on the man’s forehead. Fever. Wrong heat.

A woman said, “Don’t. He’ll spread it.”

Mobel lifted his hand and stood. “Bring boiling brine,” he said. “Bring clean cloth. No reused rags.”

One dragger blinked. “You’ll scald him.”

Mobel looked down at the man’s shaking hands. “He is being eaten from inside. I remove what does not belong.”

When the brine arrived, steam rolling off it, Mobel took the vessel without cloth. Heat licked his palms. He did not drop it. Pain confirmed control of grip.

He poured a thin stream over the man’s wrists first. The man screamed and thrashed. Two people held his shoulders down. Mobel scrubbed with clean cloth until copper-gold grains washed away into sand. He poured again. He scrubbed again. He did not stop when the man begged. Begging did not change contamination.

The crowd’s sound shifted. Less panic. More certainty.

When the man’s convulsions eased, Mobel leaned closer. Fever remained, but shimmer grains no longer clung at the creases. Breath steadied into a rough rhythm.

Mobel rinsed his own hands in seawater. Salt stung cracks in his skin. He welcomed it.

He looked up at the horizon.

The sky seam cut the air in a straight pale line. Mobel felt pressure behind his eyes when he stared at it. A push for alignment.

A second pressure answered it from farther away, softer, pulling toward closure.

A third sensation moved at the edge of perception, loosening, inviting joints to fail.

Mobel did not name any of it. He only knew the world had stacked wrongs in it now.

He looked back at the crowd. They watched him with expectation.

Purpose settled into his chest, heavy and clean.

If impurity entered through cracks, cracks had to be sealed. If people chased copper-gold without method, method had to be enforced. If enforcement required pain, pain would be used.

Mobel walked away from the pans carrying a rule that had formed fully in him:

Clean first. Always.
""")
doc.add_page_break()

add_heading("CHAPTER 11: Holding Together", level=1)
add_block(r"""
Jhace sat on forge stone with his back against the wall because benches required balance he did not want to spend.

The forge’s heat was steady. The heat-grid hissed through the vent. Cooling iron ticked. A pipe seam dripped in slow rhythm. The city’s groan came down in dull waves from above, but the forge held its own enclosure.

Jhace’s hands lay in his lap, palms up. The tremor ran anyway. Fine. Constant. It lived at the edges of his fingers and did not obey breath.

Copper-gold vein-lines remained faint under skin. They did not disappear anymore. In forge heat, they pulsed once in a while, then settled.

Bram slept on the bench with boots still on, one arm over his eyes. Jhace checked Bram’s breathing without meaning to. Chest rose. Fell. Rose.

The forge door creaked.

Tiffani stepped in and shut it without letting it slam. She carried no chalk cord. Only a cloth-wrapped bundle held tight in one hand.

She saw Jhace on the floor and paused. Assessment, not pity.

“Bram asleep?” she said.

Jhace nodded.

Tiffani unwrapped the bundle. Clean cloth strips, a small tin of salve, a short needle sealed in wax-paper.

Jhace’s jaw tightened. “I’m not bleeding.”

“You were,” Tiffani said. “Dried blood under your nose. Skin too hot. Eyes too bright.”

“I’m fine,” Jhace said.

Tiffani crossed the forge and crouched in front of him. “Hands,” she said.

Jhace didn’t move.

Tiffani waited. The waiting was steady pressure.

Jhace lifted his hands. Tremor made the movement sloppy, then settled back into its fine constant vibration.

Tiffani took his right wrist, thumb pressing below the pulse point, then turned his palm over. The skin looked normal until stared at. Then the faint branching lines appeared, a map under skin.

“Still visible,” Tiffani said.

“It’s nothing,” Jhace said.

“It isn’t nothing,” she said. “Nothing doesn’t leave a map.”

She dabbed salve onto the heel of his palm where the skin had started to crack from repeated contact. The salve cooled the surface. Jhace’s nerves flared anyway, oversensitized after the last crisis. He flinched.

Tiffani paused. “Pain?”

“No,” Jhace said.

She resumed, slower. “That was a yes spoken with your shoulders.”

She wrapped his palm with cloth, snug but not tight. Then the wrist. Then the left hand the same way. Her fingers moved with calm competence.

When she finished, she sat back on her heels.

“What are you doing here?” Jhace asked.

“You almost died,” Tiffani said.

“I didn’t,” Jhace said.

“You tried,” Tiffani said.

Memory flashed: palms on the column bed, terrace mass pushing through his arms, her hand on his wrist forcing release. His rage. Her refusal.

“You forced me,” Jhace said.

“Yes,” Tiffani said.

“That isn’t your right,” Jhace said.

“It became my right when you stopped choosing survival,” she said.

Jhace looked away toward the firebox. “You don’t know what it’s like when it starts to go.”

“Tell me,” Tiffani said.

Jhace’s throat tightened. The old count rose behind his ribs.

“I have names,” he said.

Tiffani nodded once. “I know you carry them.”

Jhace spoke them anyway, voice low and rough. “Herran. Sova. Relk. Jind. Naro. Elsha. Vem. Orin. Tael. Bruni. Keshen. Lior. Dassa. Fen. Morret. Ulne. Haska. Pellor. Jessa. Ruun. Tareth. Mova. Kerr.”

His breath caught on the last one.

“Twenty-three,” he said. “Kiln hall fall. Before the seam.”

Tiffani’s breathing slowed, heavier.

“I heard the first crack,” Jhace said. “I told myself it was noise. I told myself the braces would hold. I said I’d check later. Then it went. Brick, beam, glass. Dust so thick I couldn’t see my hands. I pulled two out alive. Then I pulled bodies until my nails tore and my arms stopped working.”

He looked at Tiffani. “After that, I don’t let later exist.”

Tiffani’s voice came quiet. “So when something starts to go now—”

“I hold,” Jhace said. “I won’t add another name.”

Silence filled the forge.

Bram shifted in sleep and exhaled.

Tiffani’s eyes held Jhace’s. “Marisse told me signal,” she said. “Before the Piercing. Before Saint Merro learned to listen.”

Jhace remembered Marisse’s hands, always close to her wrists, always ready to feel for tremor.

“She was right,” Tiffani said. “I treated her as noise.”

Jhace’s stomach tightened.

“She lived long enough under rubble to tell me where voids were,” Tiffani said. “I stood outside and shouted instructions because I feared the structure would take me too.”

Her throat worked once. “I keep her name because I left her.”

Jhace did not know how to answer without making it worse. He shifted closer until their knees nearly touched.

He lifted his bandaged hand slow and placed his palm lightly over her wrist. Contact. Not control.

Tiffani inhaled sharply, then let breath out. She turned her hand under his and curled fingers around his wrist. Steady. She held his tremor without pretending to stop it.

“Non-crisis,” Jhace said. “First contact should be that.”

Tiffani’s mouth tightened. “I said that for you.”

“Then take it too,” Jhace said.

Tiffani’s grip stayed steady. “I don’t erase cost,” she said. “I reduce waste.”

Jhace nodded once. “If you tell me to release again…”

“I will,” Tiffani said. “When you cross into collapse.”

“And if I hate you for it,” Jhace said.

“Then hate me alive,” Tiffani said.

Jhace swallowed. Brutal. Clean. True.

“Stay,” Jhace said.

Tiffani shifted closer, shoulder brushing his.

When sleep finally took him, it wasn’t because fear had left.

It was because his weight was not held by his hands alone.
""")
doc.add_page_break()

add_heading("CHAPTER 12: Choosing the Weight", level=1)
add_block(r"""
Saint Merro looked stitched.

Timber braces crossed cracked facades. Iron straps cinched pillars that had no business still standing. Fresh mortar patches gleamed pale against soot-dark stone. The city held itself together by density of effort.

Tiffani walked with Jhace along a lane that used to be a market run. Now it was a supply corridor. People moved in steady lines, bundles balanced, eyes down, shoulders hunched against dust and the constant low threat of another shudder.

Jhace’s tremor was visible even when his hands were in his pockets. It traveled into his forearms, into the angle of his shoulders.

He was alive.

They passed a brace crew re-seating wedges under a lintel. The crew chief glanced at Jhace’s hands, then looked away quickly.

Need was everywhere.

At the lane’s bend, a child had drawn the sky seam in ash on a wall—one straight line, then smaller lines branching from it. An adult wiped it away with a sleeve.

Tiffani tracked the lane’s load-bearing points: where rubble had been cleared, where it hadn’t; where people still funneled through a single throat; where a new brace had been placed correctly, and where an old brace was quietly bowing.

Uneven success. Uneven attention. Uneven survival.

Jhace spoke without turning his head. “They’re watching.”

Tiffani caught the reflection in a broken pane propped against a wall. Two figures farther back, moving at the same pace. Coats dusted enough to pretend they belonged.

“Office,” Tiffani said.

“Corvin,” Jhace said.

“Maybe,” Tiffani said. “Or his hands.”

They passed a posted notice nailed to a brace post. A fresh seal: intersecting arcs around a dot.

ALL RESONANT INCIDENTS MUST BE REPORTED. ALL USERS MUST REGISTER.

System gathering itself.

Across the lane, a Water-Guild runner hauled a small barrel toward a secured courtyard where only certain people were allowed entry. Two guards with clubs kept the path clear.

Allocation wearing a polite coat.

Tiffani felt pattern settle: factions aligning, each trying to lay claim to the method that had emerged from crisis.

“We don’t have control,” Tiffani said.

Jhace’s eyes stayed forward. “We never did.”

“But we can choose what we hold,” Tiffani said.

“And what we don’t,” Jhace said.

They turned off the main lane and climbed toward a small overlook above the forge district, rubble cleared enough to create breathing space. From here the patched tiers of Saint Merro showed their seams. Smoke threads rose from functioning kilns. Crowd movement ran through choke points in slow streams.

The sky seam sat above everything, straight and pale.

Jhace stopped at the overlook edge and rested bandaged hands on the stone cap. Tremor traveled into the stone and back into him.

“You feel it,” Tiffani said.

“It doesn’t let me forget,” Jhace said.

They returned to the forge.

Bram stood near the worktable, arms crossed. Pella stood beside him with ink stains on his fingers, posture steady. Two brace workers lingered near the door. Chloen sat on a low stool, leg bound, face pale, eyes clear. She watched Jhace’s hands with attention that carried memory.

Tiffani handed Jhace a chunk of pale chalk.

His fingers closed around it and shook. Chalk dust fell from the edge.

Bram spoke. “Three. Not two. Not ten. Three holds.”

Three points made a stable stand.

Tiffani nodded. “Three priorities,” she said.

Jhace lifted the chalk to the wall. His hand trembled against soot-black stone. He forced the first line down with slow pressure, letting the tremor chatter the chalk in tiny skips.

HOLD WHAT IS REAL.

He wrote the second line slower.

NAME THE COST.

He wrote the third line, rough letters, clear meaning.

CHOOSE TOGETHER.

When he finished, the chalk snapped in his grip and crumbled. Fine dust fell onto his bandage.

Tiffani stepped forward and drew a simple mark beneath the lines: two hands linked at the wrists, one trembling, one steady.

A brace worker stepped in and touched the wall lightly. The other followed, palm beside the first. Chloen reached out and placed her fingertips against CHOOSE TOGETHER, then pulled back, breath tight.

Bram grunted. “Good,” he said.

Jhace checked his nose for blood. Nothing.

He turned to Tiffani. The tremor in his hand reached for her without him deciding it. Habit. Need.

Tiffani took his hand.

Her grip was firm enough to brace, gentle enough to leave the tremor visible. Jhace felt the shake travel into her fingers and stop. Held, not fixed.

Outside, the city still groaned. The Office still posted notices. The Guild still carried water where it chose.

The seam in the sky still cut the day.

Inside the forge, three lines on a wall made a structure they could stand under.

Jhace squeezed Tiffani’s hand. She squeezed back.
""")
doc.add_page_break()

add_heading("EPILOGUE: The Auditor — The Not-Room", level=1)
add_block(r"""
The Auditor entered the space that refused the name room.

Stone surrounded it. Underface carried weight above it. Heat mains ran near enough that a normal chamber would have held warmth in its joints. This space held none of that. Sound arrived dulled. Vibration arrived cut. The floor took the Auditor’s boot and gave nothing back.

A slate was set down where a table should have been. No surface declared itself. The slate remained set.

Three slates followed, placed in a line, edges aligned by fingertip distance. Alignment held. The place permitted record, not structure.

Copper-gold dust hung at the center. It did not fall. It did not rise. It gathered into thin planes and cords that mapped stress without material. The cords tightened, then separated into three impressions.

The first impression formed around ink, seals, and squared stone.

Corlexi moved through Crown chambers where tables sat in grids and stools sat wrong. The impression sharpened on corrections: a wobbling leg wedged, a ledger squared to an inlay, a doorframe touched. The touch shifted a crack’s edge tighter by a fraction and left no visible cost on skin.

The second impression formed around salt, scalding steam, and hands that did not flinch.

Mobel stood at a coastal pan where stone trays had opened seams. He seated wedges with controlled taps. He poured boiling brine over a convulsing man’s wrists and scrubbed until copper-gold grains washed away. Pain registered and did not interrupt the sequence. A crowd’s fear aligned behind him, reshaping into appetite for rule.

The third impression did not begin with place. It began with linkage.

Jhace Torrin’s hands trembled at rest. Tremor ran through fingers and wrists, present even with empty palms. Copper-gold vein-lines remained faint under skin without contact. The body carried invoice forward.

Beside him, Tiffani’s presence acted as constraint. She reduced waste. She narrowed holds to true failure paths. She interrupted caretaker compulsion when it pushed past safe limits. She did not erase cost. She made cost survivable.

The Auditor’s hand hovered above the first slate.

Then observation became record.

I wrote the first line short: Three vectors confirmed.

I kept the next lines anchored to what the world had shown, not what it might mean.

Vector One — Seating. Corlexi sought hierarchy in joints and grids. Deviation registered as threat. Compliance registered as stability. Refusal did not end the system’s hunger. It redirected it.

Vector Two — Cleansing. Mobel sought correction through removal. Contamination registered as fact. Pain became process control. A crowd accepted the sequence and began to mirror it.

Vector Three — Bracing. Jhace and Tiffani formed a composite brace. Load redistributed between them. Method reduced waste under activation and delayed failure. Refusal of ownership increased external pressure.

I listened through the not-room’s damped boundary.

Above, Saint Merro held by timber and iron and exhausted hands. Aftershocks continued. Cracks patched once opened again along old planes.

The city responded in the ways it knew.

The Guild tightened route control. Barrels moved under guard. Tools moved with stamped permission. Supply became leverage.

The Church gathered language. Language became ritual. Ritual became claim.

The Crown formed offices with new doors and new seals. Lists became mandates. Mandates reached for bodies that could be named and contained.

None of those structures cared about the true mechanic behind copper-gold holds. They cared that holds worked often enough to be believed. Belief produced demand. Demand produced pressure.

I returned attention to the composite brace.

Jhace’s trajectory had already bent. Stage 1 baseline remained visible in tremor and vein-lines. Warning signs had shown under mass load: overheating skin, blood at the nostril, sensory overload pressing close. fileciteturn24file3

Tiffani slowed that bend. She did not stop it.

Unpredictability remained threat and asset.

A registered hand could be scheduled. A leased hand could be deployed. A feared hand could be erased.

An unclaimed hand could not be cleanly used.

I wrote near-term outcomes without specifics, staying inside the gate.

- Increased attempts to capture method through policy, supply, and ritual.
- Increased untrained grasping by civilians, driven by rumor and fear.
- Increased pressure on the composite brace to submit, relocate, or vanish.
- Increased probability of cost escalation events when mass-load crises recur.

I drew one mark beneath the text: three lines meeting at a joint, each line carrying load in a different direction.

The copper-gold planes broke apart into drifting grains and held suspended, waiting for new patterns.

I stepped back into the Underface where heat returned, where vibration returned, where the city’s weight returned.

Saint Merro continued to set under load.
""")

out_path = Path("/mnt/data/N01_Manuscript_Compiled.docx")
doc.save(out_path)
out_path.as_posix()

# N01: GLASS — The Holding City

## CHAPTER 1: Something Wrong  
*Tide 0, Kindling — Underface beneath Saint Merro*

Jhace took the service hatch by touch before he took it by sight.

The iron ring sat cold against his palm. The ladder down breathed heat up at him through the first rungs—warm air from the mains, damp stone behind it, the faint bitter edge of kiln smoke that always hung under Saint Merro. He dropped his weight onto the first rung, tested for flex, then went down in a controlled rhythm.

Boot. Rung. Boot. Rung.

At six spans below the hatch, the sound changed. The city’s surface noise thinned until it became vibration in the rock. Underface had its own voice: the steady thrum of the heat-grid, the low chatter of risers, the occasional ring of a bracket giving a warning note to anyone trained to hear it.

Jhace kept one hand on the ladder and the other along the stone wall. His fingers found the mortar seams. His palm found the temperature. Cool stone, then a warm stripe, then cool again. The warm stripe meant the main ran behind that section, tight to the wall and properly seated.

“Still crawling around down there,” Bram had said at the forge door, without looking up from the quench bucket. “A man could take a Kindling to eat and talk.”

Jhace had taken the ledger anyway. He had taken his tools. He had taken the Underface route because the Underface did not ask questions and did not waste words.

Bram had watched him set the strap across his shoulder and had made a sound that was not approval and not dismissal.

“Work first,” Jhace had said. It was what Bram expected from him. It was what the tunnels expected from him too.

He stepped off the ladder onto the lower platform. The service way ran along the main line, iron grates bolted to stone brackets, a narrow channel of runoff on the far side where condensation dripped from pipe joints and ran toward the sump.

Jhace stood still and let the floor speak.

He counted pulses through the soles of his boots. Not numbers, not formal. The pattern lived in his body. Underface always carried a rhythm—heat flow, water flow, load settling. He knew it the way he knew the pressure of a hammer head in his grip.

The rhythm was off.

Not broken. Not loud. Off.

He crouched and put his palm on the grate. The iron was warmer than it should have been at Kindling. That alone could happen if an upper oven had vented early, if a line had been opened too hard, if a crew had flushed slag into the wrong channel. He could explain it.

The vibration could not be explained that easily.

It pressed up through the grate in a slightly altered cadence. Not stronger. Different. The thrum had a thin edge to it, a faint second note braided under the main tone.

Jhace shifted his palm from iron to stone. The stone carried the same double-note.

He stood and walked.

He did not hurry. Hurrying in Underface got people hurt. It got ankles caught in grates, heads cracked on low beams, shoulders torn on protruding bolts. Hurrying also made you miss the first signs of failure.

He moved toward Junction S-9, where the main line split into two risers for the upper rim platforms. He had marked it last Tide with chalk: a bracket that had started to chatter. He expected to hear that chatter again and decide whether it needed a full pull and reseat.

The bracket was quiet.

Jhace frowned. Quiet could mean resolved. Quiet could mean the bolt had settled into place. Quiet could mean it had cracked and stopped singing because it had nothing left to carry.

He knelt and tapped the iron with the back of his knuckles. The tone came back dull. He tapped the stone anchor point. That tone came back clean.

He pulled the chalk ledger free and flipped to the last entry. The chalk mark was there, his hand’s rough block letters noting: **S-9 bracket chatter. No bow. Heat even. Watch next Tide.**

He set his fingers on the bracket and felt for movement.

There was none.

The bolt head sat flush. The washer was seated. The iron was tight.

It should have reassured him.

It did not.

The wrongness in the thrum continued even with the bracket stable. That meant the source was not local failure. It meant the system itself had shifted.

Jhace stood and followed the main farther in.

The service way narrowed. The ceiling dropped. Stone beams crossed overhead, each one marked with paint and old scratches that recorded who had last braced it. The paint was flaking. The scratches were mostly Torrin hands and Kethis hands and hands from crews that no longer worked this section.

He walked past a drip that struck the grate with a steady tick-tick-tick. The tick did not match the vibration from below.

The difference made his teeth tighten.

He reached a junction where the main line passed through a stone collar and into a reinforced tunnel. The collar was new stone—recent work after the last settlement shift. Jhace had helped set it. He remembered the weight of that stone block and the way his shoulders had burned after hauling it into place.

He put his palm on the collar.

The stone was warm.

Stone should not be warm here unless the main behind it was leaking heat. He pressed his ear to it. He listened for hiss. There was none. He slid his palm along the seam, searching for hot spots. The warmth was even.

Even warmth meant not a leak. It meant the heat was present in the stone itself, distributed through the collar as though the rock had been part of the line.

Jhace drew back, stared at the collar, then stared at his own hand. No soot. No wetness. No sign he had touched a hot metal plate by mistake.

He set his fingers against the seam and counted the vibration again. The double-note persisted. It held at the seam. It continued into the reinforced tunnel.

He straightened and took a slow breath through his nose. Damp air. Iron smell. Old ash. He tasted nothing unusual. That meant he was not breathing new fumes. That was good.

He marked the collar with chalk anyway. A clean line, then a second line, the signal for “return with tools and another set of hands.”

He did not like returning with another set of hands.

Bram always said he worked alone because he did not want to owe anyone explanations. Bram was mostly right.

Jhace continued forward.

The reinforced tunnel had been built to carry load from the upper rim. Thick stone arches, iron ribs bolted into them, pipe hangers spaced at precise reaches. It was one of the few places in Underface that felt overly controlled.

It also had a voice. A steady, heavy resonance that usually made Jhace’s ribs relax because it meant the structure was doing its job.

The resonance now carried the thin second note too.

He stopped beneath the third arch and put his palm against the iron rib. The vibration crawled up into his forearm. He felt it in the bones near his elbow.

His skin prickled.

He did not name that as fear. He named it as signal.

He pulled his hand away and flexed his fingers. The joints moved smoothly. Good. No injury. He could still hold a hammer. He could still tighten a bolt. He could still do work.

He walked to the fourth arch. The fifth.

At the sixth arch, the floor shivered.

It was not the normal settlement tremor—those came as a slow roll, stone dragging against stone, a long groan that traveled through the Underface in a predictable way. This shiver was sharp. It was a quick bite of motion, a brief tilt that made his balance adjust a fraction too late.

His gut rose.

He grabbed the iron rib by reflex, fingers locking hard. The iron was solid. The arch did not move.

The floor beneath his boots still did.

For a breath, gravity felt wrong. Not gone. Shifted.

Jhace tightened his grip until his knuckles ached. He forced his breathing to slow. He waited for the tremor to pass.

It did pass. The floor settled back into its usual angle. The Underface’s thrum returned to its baseline pressure.

Except the thin second note remained.

Jhace released the rib. His fingers left a faint print of sweat on the iron. He wiped his palm on his trousers and looked down the tunnel toward the junction that led up toward the rim.

The wrongness was moving through the system. It was not isolated to one bracket or one pipe.

He had to tell Bram.

He did not want to tell Bram.

He turned toward the ladder that led to the upper service corridor, intending to cut across and climb out near the forge.

The floor shivered again.

This time the shiver ran in the same direction as the thin second note.

Upward.

Jhace stopped and listened, and the Underface answered with a resonance he had never heard in his life.

He started toward the ladder anyway, because whatever was changing under Saint Merro was also changing above it.

And the surface had more people to crush.

---

## CHAPTER 2: Glass That Listens  
*Tide 0, Pouring — Observatory platform at Saint Merro’s rim*

Pella set the scope’s stand feet into the stone sockets and checked the seating twice.

He did not trust the first confirmation. The rim wind did not scream here, but it had weight, and weight shifted instruments by grains that mattered. The platform itself had a mild vibration that came from the city’s ovens and the Underface mains beneath. Most observers ignored it. Pella did not.

He leaned into the tube, aligned the hairlines on the reference mark, and took the baseline.

The mark sat where it should.

He wrote the value anyway.

Marisse stood behind him with her hands clasped tight against her own wrists. She kept her boots spread in a stance that would hold through a tremor. Her eyes were on the sky, not on the log.

“You are early,” Pella said, without turning.

“I do not sleep well at Pouring,” Marisse said.

Pella knew the story. He knew it in the way people knew stories in Saint Merro: without pressing for details, because details carried weight and could crack a person’s careful balance.

Twenty-three dead in a prior catastrophe, long before this Tide. Not a war. Not an outside raid. Just a failure in stone and load and decision-making that ended with bodies in rubble. Marisse had survived. That survival had sharpened her until she could hear a fault line in someone’s footsteps.

Pella adjusted the scope by a small turn and took the second baseline.

The mark still sat clean.

He recorded it. He turned the page and began the sequence.

A standard sweep: rim to Crown line, Crown line to upper arc, upper arc back down. The purpose was routine. Verify the air’s clarity. Verify refraction. Verify that the glass plates in the scope were still true.

The first sweep brought him to a smear.

Not in the scope glass. Not a scratch. A distortion in the sky itself, faint and pale, a thin band that cut across the upper arc.

Pella blinked. He lifted his face from the tube and looked with naked eyes.

The smear was not visible that way. The sky was its usual hard brightness above Saint Merro, with the faint haze that rose from the Glass Quarter kilns and the darker plume of smoke farther off near the water channels.

He put his eye back to the tube and checked the smear again.

It was there.

He loosened the tube’s clamp, nudged the angle, then retightened it. He took the reading again.

The smear remained. It did not shift with the instrument.

“Mark?” Marisse asked.

Pella kept his voice level. “A deviation in refraction.”

“Noise,” Marisse said, but the word came out flat. It did not sound like dismissal. It sounded like a test.

Pella wrote the number. He wrote a second number beside it, the baseline plus the deviation. He wrote a third note: **persistent?**

He ran the sweep again.

The smear appeared in the same position. The deviation held at the same range across the same arc.

Not noise.

Pella’s tongue tasted faintly of metal. He did not react outwardly. He made the small internal adjustment that always came with risk: widen the set of possibilities.

He looked along the platform’s edge at the other equipment: mirror stands, glass plates set in frames, a string of small signal lenses used to direct light down to the lower chambers. The signal lenses had begun to vibrate.

A fine vibration. Not enough to rattle. Enough to make the lens edges whisper against their frames.

Pella walked to the nearest lens and set his fingers against the frame. The vibration traveled into his hand.

He returned to the scope and took another measurement.

The deviation increased by a small step.

He wrote it down.

Marisse moved closer. She kept her stance wide. Her eyes were on the sky again.

“It is growing,” she said.

Pella did not say yes yet. He took the sweep again. The deviation held at the higher value. Repeatable.

He did not call it noise now. He did not even call it anomaly. He called it **new state** and wrote the phrase in the margin.

Marisse’s breath was controlled. Pella could hear it because the platform was quiet. That quiet did not feel stable. It felt thin.

A tremor passed through the rim.

Not a long settlement roll. A short shiver, sharp enough to make the scope’s tube twitch in its clamp.

Pella caught the stand with one hand and steadied the tube with the other. He waited for the shiver to stop, then checked alignment again.

The hairline mark had drifted by a fraction.

He tightened the clamp. He re-aligned. He took the reading again.

The deviation had increased again.

Marisse’s hands flexed at her wrists, fingers spreading and closing once, then locking again.

“I have seen this pattern,” she said. Her voice had a small edge now. “Small shifts first. Then a break.”

Pella did not look away from the scope. “This is not stone.”

“No,” Marisse said. “But it is still load. It is still pressure. It is still people trusting a baseline.”

Pella wrote the number. He wrote the number twice. He underlined it.

He tried a different check: he rotated the scope’s glass plate, changed the internal alignment, then took the sweep again.

If the smear was in the plate, it would rotate. If it was in the sky, it would not.

The smear did not rotate.

His teeth tasted more strongly of metal.

Marisse stepped to the platform edge and looked outward over Saint Merro. The city’s rim structures stood in neat tiers, stone and glass and iron in layered arcs. Underface vents released their steady heat in pale streams. Everything looked normal from this height.

Everything felt wrong through the instruments.

Pella finished the sweep and wrote a final note at the bottom of the page: **cause external.**

He capped his pen and held still for a breath.

He heard a sound then, faint and thin, from the signal lenses.

A hum.

Glass humming against its frame. Not from wind. From vibration in the material itself.

Marisse turned toward the lenses. Her eyes were wide, but she did not speak.

Pella walked to the lens frame and put his palm against the glass.

The hum rose into his skin. It was steady. It was patterned. It carried the same thin second note he had seen in the sky.

He took his hand away and looked up.

The smear had become a line.

A pale line stretched across the upper arc, clean and sharp, a white seam cut into the air itself.

The platform shuddered again, stronger, and Pella’s logbook slid across the stone.

Marisse said one word, low and certain.

“Now.”

---

## CHAPTER 3: The Sky Breaks  
*Tide 0, Striking — Underface → street level → surface*

The wall moved.

Jhace saw it in the corner of his vision and felt it through the floor before he could turn his head. Stone that had held for generations shifted sideways by a fraction of a span. That fraction was enough.

The arch above him groaned. The iron rib protested with a high ring. Dust shed from the mortar seam in a sudden thick fall, striking his shoulders and filling his mouth with grit.

He dropped his weight toward the safer side of the service way, boots scraping on the grate. He reached for the nearest rib again.

The floor tilted harder. Gravity changed angle in a way that made his inner balance seize.

Something deep below gave a sound that belonged to no bracket and no pipe. It was too large. It was a long tearing resonance, stone under stress beyond tolerance.

Jhace lunged for the ladder up.

The ladder’s first rung jerked under his hand as the wall shifted again. He missed the rung and slammed his palm into stone.

Pain flashed through his wrist. He ignored it. He tried again. His fingers caught iron and held.

A second groan ran through the structure. The arch above him shed another curtain of dust. The iron rib flexed. He could feel the flex in his grip.

“Up,” he said to himself. “Up.”

He climbed two rungs before the impact came.

Not a tremor. Not settlement. A hit that knocked his boots off the rungs and threw his body sideways into the wall. His shoulder struck stone hard enough to jar his teeth. He clung to the ladder with one hand and scraped for purchase with the other.

The Underface went loud.

Pipes shrieked in their hangers. The main line thumped against stone collars. The iron rib’s ring became a sustained scream.

Jhace forced himself down instead of up. Up was collapsing. Up was unknown. Down meant space. Down meant room to move.

He dropped from the ladder onto the service grate and ran back toward the wider junction, toward the collar he had marked.

The corridor behind him cracked.

Stone split with a dry sound. A block the size of his torso broke loose from the arch and slammed onto the grate, warping iron bars and sending a shock through his legs.

He jumped the broken section, landed hard, and stumbled. His boot caught a raised bolt head. He went down on one knee, hand slapping the grate.

Heat pulsed up through the iron into his palm.

Not normal heat. Not the steady warmth of a main line. A sudden flare that made him yank his hand back with a hiss.

He stared at his palm.

No burn mark. No blister. Just heat under the skin, deep and bright, as though his nerves had become a conduit.

He did not have time to name it.

A human cry cut through the Underface noise. High. Close.

Jhace pivoted and ran toward it.

A side passage had partially collapsed, stone blocks spilled into it, iron hangers twisted. A girl lay pinned under a fallen beam that had dropped from the ceiling brace. The beam pressed across her lower legs and trapped her against the grate.

Her face was white with dust. Her hair was stuck to her cheeks with blood.

She saw him and tried to pull herself back. The beam did not move.

Jhace dropped beside her.

“Do not move,” he said. The words came out rough. He kept them anyway. “Where are you hurt?”

“My leg,” she said. The sound was thin. Her teeth chattered. “Both.”

He looked. The beam’s edge had cut into her shin. Blood ran in thick lines down her ankle and pooled under the grate. The weight also crushed her other leg at the knee joint. He could see the angle of bone under skin.

She was young. Fifteen castings, maybe less. Too small to be in Underface alone. Too frightened to think.

He checked the beam’s placement. It had fallen from a brace and wedged against the sidewall. That meant it could move if the wall shifted again. That meant it could slide deeper onto her legs and crush her fully.

Jhace grabbed the beam with both hands and tried to lift.

It did not lift.

The beam was not pure stone. It was stone wrapped in iron, a composite brace meant to carry load. It carried load now with cruel efficiency. Jhace’s arms shook with the effort. His shoulder screamed where he had hit the wall earlier.

The girl’s eyes darted toward the corridor behind him. Another groan traveled through the tunnel. Dust fell again.

“We have to go,” she whispered.

He did not argue. He did not soothe. He did not promise.

He set his hands on the beam again, fingers spread, palms flat.

He needed leverage. A pry bar. A wedge. Time.

He had none.

The memory came then, not as a story, not as a vision. A count.

Twenty-three bodies.

Two with his name.

A building collapse years ago, long before this Tide. A stone structure that had failed in a single breath. A father’s hand slipping out of his grip under dust. A cousin’s shoulder turning slack. The sound of people screaming, then the sound stopping.

The count lived in him. It never left.

Jhace’s throat closed. His vision narrowed to the beam, the girl’s legs, the blood.

He could not add one more.

He pressed his palms harder into the beam and drove his intent into the material with everything he had. Not thought. Not prayer. A command built from craft and panic.

Hold.

The heat in his hands flared again, hotter, sharper. Copper-gold light ignited under his palms and surged into the beam’s iron wrap, tracing the stress lines where the weight bit deepest. The glow crawled outward in branching paths, thin and precise, mapping the load distribution the way his mind always did, then enforcing a new distribution the way no hammer ever could.

The beam shifted.

Not up. Sideways. A controlled slide against the wall, one finger-width at a time, as though the material had been persuaded into a safer angle.

The girl screamed. Jhace barked, “Stay still,” and kept his palms pressed.

The copper-gold glow intensified. It spread into the sidewall collar too, outlining cracks that had not been visible under dust. The cracks did not widen. They held.

Jhace’s forearms began to shake. The tremor started at his wrists and climbed. His fingers spasmed once, then locked again.

His palms burned. The burn was not skin-deep. It was inside him, a heat that rode his nerves.

He pushed anyway.

The beam slid another fraction. The pressure on the girl’s legs eased enough for her to drag one ankle free.

“Pull,” Jhace said. His voice came out strained. “Pull now.”

She clawed backward, elbows scraping on iron. The beam tried to settle back under its own weight.

Jhace drove the glow harder into the stress lines. Copper-gold light flared up the beam, then down into the grate beneath. The grate’s bars stiffened. The beam’s iron wrap tightened.

The girl’s second leg came free with a wet sound that made Jhace’s stomach lurch.

She collapsed against the grate, sobbing in short bursts. Blood soaked her trousers. Her eyes rolled back.

Jhace released the beam and grabbed her under the shoulders. He hauled her away from the collapse zone, dragging her across the grate toward the wider junction.

Behind them, the brace groaned and settled. It did not fall fully. Not yet.

Jhace’s hands shook violently now. Vein-lines—darkened paths under the skin—lit faintly along his forearms, following the same branching pattern the glow had traced in the iron. He stared at them for a half breath, then forced himself back to motion.

The tunnel began to fail.

The earlier wall shift repeated, stronger. The sidewall bulged inward. The arch overhead cracked at the key stone. A slab dropped and smashed through the grate, opening a hole into the runoff channel below.

Jhace kept dragging the girl. Her head lolled. Her blood smeared the iron.

He reached the collar he had marked earlier. The stone was hot now. The thin second note in the vibration had become a deep roar.

The world above the Underface screamed in a different register. A long tearing sound ran through stone. The Piercing’s force traveled through Te’Oga as a brutal message.

Jhace had one thought: get out. Get her out. Get Bram out.

He found the ladder to the upper corridor and shoved the girl onto the first rungs. She could not climb. He climbed behind her and pushed her upward with his shoulder and forearm, one rung at a time.

The ladder shook. Dust poured down. His hands slipped once because sweat and blood made iron slick. His tremor made it worse.

They reached the hatch.

Jhace shoved it open with his shoulder and hauled the girl through.

Surface light hit his face. The air was full of dust and smoke. The sound of stone breaking came from every direction. People screamed. A nearby wall had cracked and sagged. Glass panes shattered in a rain that flashed briefly before they fell.

Jhace dragged the girl away from the hatch and into the open street space between two supports, the safest place he could reach in the instant.

He looked up.

A white line split the sky.

It cut across the air in a clean seam, bright enough to hurt his eyes. The sky around it warped, bending the light in a way his brain did not want to accept.

He heard footsteps behind him—fast, controlled. Not panicked.

A person dropped to a crouch beside the girl and did not touch her. She looked first at the girl’s legs, then at the blood, then at Jhace’s hands.

Her gaze fixed on the vein-lines on his forearms.

Copper-gold residue still glimmered faintly at his palms.

“Did you do that,” she said, and the words were not accusation. They were classification.

Jhace tried to answer. His mouth did not form sound.

The stranger’s eyes flicked up to the cracked support wall and then back to his hands.

“Then you can do it again,” she said. “Not everywhere. Here. With intent. When it is real.”

The street shuddered.

Jhace swallowed grit and forced air into his lungs.

He looked at the failing wall.

He looked at the girl bleeding on the stones.

He looked at the stranger who had already turned his collapse into a question he could not ignore.

---

## CHAPTER 4: After the Shudder  
*Tide 0, Quench — Saint Merro lower streets and triage wall*

Jhace woke with his hands already shaking.

He sat up too fast and paid for it immediately. His head spun. His stomach clenched. Dust coated his tongue. He coughed once, then twice, and the cough scraped his throat raw.

He was on a low pallet in a corridor that smelled of wet stone and burned glass. People lay on either side of him—some groaning, some silent, some staring at the ceiling with eyes that did not blink.

A triage corridor. Not the forge. Not the Underface. Not safety.

His forearms were bare. The vein-lines were still there, dark and branching, running from wrist to elbow. They looked like bruises shaped by a careful hand.

Jhace held his arms away from his body and watched the tremor. Fine vibration in his fingers. Worse in his right hand. He tried to close his fist.

The fist closed. It did not close cleanly. The last two fingers lagged.

He forced them down anyway.

Work first.

He swung his legs off the pallet and stood. His knees threatened to fold. He steadied himself against the wall and counted breaths until the dizziness reduced enough to move.

A group of people surged past the corridor opening, carrying a child wrapped in cloth. Someone shouted for water. Someone else shouted for a brace crew. The air had the dense pressure of a city holding itself together by sheer refusal to fall.

Jhace left the pallet behind and walked toward the nearest open space. He found a half-collapsed stairwell leading to an alley. The alley’s stones were cracked, but the walls still stood.

He stepped outside.

The sky seam was still there.

The white line cut across the upper arc, permanent in its wrongness. Dust still drifted through the air in slow sheets. It settled on everything and turned Saint Merro’s stone a dull gray.

Jhace’s gaze dropped to the nearest crack in the alley wall. A simple fracture line running through mortar and stone. Not critical. Not load-bearing. A shallow crack that would be patched by any competent crew in a future Tide.

He crouched and put his palm on it.

He tried to summon the copper-gold glow.

He did not do it with desperation. He did it with intent, with craft knowledge, with the memory of how it had felt in the Underface.

Nothing happened.

No glow. No warmth. No shift in the crack.

Jhace pressed harder, jaw clenched, and tried again.

Still nothing.

Instead, a small pulse of heat flared in his palm and died immediately, leaving behind a sharp ache that ran up his forearm. The tremor spiked once. His fingers jerked.

He pulled his hand away and stared at it.

So it was not a tool he could pick up on command.

The stranger’s words returned: *When it is real.*

Jhace did not curse. He stood and flexed his hand until the tremor eased back to its baseline vibration.

He turned to go back inside and almost collided with someone stepping into the alley.

The stranger from the street. Close now, dust streaking her hair, a logbook held tight under one arm.

Her eyes went straight to the crack he had touched.

“You tested,” she said. Not a question. A conclusion.

Jhace kept his voice short. “It failed.”

“It should,” she said. “No immediate threat. No protective need. No trigger state.”

Jhace stared at her. “You speak as though you have written rules.”

“I speak as though I record what repeats,” she said. “Rules come later.”

She angled the logbook slightly toward him, then pulled it back again before he could read it. The motion was controlled, not teasing. Boundary.

“Where is the girl,” she asked.

“Inside,” Jhace said. “Triage corridor.”

He expected her to push past him. Instead she stayed in the alley and watched him.

“You shook in your sleep,” she said.

Jhace’s jaw tightened. “I do not—”

“You do,” she said. “Your hands. Your forearms. Your grip.”

Jhace looked down at his hands as if that would make them behave. The tremor continued. It did not care about pride.

The stranger wrote a short note in her logbook without looking away from him.

“What is your name,” she said.

“Jhace Torrin.”

A flicker crossed her face at the name. Not surprise. Recognition of a type: Underface worker, forge-hand, someone used to weight.

“And you,” he said, because the exchange required balance.

“Tiffani,” she said. “Observatory.”

That explained the way she watched. The way she turned disaster into measured categories.

A shout rose from the corridor behind them. A different shout this time—urgent, panicked.

“Wall!” someone cried. “The triage wall is going!”

Jhace’s body moved before his mind finished deciding. He ran back into the corridor with Tiffani at his side.

They reached the main triage space and saw the problem immediately.

A long retaining wall held back a slope of broken stone and debris from a collapsed upper tier. The wall had been cracked during Striking. It had been braced with hastily set timbers and iron straps. It was now bowing outward, slowly, under the weight of the rubble behind it.

People were clustered too close. Healers—hands only, no magic, just bandages and splints—worked on the injured along the wall’s base because it was the only open stretch of stone that had not flooded with runoff. If the wall failed, the debris would bury them all.

Jhace pushed through the crowd, shoulders braced, ignoring protests.

He put his hand on the wall.

The stone vibrated. The crack in the center seam widened by a grain with every pulse of load shifting behind it.

This was real.

This was immediate.

This was people about to be crushed.

Jhace’s palms heated at once. The copper-gold glow flared under his skin and spilled into the wall’s stress lines. It traced the crack, then moved outward in branching paths, seeking the load-bearing points and reinforcing them with a force that made his teeth grit.

The wall’s bow slowed.

Not stopped. Slowed.

Jhace pressed harder, fingers splayed, both palms flat on the stone. The glow brightened. The vein-lines in his forearms darkened and extended. The tremor climbed into his elbows.

A sharp pain stabbed behind his eyes. He swallowed it and drove his intent deeper.

Hold.

He felt the structure respond. The crack stopped widening. The wall’s outward creep arrested by a fraction, then another.

But the wall was too wide for one pair of hands.

“Tiffani,” Jhace forced out. His voice was strained now. “Where.”

She did not hesitate. She ran her hand along the wall’s surface, eyes tracking the crack paths and the brace points. She did not touch his hands. She did not touch the glow. She stayed just outside it and read the geometry.

“Left brace is wrong,” she said. “The iron strap is seated off-center. Your glow is feeding a weak joint.”

Jhace’s teeth clenched harder. “Fix it.”

“I cannot,” she said. “Your hands are occupied and the wall is moving.”

She grabbed a nearby timber wedge from a brace pile, shoved it under the off-center strap’s anchor point, and kicked it into place with her boot heel. The strap shifted a fraction and seated deeper into the stone groove.

“Now,” she said. “Feed the strap line. Not the crack. The strap.”

Jhace forced his focus to follow her instruction. He did not understand the mechanism. He understood load. He understood where force had to go.

He moved the copper-gold flow, guided by her words, into the iron strap and its anchor bolts.

The strap glowed. The bolts glowed. The glow ran down into the stone around them, thickening the load path.

The wall’s bow reduced by a grain.

The crowd noise changed. People noticed. They fell silent in the way people did when they saw a structure decide not to kill them yet.

Jhace’s hands shook violently. Sweat ran down his wrists. The heat in his palms became pain. His vision narrowed. He held anyway.

The wall steadied. It held.

Jhace released the stone and staggered back a step. His fingers refused to unclench at first. He forced them open and saw that his hands were trembling so badly he could not hold them steady.

A brace worker shoved a nail into his palm. “Pin that timber,” the worker said. “Quick, before it shifts.”

Jhace reached for the hammer.

His hand closed around the hammer handle and slipped.

He tried again. His fingers did not obey. The tremor made the handle jump against his palm. The hammer fell and clanged on stone.

The brace worker stared at him, then at the vein-lines on his forearms.

Jhace swallowed. The bitter taste of metal flooded his mouth.

Tiffani stepped between him and the brace worker without raising her voice.

“He cannot,” she said. “Not after that. You need another set of hands.”

The brace worker hesitated, then ran off shouting for help.

Jhace stood there, breathing hard, staring at his own useless grip.

The wall behind him held steady, the crack arrested, the bracing now seated properly.

His hands shook.

Tiffani looked at his forearms again, then at the wall, then at her logbook.

She wrote one line.

Jhace watched her pen move and understood the shape of the problem with a clarity that hurt more than the tremor:

He could hold stone together.

He could not hold himself steady enough to pick up a hammer afterward.

The wall held.

His grip did not.

---

## CHAPTER 5: Lines That Don’t Lie

Tiffani’s logbook gained weight in her hand without gaining pages.

She kept it dry anyway. Habit. Oilcloth wrap, tie twice, tuck into the inner pocket of her field satchel. Even with Soulpulse dust in the air, even with mortar grit everywhere, even with the city’s breath turning coarse after the Piercing, paper still mattered. Paper held numbers. Numbers held memory without needing permission.

The Kiln Corridor ran crooked through the Glass Quarter, ovens on one side, cracked stone on the other. Firehouse vents still exhaled when they could. Some were dead. Some were choked. The ones that worked made the corridor warmer than the lanes that fed into it, and warmth made people gather. Warmth made it harder to keep clear passages. Warmth made a load path more complicated.

Tiffani stopped at the first hold site of Tide 2, Kindling. A small archway that had already been shored twice. The crew had used timber braces and iron straps. The straps were too thin and too long; the timber was warped. The arch carried weight from a broken upper walk and redirected it into a corner abutment that had lost its footing when the ground rotated during the Piercing.

She put her palm on the stone and did not pretend it was calm.

Her fingers felt micro-vibration, a fine pulse that traveled through her wrist and up into her forearm. She steadied herself with the other hand on the strap and counted the cadence. Not a sound. A repeatable rhythm. Repeatable meant real.

She opened the logbook to the current section and wrote:

**Hold Site 1 — Kiln Corridor / West bend / Oven 14 vent line**  
**Structure:** arch + corner abutment  
**Material:** stone + mortar, iron strap add-on  
**State:** creep present, low amplitude, constant  
**Human traffic:** high, uncontrolled  
**Response:** additional brace recommended; traffic control required  
**Note:** baseline vibration increased by one subdivision since Tide 1, Quench

She underlined **traffic control** twice. Not because it would happen, but because she wanted it documented that it should have happened.

She moved down the corridor in measured Strides, stopping at each shift in the paving where the stone had split. She marked deviations on her corridor map: hairline fissures, buckled joints, slumped grout. She checked the same points she had checked during the first Tides after the Piercing, and she compared. Some cracks were stable. Some had widened by less than a finger’s breadth. Two had opened enough for grit to pour through when she tapped them with her knuckle.

When she reached the second hold site, she did not have to ask whose work it was.

The copper-gold stain was faint but visible in the stone. Not a dye. Not paint. A residue in the surface where heat and light had passed through fractured material and left the cracks cleaner than the surrounding dust. Tiffani knelt, wiped away grit with the side of her hand, and traced the path with her fingertip.

Stress lines. Real ones. The route was not random. The glow had flowed along the fracture network that carried load.

She wrote again.

**Hold Site 2 — Kiln Corridor / Junction to Underface stair**  
**Structure:** stair throat + supporting lintel  
**State pre-hold:** recorded sag by two subdivisions, Tide 1 late Quench  
**State post-hold:** stable within half subdivision, Tide 2 Kindling  
**Indicator:** copper-gold residue along primary fractures  
**Hypothesis:** activation follows immediate need, follows load path, prefers existing fracture routes

She paused with the pen hovering.

Hypothesis needed testing. Hypothesis needed failures documented as carefully as successes.

That was the point of the logbook. Not praise. Not story. Pattern.

A shout from deeper in the corridor pulled her head up. A crew dragged a brace into position. Someone argued with a woman carrying a bundled child. The child did not cry. The woman’s face was too empty for crying.

Tiffani closed the logbook and moved.

The next hold site was not in the corridor itself but in a side lane where a glass furnace had collapsed. The oven’s dome had cracked and dropped inward, spilling ash and broken brick. The roof beams above had bowed. The beam ends had torn into their sockets. A crew had propped them with scavenged columns and wedges. The wedges were too dry. Dry wedges slipped.

She examined the beam ends and the sockets. The stone around the sockets had sheared, and the shear line had the same faint copper-gold residue as the lintel site.

Two points of residue now. Two points meant the thing existed beyond a single event. Two points did not mean enough.

Tiffani’s hand shook once while she drew the shear line in the logbook. She braced her wrist on the page, stabilized, and continued.

She did not call it noise now.

—

By the time Tide 2 reached Pouring, she had six hold sites mapped and three confirmed sites with copper-gold residue. She had two recorded failures where the residue was absent and the structure continued to creep despite shoring.

Failures mattered. Failures defined the boundary.

She chose an observation position above the Saint Merro junction approaches, where the main walk sloped down toward an intersection of arches, columns, and ramps that carried people between the Crown’s inner districts and the Glass Quarter camps. The junction had become a throat. Everything moved through it.

Tiffani set up her glass-sight on a stable parapet. The lens had survived because she had wrapped it in wool and stored it in a padded case during the Piercing. The lens was not perfect anymore; a hairline crack ran along one edge, but the crack did not distort within the central field. Good enough for measuring.

She aligned the sight with a known stone mark on the far arch and checked baseline. The arch carried slightly low. The deviation was within one subdivision, but the direction was consistent with the creep she had logged at other sites.

She wrote: **Junction arch A — low by 0.8 subdivision; trend stable**.

Then she waited.

Waiting was not passive. Waiting was data collection with legs still.

When the shoring crew set a brace and the arch answered with a small shift, she recorded the shift. When a line of refugees pushed through and the load increased, she recorded the increased vibration. When an aftershock ran under the stone and the arch responded with a groan that made the lens tremble, she recorded that too.

The man she was tracking arrived from the Underface stair at the junction’s left side.

Jhace Torrin moved with the gait of someone who had spent most of his life under stone. He watched the joints first, then the crowd, then the shoring. He spoke to the crew chief with minimal words. He carried a hammer but did not swing it. His right hand held the handle too tightly, and his fingers did not settle into a stable grip.

His tremor showed even at distance. Small. Persistent. Not a shaking fit. A baseline instability that affected fine motor control. Tiffani had seen it in Tide 1. It was worse now.

He set his palm against the arch base. He did not close his hand around anything. Skin to stone. Contact. Intent. Need.

The copper-gold light did not flare theatrically. It moved.

Tiffani watched it through the glass-sight as it ran along existing fractures and into a joint that had begun to separate. The arch shifted upward by half a subdivision. Then it held.

Jhace pulled his hand away quickly, as if the stone had burned. His shoulders tightened. He hid the tremor by clenching his hands into fists and putting them against his thighs. The veins in his forearms stood out darker than they should, with faint lines that followed no normal pattern. Copper-gold lines under skin.

Cost. Visible. Physical.

Tiffani recorded:

**Hold Event — Junction arch A**  
**Trigger context:** crowd load + aftershock + audible joint movement  
**Method:** palm contact at arch base; no tool grip during activation  
**Effect:** immediate stabilization + lift of 0.5 subdivision  
**Residue:** visible line along fracture network; copper-gold  
**User condition:** tremor increased post-use; vein-lines visible; grip avoidance observed

She did not write his name in the logbook. Not yet. Names invited narrative. Narrative invited distortion. She wrote **User A** and left it that way.

Then the Resonance Regulation Office arrived.

They came in pairs, wearing clean coats that did not match the dust in the air. The coats were heavy cloth, the cut formal, the collars stiff. Their boots were polished. Their faces carried the calm of people who believed paper could still control stone.

Tiffani recognized the insignia stitched into the collar seam. A simple mark, newly standardized: intersecting arcs around a central dot. Resonance. Regulation. The office that had been a committee during the first Tide after the Piercing was learning to walk.

They did not approach the shoring crew first. They climbed to the parapet where Tiffani stood.

One of them was older, with a ledger tucked under his arm. The other was younger and watched the crowd more than he watched Tiffani.

“Observer,” the older one said. He did not ask if she was. He told her what he saw. “We were told you’ve been tracking the new phenomenon.”

Tiffani did not answer immediately. She closed the logbook, secured the tie, and set the glass-sight cap in place. She did not move away from the parapet.

“You were told correctly,” she said.

He smiled as if the correctness belonged to him. “The Resonance Regulation Office is consolidating reports. We can provide paper, ink, guards for your work sites. We can provide access to restricted zones. In return, we require your records. All of them.”

The younger one shifted his stance. He watched Jhace below with interest that did not register as concern.

Tiffani kept her voice even. “My records are not for trade.”

“They are not trade,” the older man said. “They are public safety. This phenomenon is dangerous. It must be tracked by an authorized body.”

“It is being tracked,” Tiffani said. “By me. With methods that do not depend on your authorization.”

The older man’s smile thinned. “You misunderstand. We are not requesting. We are instructing. We have authority from the Crown’s administrative chambers to collect all resonance-related observations.”

“Authority does not stabilize arches,” Tiffani said. “Braces do. Correct marking does. Competent hands do. Your papers do not hold weight.”

The younger one’s eyes narrowed. “You sound uncooperative.”

“I sound accurate,” Tiffani said.

The older man leaned forward slightly. “You will be required to register your involvement and the names of any resonance users you observe.”

Tiffani looked down at the junction. Jhace had stepped away from the arch and was speaking to a crew member. His hands were still clenched. He was trying to keep them steady by force.

“No,” she said.

The older man blinked once. “No?”

“No,” Tiffani repeated. “I will not hand over records. I will not provide names. I will not allow you to turn a survival mechanism into a ledger of control.”

The older man’s face hardened. “That mechanism is unstable. It must be regulated.”

“Then regulate your own response,” Tiffani said. “Assign traffic control. Provide sound timber. Stop moving people through failing arches without supervision.”

The younger one’s jaw tightened. “You will regret this. The Office will remember who obstructs.”

Tiffani’s pulse picked up. Not fear. Anticipation.

“I will remember,” she said, “who tried to take data by threat.”

They left without taking the logbook, but they left with a look that promised return.

Tiffani did not feel triumphant. She felt the burden of attention shift toward her. Attention changed outcomes. Outcomes changed costs.

She wrote a brief note after they were gone:

**RRO approach — Tide 2 Pouring**  
**Offer:** resources with control strings; demand names + full records  
**Response:** refused  
**Risk:** escalation likely; concealment of User A identity required

—

The second pressure arrived before Tide 3 reached Kindling.

It came with water.

Not rain. Water in barrels, hauled by teams who still had access to functioning mains and reservoirs. Water meant power in the Glass Quarter. Water meant control over kiln operations, over refugee cooking fires, over mortar mixing, over basic survival.

A Water-Guild factor walked with the barrels. He wore a coat that had been cleaned recently. His hands were not callused in a way that suggested shoring work. He carried a small seal stamp on a chain at his belt.

Tellis.

Tiffani recognized him from council meetings before the Piercing, when the Glass Quarter argued for repair budgets and the Water-Guild argued for tariff adjustments. He had always spoken politely. Politeness did not make an offer benign.

He climbed to the parapet without asking permission and glanced at the glass-sight as if it were an amusing tool.

“Observer,” he said. “You’re out here watching the arches settle.”

“I’m measuring,” Tiffani said.

Tellis smiled. “Measuring, watching. Both require distance. I respect it.”

He nodded toward the junction. “The Crown will decide what gets rebuilt. The Guilds will decide what gets supplied. The Glass Quarter is not winning that argument.”

“Noted,” Tiffani said.

Tellis leaned his elbows on the parapet, careful not to dirty his sleeves. “You have influence right now. Not formal. Practical. People listen when you point at a crack and say it will fail. People move when you tell them to move.”

Tiffani waited.

“I can make that influence profitable,” Tellis said. “You could advise the Water-Guild’s retreat plan. There are districts that should be abandoned. There are routes that should be redirected. There are assets that should be salvaged before the rubble gets claimed by other hands.”

“Your retreat plan,” Tiffani said, “requires calling occupied structures unsalvageable.”

“It requires realism,” Tellis said. “The Piercing changed the city. The Glass Quarter was fragile before. Now it will bleed resources. Better to reallocate. Better to move the ovens that can move and let the rest fall.”

“People live in those ovens now,” Tiffani said.

Tellis’s smile did not change. “People live in many unsuitable places. The Guild does not control that.”

He shifted closer. His voice lowered slightly. “I’m not asking you to lie. I’m asking you to choose which truths matter. If you declare certain arches beyond repair, traffic routes shift. Supplies shift. That shift can be directed. We can protect the routes that serve production.”

Tiffani looked at him directly. “You want my measurements as a tool for profit.”

Tellis lifted his hands in a gesture that implied innocence. “Profit funds rebuilding.”

“Profit funds consolidation,” Tiffani said. “Rebuilding requires allocation by need.”

Tellis’s eyes sharpened. “Need does not keep guild halls standing. The Crown will not feed everyone. We will.”

“You will feed those who serve you,” Tiffani said.

Tellis’s smile thinned. “You’re making an enemy out of someone offering stability.”

“I am refusing,” Tiffani said. “Stability cannot be bought by abandoning the unstable.”

Tellis glanced down at the junction again, at Jhace moving through the crowd with tremoring hands. “That man is a liability. He will get someone killed. Better to let the Office take him.”

Tiffani’s throat tightened. Not fear. Anger classified as tension.

“I will not trade him to you,” she said, surprised by the clarity of the sentence even as she spoke it.

Tellis’s gaze flicked back to her. “Trade him?”

“Trade the data,” Tiffani corrected. “Trade the name. Trade the work. No.”

Tellis’s face cooled. “You will regret being alone.”

Tiffani watched him leave. The barrels went with him, down the parapet steps, toward the crowd that would follow water the way fire follows air.

Pressure from the Office. Pressure from the Guilds. Different angles. Same intent.

Control.

She wrote:

**Guild approach — Tide 3 Kindling**  
**Offer:** profitable retreat advisory; redirect supply routes via structural condemnation  
**Response:** refused  
**Risk:** Water-Guild obstruction likely; political pressure rising

Her hand shook again, stronger than before. Not because of the Guild. Because she had spoken his relevance aloud.

Him.

User A.

Jhace Torrin.

She should not know his name, but she did. Names traveled in crisis even when she tried to keep them out of paper. A crew member had shouted it once. Someone had cursed it. Someone had said it in gratitude, softly, after a wall held.

Names were unavoidable. The question was what they did to the work.

—

Marisse found her at the third hold site of Tide 3, Pouring.

Marisse did not move with the careless speed of people who believed the world would be stable again. She walked as if each Stride needed confirmation. She scanned cracks in stone automatically, her gaze never resting.

Her face was thinner than before the Piercing. The vigil had eaten her.

“You’ve been recording,” Marisse said. Not a question.

“Yes,” Tiffani said.

Marisse crouched beside the corner abutment and touched the stone lightly with two fingers. “This joint is worse than your last mark.”

Tiffani checked the mark. Marisse was correct. The separation had widened by a fraction. Enough to matter.

“I missed that change,” Tiffani said.

Marisse’s jaw tightened. “That is how it starts.”

Tiffani did not ask what Marisse meant. She already knew.

The pre-Piercing catastrophe had been in a different district. A kiln hall collapse blamed on material fatigue. Twenty-three people had died under stone and brick. Marisse had been pulled out alive. She had named the sound before the collapse. She had said it was not noise. The investigation had said she was anxious.
