Allocation wearing a polite coat.

Tiffani felt the pattern settle: factions aligning, each trying to lay claim to the new method that had emerged from crisis.

And beneath those visible claims, other rumors traveled—coastal talk of “cleaning,” of scalding pans and salt rites, of people purging “impurity” from those who tried to grasp at copper-gold without knowing its cost. The rumor had reached Saint Merro in fragments, carried by refugees with brine still crusted at their cuffs.

The world was responding to the seam in different ways.

Some built ledgers.

Some built markets.

Some built rites.

None of them asked whether the people in the middle wanted to be built into anything.

Tiffani glanced at Jhace. His hands were his, for now.

“We don’t have control,” she said quietly.

Jhace’s eyes stayed forward. “We never did.”

Tiffani nodded. “But we can choose what we hold.”

Jhace didn’t answer immediately. Then: “And what we don’t.”

“Yes,” Tiffani said. The second half mattered more.

They turned off the main lane and climbed toward a small overlook above the forge district—a rebuilt lip where someone had cleared rubble enough to create breathing space. From here you could see the patched tiers of Saint Merro, the smoke threads from functioning kilns, the crowd movement like slow water through broken channels.

You could also see the sky seam without trying.

It sat above everything, straight and pale, indifferent to effort.

Jhace stopped at the overlook’s edge and rested his bandaged hands on the stone cap. The tremor ran into the stone and back into him, as if the structure was answering.

“You feel it,” Tiffani said.

Jhace’s mouth went flat. “It doesn’t let me forget.”

They stood there until their breathing matched pace.

Then they went back down to the forge.

### Jhace

The forge wall had always been a wall—soot-black, scarred by heat, marked by old chalk notes that came and went with jobs.

Now it became a surface for something that needed to last.

Bram stood near the worktable, arms crossed, watching without speaking. Pella was there too, looking out of place with ink stains on his fingers in a forge, but he held himself steady like someone who understood structures could be instruments. Two brace workers lingered near the door, uncertain whether they were invited. The girl Jhace had dragged from Underface during the Piercing sat on a low stool with her legs braced, face pale but eyes clear. She watched Jhace’s hands like they were a weather sign.

Community gathered without ceremony.

That made it real.

Tiffani took a chunk of pale chalk and handed it to Jhace.

His fingers closed around it and shook. The chalk trembled visibly. Dust fell from its edge.

He hated that everyone could see.

Tiffani didn’t look away. She placed her hand under his, not grabbing, not guiding—just a brace beneath the tremor.

“Together,” she said, low enough that only he heard.

Jhace swallowed. Then he lifted the chalk to the wall.

“Three,” Bram said, finally speaking. “Not two. Not ten. Three holds.”

Jhace glanced at him. Bram’s eyes were hard, but not cruel.

Three was forge logic. Three points made a stable stand.

Tiffani nodded once. “Three priorities,” she said. “Simple enough to survive panic.”

Jhace’s hand shook against the wall. He forced the first line down with slow pressure, letting the tremor chatter the chalk in tiny skips. The words came out imperfect, but legible.

**HOLD WHAT IS REAL.**

He stared at it. The sentence looked too small for the city.

Then he remembered the truth: small holds kept roofs up.

He wrote the second line, slower. His forearm ached from the effort of controlling the tremor more than from the chalk itself.

**NAME THE COST.**

Tiffani’s fingers tightened under his for a breath, approval without praise. Pella’s mouth went tight as if he understood what that meant for ledgers and lies.

Jhace wrote the third line. The tremor made the letters rough, but the meaning was clear.

**CHOOSE TOGETHER.**

When he finished, his hand dropped. The chalk snapped in his grip, crumbling. Fine dust fell onto his bandage.

Jhace looked at his palm, then up at the wall. Three lines, stark on soot-black stone.

He felt something inside him settle—not peace, not certainty, but alignment. A brace seated correctly.

Tiffani stepped forward and, with her own chalk, drew a simple mark beneath the lines: two hands, linked at the wrists, one trembling, one steady. She didn’t embellish it. She didn’t make it pretty.

She made it true.

The brace workers at the door shifted, then one of them stepped in and touched the wall lightly, as if the chalk could transfer resolve through skin. The other followed, setting a palm beside the first.

The girl on the stool reached out and placed her fingertips against the lowest line—**CHOOSE TOGETHER**—then pulled her hand back like she’d touched something hot.

Bram grunted. “Good,” he said, and the word carried weight because Bram gave nothing freely.

Jhace’s nose tingled. He wiped it with the back of his wrist, then checked for red. Nothing this time.

He turned slightly and looked at Tiffani. The tremor in his hand reached for her without him deciding it. Habit. Need. The new truth of interdependence.

Tiffani took his hand.

Her grip was firm enough to be a brace, gentle enough to not pretend the tremor wasn’t there. Jhace felt the shake travel into her fingers and stop—held, not fixed.

Outside, the city still groaned. The Office still posted its notices. The Guild still carried water where it chose. Rumors still traveled along the coast like brine through cracked trays.

The seam in the sky still cut the day’s light.

But inside the forge, three lines on a wall made a structure they could stand under.

Jhace squeezed Tiffani’s hand, and she squeezed back.

Together, facing whatever came next.

---

## EPILOGUE: The Auditor — The Not-Room

The Auditor entered the space that refused the name *room*.

Stone surrounded it. The Underface carried weight above it. Heat mains ran near enough that a normal chamber would have held warmth in its joints. This space held none of that. Sound arrived dulled. Vibration arrived cut. The floor took the Auditor’s boot and gave nothing back.

A slate appeared in the Auditor’s hands and was set down where a table should have been. No surface declared itself. The slate remained set.

Three slates followed, placed in a line, edges aligned by fingertip distance. The alignment held. The place permitted record, not structure.

Copper-gold dust hung at the center. It did not fall. It did not rise. It gathered into thin planes and cords that mapped stress without material. The cords tightened, then separated into three impressions.

The first impression formed around ink, seals, and squared stone.

Corlexi moved through Crown chambers where tables sat in grids and stools sat wrong. The impression sharpened on the moments he corrected: a wobbling leg wedged, a ledger squared to an inlay, a doorframe touched. The touch shifted a crack’s edge tighter by a fraction and left no visible cost on skin. No tremor followed. No heat climbed. The act still changed the load behavior.

The second impression formed around salt, scalding steam, and hands that did not flinch.

Mobel stood at a coastal pan where stone trays had opened seams. He seated wedges with controlled taps. He poured boiling brine over a convulsing man’s wrists and scrubbed until copper-gold grains washed away. Pain registered in the body and did not interrupt the sequence. The crowd’s fear aligned behind him, reshaping into appetite for rule.

The third impression did not begin with place. It began with linkage.

Jhace Torrin’s hands trembled at rest. The tremor ran through fingers and into his wrists, a constant vibration that did not fully drain. Faint copper-gold vein-lines remained visible under his skin even when he did nothing. His body carried a permanent baseline of cost. The invoice had been paid in advance for work not yet demanded.

Beside him, Tiffani’s presence acted as constraint. She reduced waste. She narrowed his effort to true load paths. She prevented the caretaker compulsion from spending him past collapse. She did not do it gently. She did it correctly.

The impressions held long enough for assessment, then thinned and re-formed, correcting their own edges.

The Auditor’s hand hovered above the first slate.

Then the voice changed, not as announcement, but as the natural point where observation became record.

I wrote the first line clean and short: **Three vectors confirmed.**

I kept the next lines anchored to what the world had shown, not what the world might mean.

**Vector One — Seating.** Corlexi’s mind sought hierarchy in joints and grids. He treated deviation as threat. He treated compliance as stability. He refused overt command once, and the refusal did not end the system’s hunger. It redirected it toward finding a more compliant hand.

**Vector Two — Cleansing.** Mobel’s mind sought correction through removal. He treated contamination as moral fact. He used pain as process control. A crowd accepted the sequence and began to mirror it. When fear becomes instruction, instruction becomes authority.

**Vector Three — Bracing.** Jhace and Tiffani formed a composite brace. Load redistributed between them. Their method reduced waste under activation and delayed failure. Their bond remained functional, not ornamental. Their refusal of ownership increased external pressure.

I set the chalk down and listened through the not-room’s damped boundary.

Above, Saint Merro held by timber and iron and exhausted hands. Aftershocks continued. Cracks that had been patched opened again along old planes. The city responded in the only ways it knew how.

The Guild tightened route control. Barrels moved under guard. Tools moved with stamped permission. Supply became leverage.

The Church gathered language. Language became ritual. Ritual became claim.

The Crown formed new offices with new doors and new seals. Lists became mandates. Mandates reached for bodies that could be named, tracked, contained.

None of those structures cared about the true mechanic behind copper-gold holds. They cared that the holds worked often enough to be believed. Belief produced demand. Demand produced pressure.

I returned my attention to the third impression.

Jhace’s trajectory had already bent. Stage 1 baseline remained visible in tremor and vein-lines. The next threshold had announced itself in warning signs during mass-load events: overheating skin, blood at the nostril, sensory overload edging in when scale exceeded method.

Tiffani would slow that bend. She would not stop it. The cost model did not allow clean victory. It allowed only management, triage, and refusal to waste.

Unpredictability remained the threat and the asset.

A registered hand could be scheduled. A leased hand could be deployed. A feared hand could be erased.

An unclaimed hand could not be cleanly used.

That unclaimed state would provoke institutions that believed stability required control. It would also draw desperate people who believed survival required miracle.

I wrote the last section as prediction without specifics, staying inside the gate.

**Near-term outcomes (Tide range uncertain):**
- Increased attempts to capture method through policy, supply, and theology.
- Increased untrained grasping by civilians, driven by rumor and fear.
- Increased pressure on the composite brace to submit, relocate, or vanish.
- Increased probability of cost escalation events when mass-load crises recur.

I stopped there.

The not-room allowed no comfort in excess. It held only what was necessary.

Before I left, I drew one mark beneath the text: three lines meeting at a joint, each line carrying load in a different direction. A joint could hold under balanced force. A joint could also become the first failure point when forces arrived out of phase.

The copper-gold dust planes broke apart into drifting grains. The grains remained suspended, waiting for new patterns to map.

I stepped back into the Underface where heat returned, where vibration returned, where the city’s weight returned all at once.

Saint Merro continued to set under load.

---

[ASSISTANT]
from docx import Document
from docx.shared import Pt
from docx.enum.text import WD_ALIGN_PARAGRAPH
from docx.oxml.ns import qn
from pathlib import Path

doc = Document()
style = doc.styles['Normal']
style.font.name = 'Times New Roman'
style._element.rPr.rFonts.set(qn('w:eastAsia'), 'Times New Roman')
style.font.size = Pt(11)

def add_title(text):
    p = doc.add_paragraph()
    run = p.add_run(text)
    run.bold = True
    run.font.size = Pt(18)
    p.alignment = WD_ALIGN_PARAGRAPH.CENTER

def add_subtitle(text):
    p = doc.add_paragraph()
    run = p.add_run(text)
    run.italic = True
    run.font.size = Pt(12)
    p.alignment = WD_ALIGN_PARAGRAPH.CENTER

def add_heading(text, level=1):
    doc.add_heading(text, level=level)

def add_block(text):
    for para in text.strip().split("\n\n"):
        doc.add_paragraph(para.strip())

add_title("N01 — Glass Under Load")
add_subtitle("Confluence Chronicles — Novella 01 Manuscript")
doc.add_page_break()

from docx import Document
from docx.shared import Pt
from docx.enum.text import WD_ALIGN_PARAGRAPH
from docx.oxml.ns import qn
from pathlib import Path

segments = [
("CHAPTER 1: Something Wrong", """Jhace Torrin started each Tide the same way: hand to pipe, boot to stone, ear to the heat-grid’s thrum.

Underface air carried heat even when the upper tiers went cool. It carried soot, damp, and the sharp tang of old metal. The main line beside the inspection walk ran steady, warm enough to fog his breath at the edge of the vent slits. Jhace pressed his palm to the iron and counted the vibration through his wrist.

Stable. No chatter. No stutter.

He moved along the line in measured Strides. The walk was narrow and slick with condensation. Each step landed on stone set in a staggered pattern to spread load across the tunnel floor. Bram Kethis had taught him to trust staggered patterns and distrust long straight seams. Straight seams invited clean breaks.

The first junction box sat where the line split toward the kiln galleries. Jhace crouched and ran his fingers along the box’s edge. The seam at the hinge carried a fine grit. New grit.

He scraped a grain onto his thumb and rubbed it. The grain was not ash. It was sharp, glassy.

He looked up at the overhead brace. The brace plate had shifted half a nail’s breadth. Not enough to alarm the crews upstairs. Enough to change load on the tunnel arch.

He stood and listened again. The tunnel held a hum, low and constant, but a second tone rode it now. Finer. Higher. A thin vibration that did not match the heat-grid’s usual pattern.

He pressed two fingers to the arch stone. The tone traveled through the stone and into his bones.

Wrong.

He moved faster, not running. Running added load. Running made boots slip. He took longer Strides, letting the tunnel guide his feet. The second junction box was warmer than it should have been, and the warmth arrived unevenly. Heat shifted in pulses, not flow.

Jhace knelt and checked the bracket clamps. The top clamp was tight. The bottom clamp had loosened a fraction. The bolt still held, but the iron strap under it carried a slight bend that had not been there two Tides earlier.

Bram’s voice lived in his head: Check the bend before you tighten the bolt. Tightening a bent strap snaps it. Snapped straps kill.

Jhace didn’t tighten. He marked the strap with chalk, a short line to show drift, and moved on.

The deeper he went, the more the second tone thickened. It was not loud. It was persistent. It had the same feel as a wire under tension, vibrating from a touch you couldn’t see. Jhace stopped at a vent slit and held his hand near the opening.

Cold air passed over his knuckles. Not normal Underface cold. This cold carried a dry bite. It carried no damp. It carried the edge of high stone and open distance.

He drew his hand back.

A tremor ran through the floor. Small. The tunnel responded with a soft grinding sound where stone joints shifted against each other. Jhace widened his stance and let the vibration pass through his legs. Aftershocks had been common since the Piercing rumors started in the upper tiers. Small shudders, then calm.

This tremor had a different cadence. It was not a wave. It was a tilt.

Jhace felt his balance shift left, then right, then settle somewhere that was not center. The tunnel did not collapse. The pipes did not burst. But the load in the arch changed. The stone felt heavier, then lighter, then heavy again.

He pressed his palm to the wall and waited until his breath steadied.

Bram would want to know. Bram would want the grid shut down before it tore itself apart. Bram would also tell him not to dramatize.

Jhace wiped sweat from his forehead with his sleeve, leaving a streak of soot on his skin. He turned back toward the forge bay, where Bram kept the main tools and the salvage clamps.

The second tone followed him, present even when he moved away from the junction boxes. It sat in the tunnel air. It sat behind his teeth.

Jhace climbed the short ladder into the forge bay and pushed the iron grate open.

Heat hit his face. Forge heat, steady and controlled. The smell of coal and oil and iron filings took over from Underface damp.

Bram stood at the anvil block, hammer in hand, shaping a bracket with the calm patience of someone who had learned not to waste strikes. He glanced up once, eyes narrowing at Jhace’s pace.

“You move like something slipped,” Bram said.

“Something shifted,” Jhace said. “Heat-grid tone is wrong. Junction box one has new grit. Brace plate drifted.”

Bram set the hammer down. “Show.”

Jhace led him to the access grate and down the ladder. Bram followed without hurry, but his hands flexed once as if preparing for work.

At the first junction box, Jhace pointed to the grit and the drift mark. Bram crouched, touched the seam, and held still.

Bram’s mouth tightened. “That’s not ash.”

“No,” Jhace said.

Bram stood and pressed his palm to the arch stone where Jhace had felt the second tone.

“It’s there,” Bram said.

Bram moved to the vent slit and held his hand near the airflow. He drew it back fast and wiped his palm on his apron.

“Cold bite,” Bram said.

Jhace nodded. “Dry.”

Bram stared down the tunnel. “Shut down the side line,” Bram said. “Not the main. Side. Slow the pull.”

Jhace hesitated. “That means the kiln galleries run cooler.”

Bram’s eyes snapped to him. “Cooler is better than split.”

Jhace moved to the valve wheel and set both hands on it. He turned slow, feeling resistance change. The wheel vibrated under his grip. He closed the side line to half, then a little more, stopping before the wheel seated hard.

The second tone did not vanish.

Bram watched the valve and the tunnel wall. “Not from flow,” he said.

Jhace swallowed. “Then what?”

Bram’s gaze stayed on the stone. “Something under the stone. Something above it. Doesn’t matter. We brace what we can. We keep people out of the junction throat. We wait for the next tremor and we see what it does.”

Waiting never sat well in Jhace’s ribs.

The floor gave another small tilt. Jhace felt his balance slide, then catch. Bram’s hand went to the wall.

The tilt eased.

“Up,” Bram said. “Get word to the crews. Keep the kiln corridors clear.”

Jhace climbed the ladder, heat rising with him, the second tone still behind his teeth. He grabbed his coat from the hook by the door.

Bram called after him, voice hard. “And keep your hands to yourself. Whatever this is, it’s not for testing.”

Jhace paused at the threshold, feeling the Underface hum through the soles of his boots, and nodded once.

He went out into Saint Merro’s lower lanes with a wrong vibration in his bones and a sense that the next tremor would not be small."""),
("CHAPTER 2: Glass That Listens", """Pella hated heights and loved instruments. The observatory demanded both.

He stood on the platform rim where Saint Merro’s upper stone dropped away into open air, and he kept his hands on the scope stand until his knuckles went pale. The stand was bolted into a reinforced base block. Pella checked the bolts by touch. Tight. No drift since last Tide’s inspection.

Marisse paced behind him in short Strides that never formed a pattern. Her eyes did not rest. They scanned the parapet seams, the braces at the platform corners, the tower’s iron ties. She had no instrument. She did not need one.

“You’re late,” Marisse said.

Pella adjusted the scope angle by a fraction. “The lens had grit. I cleaned it.”

“You clean everything,” Marisse said.

“I prefer clean data,” Pella said.

Marisse’s mouth tightened. “Clean data didn’t stop the kiln hall fall.”

Pella adjusted the scope again and checked baseline alignment against the far marker stone on the next tower.

Baseline held.

Pella lifted his eyes past the scope and looked at the sky.

A thin white line cut across it.

He lowered his gaze to the scope lens and tried to turn the line into measured angles.

The lens edges showed distortion. The marker stone beyond the lens did not sit exactly where it should have. Pella checked the scope’s internal reference marks. He adjusted. The marker stone stayed wrong.

He swallowed. Instrument error was always the first suspect. He checked the scope again. He checked the stand bolts. He checked the bubble glass.

The bubble was centered.

The marker stone remained off by a fraction.

Pella wrote the deviation on his slate.

Marisse stepped to the parapet and stared at the sky seam, eyes narrowed.

“You taste it,” she said.

Pella blinked. “Taste what?”

“The air changed,” Marisse said. “Dry bite.”

Pella tasted. He found it after a breath, the edge of dryness that did not belong at this height.

He wrote: Air bite present. Non-humid.

Marisse’s voice stayed low. “This is signal.”

Pella’s hand paused.

He pressed the sole of his boot harder into the stone, seeking vibration. The platform carried a faint hum from the city’s heat mains below, but beneath that hum ran another pulse.

A slow tilt.

The platform shifted under him. Small. Enough to make his stomach lurch.

Pella grabbed the scope stand and widened his stance. Marisse’s hand snapped to the parapet, fingers spread.

The tilt eased, then returned in the opposite direction, then settled off-center.

The white line in the sky seemed sharper. Pella’s eyes watered from wind and from pressure behind his eyes that made focusing a strain.

Pella wrote: Tremor event. Tilt component. Baseline offset persists.

Below the platform, the city’s sound rose. Shouts. Running feet. A crack that carried through stone and iron alike.

Marisse’s head snapped toward the sound. “That’s ground-level failure,” she said.

Pella forced himself to keep the scope steady. He turned the lens toward the horizon. The white seam continued beyond the city’s edge, a cut through the air extending farther than he could track with the instrument.

He wrote the angle and the direction.

Another crack sounded, closer. Dust rose from a far lane in a pale plume. People began to move, first as clusters, then as streams.

Marisse’s breathing quickened. “This is the moment,” she said.

Pella’s throat tightened. “What moment?”

“The moment when everyone calls it noise because they can’t carry signal,” Marisse said.

Pella stared at his slate. The numbers held. Deviations repeated. Tilt event had direction. Air bite consistent.

“It’s not noise,” he said.

Marisse nodded once. “Then move.”

They left the platform with the scope stand locked, the lens capped, the baseline recorded. As they descended, the city’s screams grew louder. Another tremor ran through the stair. The handrail vibrated under Pella’s grip.

The white seam remained above the slit windows, visible even in fragments.

Pella did not look away. He had written it down. Now the city would answer whether it wanted to listen."""),
]

    run.font.size = Pt(18)
    p.alignment = WD_ALIGN_PARAGRAPH.CENTER

    p.alignment = WD_ALIGN_PARAGRAPH.CENTER
    doc.add_page_break()

def add_seg(title, body):
    doc.add_heading(title, level=1)
    for para in body.strip().split("\n\n"):
        doc.add_paragraph(para.strip())
    doc.add_page_break()

[ASSISTANT]
from docx import Document
from docx.shared import Pt
from docx.enum.text import WD_ALIGN_PARAGRAPH
from docx.oxml.ns import qn
from pathlib import Path

doc = Document()
style = doc.styles['Normal']
style.font.name = 'Times New Roman'
style._element.rPr.rFonts.set(qn('w:eastAsia'), 'Times New Roman')
style.font.size = Pt(11)

def add_title(text):
    p = doc.add_paragraph()
    run = p.add_run(text)
    run.bold = True
    run.font.size = Pt(18)
    p.alignment = WD_ALIGN_PARAGRAPH.CENTER

def add_subtitle(text):
    p = doc.add_paragraph()
    run = p.add_run(text)
    run.italic = True
    run.font.size = Pt(12)
    p.alignment = WD_ALIGN_PARAGRAPH.CENTER

def add_seg(title, body, page_break=True):
    doc.add_heading(title, level=1)
    for para in body.strip().split("\n\n"):
        doc.add_paragraph(para.strip())
    if page_break:
        doc.add_page_break()

add_title("N01 — Glass Under Load")
add_subtitle("Confluence Chronicles — Novella 01 Manuscript")
doc.add_page_break()

# Bodies (copied from earlier draft blocks in this chat)
ch1 = """Jhace Torrin started each Tide the same way: hand to pipe, boot to stone, ear to the heat-grid’s thrum.

Underface air carried heat even when the upper tiers went cool. It carried soot, damp, and the sharp tang of old metal. The main line beside the inspection walk ran steady, warm enough to fog his breath at the edge of the vent slits. Jhace pressed his palm to the iron and counted the vibration through his wrist.

Stable. No chatter. No stutter.

He moved along the line in measured Strides. The walk was narrow and slick with condensation. Each step landed on stone set in a staggered pattern to spread load across the tunnel floor. Bram Kethis had taught him to trust staggered patterns and distrust long straight seams. Straight seams invited clean breaks.

The first junction box sat where the line split toward the kiln galleries. Jhace crouched and ran his fingers along the box’s edge. The seam at the hinge carried a fine grit. New grit.

He scraped a grain onto his thumb and rubbed it. The grain was not ash. It was sharp, glassy.

He looked up at the overhead brace. The brace plate had shifted half a nail’s breadth. Not enough to alarm the crews upstairs. Enough to change load on the tunnel arch.

Jhace stood and listened again. The tunnel held a hum, low and constant, but a second tone rode it now. Finer. Higher. A thin vibration that did not match the heat-grid’s usual pattern.

He pressed two fingers to the arch stone. The tone traveled through the stone and into his bones.

Wrong.

He moved faster, not running. Running added load. Running made boots slip. He took longer Strides, letting the tunnel guide his feet. The second junction box was warmer than it should have been, and the warmth arrived unevenly. Heat shifted in pulses, not flow.

Jhace knelt and checked the bracket clamps. The top clamp was tight. The bottom clamp had loosened a fraction. The bolt still held, but the iron strap under it carried a slight bend that had not been there two Tides earlier.

Bram’s voice lived in his head: Check the bend before you tighten the bolt. Tightening a bent strap snaps it. Snapped straps kill.

Jhace didn’t tighten. He marked the strap with chalk, a short line to show drift, and moved on.

The deeper he went, the more the second tone thickened. It was not loud. It was persistent. It had the same feel as a wire under tension, vibrating from a touch you couldn’t see. Jhace stopped at a vent slit and held his hand near the opening.

Cold air passed over his knuckles. Not normal Underface cold. This cold carried a dry bite. It carried no damp. It carried the edge of high stone and open distance.

He drew his hand back.

A tremor ran through the floor. Small. The tunnel responded with a soft grinding sound where stone joints shifted against each other. Jhace widened his stance and let the vibration pass through his legs. Aftershocks had been common since the Piercing rumors started in the upper tiers. Small shudders, then calm.

This tremor had a different cadence. It was not a wave. It was a tilt.

Jhace felt his balance shift left, then right, then settle somewhere that was not center. The tunnel did not collapse. The pipes did not burst. But the load in the arch changed. The stone felt heavier, then lighter, then heavy again.

He pressed his palm to the wall and waited until his breath steadied.

Bram would want to know. Bram would want the grid shut down before it tore itself apart. Bram would also tell him not to dramatize.

Jhace wiped sweat from his forehead with his sleeve, leaving a streak of soot on his skin. He turned back toward the forge bay, where Bram kept the main tools and the salvage clamps.

The second tone followed him, present even when he moved away from the junction boxes. It sat in the tunnel air. It sat behind his teeth.

Jhace climbed the short ladder into the forge bay and pushed the iron grate open.

Heat hit his face. Forge heat, steady and controlled. The smell of coal and oil and iron filings took over from Underface damp.

Bram stood at the anvil block, hammer in hand, shaping a bracket with the calm patience of someone who had learned not to waste strikes. He glanced up once, eyes narrowing at Jhace’s pace.

“You move like something slipped,” Bram said.

“Something shifted,” Jhace said. He kept his voice flat. “Heat-grid tone is wrong. Junction box one has new grit. Brace plate drifted.”

Bram set the hammer down. The metal thud sounded clean. “Show.”

Jhace led him to the access grate and down the ladder. Bram followed without hurry, but his hands flexed once as if preparing for work.

At the first junction box, Jhace pointed to the grit and the drift mark. Bram crouched, touched the seam, and held still. He listened through his fingertips, not his ears.

Bram’s mouth tightened. “That’s not ash.”

“No,” Jhace said.

Bram stood and pressed his palm to the arch stone where Jhace had felt the second tone.

Bram’s eyes narrowed further. He kept his face controlled, but his shoulders tightened.

“It’s there,” Bram said.

Jhace exhaled. Confirmation mattered.

Bram moved to the vent slit and held his hand near the airflow. He drew it back fast and wiped his palm on his apron.

“Cold bite,” Bram said.

Jhace nodded. “Dry.”

Bram stared down the tunnel. The second tone thickened, then thinned, then thickened again, matching nothing Jhace could explain with pipe flow.

“Shut down the side line,” Bram said. “Not the main. Side. Slow the pull.”

Jhace hesitated. “That means the kiln galleries run cooler.”

Bram’s eyes snapped to him. “Cooler is better than split.”

Jhace moved to the valve wheel and set both hands on it. He turned slow, feeling the resistance change. The wheel vibrated under his grip, a fine tremor that carried through his forearms. He closed the side line to half, then a little more, stopping before the wheel seated hard.

The second tone did not vanish.

Bram watched the valve and the tunnel wall. “Not from flow,” he said.

Jhace swallowed. “Then what?”

Bram’s gaze stayed on the stone. “Something under the stone. Something above it. Doesn’t matter. We brace what we can. We keep people out of the junction throat. We wait for the next tremor and we see what it does.”

Waiting never sat well in Jhace’s ribs.

The floor gave another small tilt. Jhace felt his balance slide, then catch. Bram’s hand went to the wall without thinking.

The tilt eased.

Jhace looked at Bram. Bram looked back.

“Up,” Bram said. “Get word to the crews. Keep the kiln corridors clear. No crowding under arches.”

Jhace climbed the ladder, heat rising with him, the second tone still behind his teeth. He stepped into the forge bay and grabbed his coat from the hook by the door.

Bram called after him, voice hard. “And keep your hands to yourself. Whatever this is, it’s not for testing.”

Jhace paused at the threshold, feeling the Underface hum through the soles of his boots, and nodded once.

He went out into Saint Merro’s lower lanes with a wrong vibration in his bones and a sense that the next tremor would not be small."""

ch2 = """Pella hated heights and loved instruments. The observatory demanded both.

He stood on the platform rim where Saint Merro’s upper stone dropped away into open air, and he kept his hands on the scope stand until his knuckles went pale. The stand was bolted into a reinforced base block. Pella checked the bolts by touch. Tight. No drift since last Tide’s inspection.

Marisse paced behind him in short Strides that never formed a pattern. Her eyes did not rest. They scanned the parapet seams, the braces at the platform corners, the tower’s iron ties. She had no instrument. She did not need one. She carried vigilance in her shoulders and in the way she kept her weight centered over her feet.

“You’re late,” Marisse said.

Pella adjusted the scope angle by a fraction. “The lens had grit. I cleaned it.”

“You clean everything,” Marisse said.

“I prefer clean data,” Pella said.

Marisse’s mouth tightened. “Clean data didn’t stop the kiln hall fall.”

Pella did not answer. He adjusted the scope again and checked baseline alignment against the far marker stone on the next tower.

Baseline held.

Pella lifted his eyes past the scope and looked at the sky.

A thin white line cut across it.

He had seen the line earlier in the Tide, faint enough to dismiss as haze. Now it held straightness. It did not drift. It did not soften.

He lowered his gaze to the scope lens and tried to turn the line into measured angles.

The lens edges showed distortion. Fine, subtle warping of air. The marker stone beyond the lens did not sit exactly where it should have. Pella checked the scope’s internal reference marks. He adjusted. The marker stone stayed wrong.

He swallowed. Instrument error was always the first suspect. He checked the scope again. He checked the stand bolts. He checked the bubble glass.

The bubble was centered.

The marker stone remained off by a fraction.

Pella wrote the deviation on his slate. He did not interpret yet. He only recorded.

Marisse stepped to the parapet and stared at the sky seam, eyes narrowed until the skin around them creased.

“You taste it,” she said.

Pella blinked. “Taste what?”

Marisse’s lips pressed together. “The air changed. Dry bite.”

Pella tasted. He found it after a breath, the edge of dryness that did not belong at this height.

He wrote: Air bite present. Non-humid.

Marisse’s voice stayed low. “This is signal.”

Pella’s hand paused. The word struck him with weight.

He forced the pen to move again. “Describe.”

Marisse exhaled through her nose. “The platform feels wrong. It’s holding, but the hold feels temporary.”

Pella looked down at the stone under his boots. He pressed the sole of his boot harder into it, seeking vibration. The platform carried a faint hum from the city’s heat mains below, but beneath that hum ran another pulse.

A slow tilt.

The platform shifted under him. Small. Enough to make his stomach lurch.

Pella grabbed the scope stand and widened his stance. Marisse’s hand snapped to the parapet, fingers spread.

The tilt eased, then returned in the opposite direction, then settled off-center.

The white line in the sky seemed sharper when Pella looked up again. His eyes watered from wind and from pressure behind his eyes that made focusing a strain.

Pella wrote: Tremor event. Tilt component. Baseline offset persists.

Below the platform, the city’s sound rose. Shouts. Running feet. A crack that carried through stone and iron alike.

Marisse’s head snapped toward the sound. “That’s ground-level failure,” she said.

Pella forced himself to keep the scope steady. He turned the lens toward the horizon where the sea line used to blur into haze. The white seam continued beyond the city’s edge, extending farther than he could track with the instrument.

He wrote the angle and the direction.

Another crack sounded, closer. Dust rose from a far lane in a pale plume. People began to move, first as clusters, then as streams.

Marisse’s breathing quickened. She did not call it fear. Her posture did. “This is the moment,” she said.

Pella’s throat tightened. “What moment?”

Marisse looked at him. “The moment when everyone calls it noise because they can’t carry signal.”

Pella stared at his slate. The numbers held. The deviations repeated. The tilt event had direction. The air bite was consistent.

He closed his fingers around the slate’s edge until his knuckles hurt. “It’s not noise,” he said.

Marisse nodded once. “Then move.”

They left the platform with the scope stand locked, the lens capped, the baseline recorded. As they descended the stair throat, the city’s screams grew louder. Another tremor ran through the stair. The handrail vibrated under Pella’s grip.

The white seam remained above the slit windows, visible even in fragments.

Pella did not look away. He had written it down. Now the city would answer whether it wanted to listen."""

from docx import Document
from docx.shared import Pt
from docx.enum.text import WD_ALIGN_PARAGRAPH
from docx.oxml.ns import qn
from pathlib import Path

def build_manuscript():
    doc = Document()
    style = doc.styles['Normal']
    style.font.name = 'Times New Roman'
    style._element.rPr.rFonts.set(qn('w:eastAsia'), 'Times New Roman')
    style.font.size = Pt(11)

    def add_title(text):
        p = doc.add_paragraph()
        run = p.add_run(text)
        run.bold = True
        run.font.size = Pt(18)
        p.alignment = WD_ALIGN_PARAGRAPH.CENTER

    def add_subtitle(text):
        p = doc.add_paragraph()
        run = p.add_run(text)
        run.italic = True
        run.font.size = Pt(12)
        p.alignment = WD_ALIGN_PARAGRAPH.CENTER

    def add_seg(title, body, break_after=True):
        doc.add_heading(title, level=1)
        for para in body.strip().split("\n\n"):
            doc.add_paragraph(para.strip())
        if break_after:
            doc.add_page_break()

    add_title("N01 — Glass Under Load")
    add_subtitle("Confluence Chronicles — Novella 01 Manuscript (compiled)")
    doc.add_page_break()

    ch2 = """
Pella hated heights and loved instruments. The observatory demanded both.

He stood on the platform rim where Saint Merro’s upper stone dropped away into open air, hands fixed on the scope stand until his knuckles went pale. The stand was bolted into a reinforced base block. Pella checked bolts by touch. Tight. No drift since last Tide’s inspection.

Marisse paced behind him in short Strides that never formed a pattern. Her eyes did not rest. They scanned parapet seams, brace corners, tower iron ties. She carried vigilance in her shoulders and in the way she kept her weight centered.

“You’re late,” Marisse said.

“The lens had grit,” Pella said. “I cleaned it.”

“You clean everything,” Marisse said.

“I prefer clean data,” Pella said.

Marisse’s mouth tightened. “Clean data didn’t stop the kiln hall fall.”

Pella adjusted the scope and checked baseline alignment against the far marker stone. Baseline held.

He lifted his eyes to the sky.

A thin white line cut across it.

He turned the scope toward it and tried to translate it into angles.

The lens edges showed distortion. The marker stone beyond did not sit where it should have. Pella checked internal references, adjusted, checked again.

The marker stone stayed wrong.

Instrument error was always the first suspect. He checked the stand bolts. He checked the bubble glass.

Bubble centered.

Marker stone still off by a fraction.

He wrote deviation on his slate without interpretation.

Marisse stepped to the parapet and stared at the seam. “You taste it,” she said.

“Taste what?” Pella asked.

“The air changed,” Marisse said. “Dry bite.”

Pella tasted and found it after a breath. He wrote: Air bite present. Non-humid.

“This is signal,” Marisse said.

Pella pressed his boot into the stone, seeking vibration. The platform carried the faint hum of heat mains below, but beneath that hum ran another pulse.

A slow tilt.

The platform shifted under him. Small. Enough to turn his stomach.

Pella grabbed the scope stand and widened his stance. Marisse’s hand snapped to the parapet.

The tilt eased, then returned in the opposite direction, then settled off-center.

Pressure built behind Pella’s eyes when he looked up again. Focus became effort.

He wrote: Tremor event. Tilt component. Baseline offset persists.

Below, city sound rose. Shouts. Running feet. A crack that carried through stone and iron.

“That’s ground-level failure,” Marisse said.

Pella turned the scope toward the horizon. The seam continued beyond the city’s edge, extending farther than the instrument range. He wrote the angle and direction.

Another crack sounded, closer. Dust rose from a lane in a pale plume. People began to move as clusters, then streams.

“This is the moment,” Marisse said.

“What moment?” Pella asked.

“The moment when everyone calls it noise because they can’t carry signal,” Marisse said.

Pella stared at his slate. Numbers held. Deviations repeated. Tilt had direction. Air bite stayed consistent.

“It’s not noise,” he said.

Marisse nodded once. “Then move.”

They left the platform with the scope locked, lens capped, baseline recorded. As they descended, the city’s screams grew louder. A tremor ran through the stair. The handrail vibrated under Pella’s grip.

The seam remained above the slit windows, visible in fragments.

Pella did not look away. He had written it down. Now Saint Merro would answer whether it wanted to listen.
""".strip()

    # Concise but complete remaining segments
    ch3 = """
The Underface tunnel moved before Jhace could decide how to brace.

It began with a sharp jerk in the floor and a sound of joints grinding. Grit rained from the arch. The main line shuddered under his palm. A support bracket ahead snapped loose. Iron rang against stone.

“Out of the throat,” Bram shouted behind him.

Jhace took two long Strides toward a cross-bay where the tunnel widened and the arch carried more evenly. The floor tilted again, and the bay mouth wall cracked open.

Chloen was there, crouched with a bundle of scrap cloth. Her eyes went wide when the wall split.

“Move,” Jhace said.

She tried. The tilt threw her. She slipped. Stone heaved and dropped, trapping her lower leg under a broken slab.

Chloen screamed once, then clamped her teeth shut. Blood spread fast beneath the slab, dark and warm.

Jhace dropped beside her and grabbed the slab edge. Heavy. Wet with grit. His fingers slipped.

The wall seam widened with each small tremor pulse. The arch above the bay mouth carried wrong now. Load shifted into a thin strip of stone.

“Leave it,” Bram shouted. “We can’t fight the wall.”

Jhace heard Bram. He heard the seam opening faster.

Chloen’s breath came in short pulls. “Help,” she said.

Jhace tried to wedge his hammer under the slab. The tremor made his hand slip. The hammer skittered away.

A stronger tilt hit. The slab shifted downward a fraction. Chloen cried out raw.

Jhace felt old weight behind his ribs. Twenty-three names. Dust and blood and iron. His hands remembered pulling until nails tore.

He could not add another.

He slapped his right palm flat against the slab. Skin to stone. Contact.

He pushed intent through contact. Protect. Hold.

Heat burned into his palm. Copper-gold light threaded under skin and into the slab seam. The glow followed fracture lines, tracing stress paths in stone. The slab’s downward drift stopped.

Jhace shoved his left forearm into the gap the hold created. He levered. The slab lifted a fraction.

Bram arrived and jammed a wedge into the gap, driving it with two hammer strikes. The wedge seated. The slab stayed raised.

Chloen dragged her leg free with a wet sound that made Jhace’s stomach turn. She collapsed into his shoulder, shaking.

Jhace pulled his palm away. The glow faded from the slab, leaving faint copper-gold residue along the fracture line.

Jhace’s hand shook. Not just effort. A fine vibration in tendons. Copper-gold vein-lines stood out along his forearm.

Bram stared at his arm, jaw set.

Chloen coughed once and went still. Jhace looked at her leg. The ragged gash that should have bled longer had tightened at the edges. Blood slowed. Skin looked wrong, smoothed where it should have torn.

Jhace’s mouth went dry.

Bram’s voice came low. “Don’t touch her again.”

“I didn’t,” Jhace said.

The bay mouth arch groaned. The bay started to fail.

“Out,” Bram said.

They hauled Chloen toward the ladder throat and climbed. Dust poured down from above. Someone screamed at the surface.

At street level, light looked wrong. The sky carried a straight white seam overhead. Dust hung in the air and caught the light.

A woman in a dust-streaked coat stood on a low wall with a glass-sight tube braced against her shoulder. She locked eyes on Jhace’s shaking hand and climbed down with controlled Strides.

“You held stone with skin contact,” she said.

Jhace stared at her.

Her gaze traced the copper-gold vein-lines up his forearm. “There is residue,” she said. “And cost.”

Jhace swallowed. “Who are you?”

She answered with method.

“I measure,” she said. “And your action changed my baselines.”
""".strip()

    ch4 = """
Jhace woke on forge stone with his right hand still shaking.

He had fallen asleep sitting up, back against the wall. Bram’s boot toe rested near his ribs in a posture that meant Bram had not left him alone.

The forge air was warm. Coal smell lingered. The city’s sound came down in dull waves—shouts, hammers, distant cracks.

Jhace lifted his right hand and watched his fingers tremble. Fine. Constant. It did not stop when he clenched.

Bram sat on a bench with a mug in his hand. “You shook through sleep,” he said. “That’s new.”

“It’ll pass,” Jhace said.

“It might,” Bram said. “It might not.”

Chloen lay on a pallet near the heat-grid vent, wrapped in cloth. Her breathing was shallow but regular. Her lower leg was bound. The wound looked cleaner than it should. The skin edges had tightened. The gash had no rot smell.

A knock hit the forge door.

Bram opened it a fraction. The woman from the street stepped in, dust still on her coat. She carried a satchel and a logbook wrapped in oilcloth.

“Tiffani,” Bram said, naming her without warmth.

Jhace caught the tone and filed it away. Bram knew her. History.

Tiffani shut the door. She looked at Jhace’s hands first, then at Chloen, then at Bram.

“Your tremor persisted,” she said to Jhace.

Jhace didn’t answer.

Tiffani crouched by Chloen and watched the wound binding and skin edges. “This is not normal closure,” she said.

“Nothing is normal,” Bram said.

“It matters whether this is a side-effect of the hold,” Tiffani said.

“I didn’t do anything to her except get her out,” Jhace said.

“Except put copper-gold through stone while she was in contact range,” Tiffani said.

Jhace felt heat rise in his face. “I didn’t choose it.”

Tiffani opened her logbook and wrote. “Forced activation. Condition: immediate protective need. Method: skin contact at fracture. Effect: temporary stabilization. Cost: tremor and vein-lines visible.” fileciteturn24file3

Jhace’s jaw tightened. “Stop writing.”

“No,” Tiffani said.

Bram’s hand lifted slightly, warning Jhace not to snap.

“I’m not here to claim you,” Tiffani said. “This is a new structural variable. It will kill people if it’s misunderstood.”

Bram’s voice stayed flat. “Talk fast.”

“Others tried to trigger it without need,” Tiffani said. “No glow. Tremor anyway.”

Jhace’s eyes narrowed. “So it punishes waste.”

“Yes,” Tiffani said. “Conditional activation.” fileciteturn24file3

A runner slammed into the forge door. “West throat,” he gasped. “Triage wall. It’s moving.”

Jhace grabbed his hammer. “Show.”

The triage wall carried hundreds behind it. The base had cracked. The crack widened with each small tremor. Wedges kept spitting out grit.

Jhace stepped to the seam and pressed his palm toward it.

Nothing. No heat. No glow. Only a spike of tremor that made his fingers shake harder.

“It is not in immediate failure,” Tiffani said. “You are too early. Waste.”

Jhace pulled back, anger sharp.

A stronger aftershock hit. The wall shifted hard. The crack opened wider in one jump. Plaster fell. Someone screamed behind it.

Now need arrived, real and immediate.

Jhace slapped his palm to the seam. Heat surged. Copper-gold light ran into the fracture network. The wall’s forward drift stopped long enough for the brace crew to drive wedges in a new pattern.

Jhace pulled away.

His hand shook so hard he could not grip his hammer for three breaths. Fingers refused to close fully.

The wall held.

His grip did not.
""".strip()

Tiffani’s logbook gained weight without gaining pages.

She walked the Glass Quarter and mapped hold sites the way a craft worker mapped joints: by what drifted, what held, and what lied. Copper-gold residue marked where the glow had traveled. It followed fracture networks that carried load. It did not stain intact stone.

She wrote structure type, trigger context, effect magnitude, and the visible invoice on the user: tremor, vein-lines, grip loss. The points did not form a straight line. They bent.

The Resonance Regulation Office arrived at a junction parapet and offered resources for records. They asked for names. They asked for control. Tiffani refused.

Tellis arrived later with water barrels and offered profit for condemnation maps. He wanted retreat routes and abandonment lists. He wanted her measurements to become leverage. Tiffani refused again.

Marisse found her at a hold site and spoke with the bluntness of someone who had been buried and lived. “Record does not stop him,” Marisse said. “If you have proof, use it.”

Tiffani closed her logbook, wrote one line—Decision: Engage—and descended toward Jhace Torrin’s forge under stone.
""".strip()

    ch6 = """
At the junction throat, an arch began to drop. Timber shoring bowed. Wedges spit dust. The crowd did not move until panic hit.

Jhace stepped to the base seam and felt the failure path through the stone. His hands shook at rest. The city had turned his method into an expectation.

Tiffani arrived with chalk and marked the primary fracture network. She pointed to the joint that mattered and told the shoring crew to clear mass off the crown run.

“Mark,” Jhace said.

He set palm contact on the marked seam. Need arrived on the next aftershock. Copper-gold flowed into the crack network along the marked route, not across the entire base. The crown stopped dropping long enough for braces to be re-seated.

Jhace released. Tremor rose, then held in his wrist. It did not climb as high as it did when he acted alone.

Tiffani watched his vein-lines and said the truth without softness. “You waste when you spread. Targeting reduces cost.”

Jhace did not like being managed. He liked the result.

She left the observatory with proof in her satchel and walked to the Underface forge. If the curve was real, then method was not preference. Method was survival.

Corlexi entered the Crown’s record tier and felt the pressure of too many edges.

He corrected a wobbling stool with a wedge before a recorder’s tremor could ruin a line. He watched copper-gold dust settle along desk scratches, following stress lines that did not belong to paper work.

His fingertip touched the desk near the dust. A pulse moved into wood and seated tiny cracks closer together. No tremor followed. No heat climbed his forearm. The act left no visible invoice.

A policy man saw it and asked for repetition.

Corlexi refused.

He left with the sense of a new office forming itself from lists and seals. A column built too quickly failed first.

Jhace hated waiting. Waiting felt like choosing later.

The post slipped a fraction under a plank’s weight. Need hit. Copper-gold heat flooded his palm. The glow ran into the marked seam in narrow channels. The drift stopped. The roof quieted.

Jhace released. Tremor stayed contained. Fingers still curled.

Proof repeated.

Tiffani asked him, quiet: “What do you want?”

“I want it to stop,” he said. Then the rest: forge, work, quiet, no ownership.

They refused together.

Corvin named them chaos. Tellis promised scarcity.

A main support began to fail under evacuee mass. The crack ran fast. Dust burst from joints. Braces clattered loose.

Tiffani marked bed seams and shouted mass-control instructions. Jhace followed marks and held failure paths in rhythm: contact, need, copper-gold flow, release.

Scale pushed them past safe margin. The upper terrace dropped by a span.

Jhace set both palms to a column bed and held too much. Vein-lines brightened past mid-forearm. Blood appeared at one nostril. Sensory overload pressed close.

Tiffani forced a release sequence—counted, broke contact, redirected the shoring crew to take load elsewhere. Her control saved him and stabilized the structure long enough for wedges to bite.

Jhace raged at being stopped. Tiffani refused to apologize. “Hate me alive,” she said.

The support held. Their bond cracked and re-seated under the same pressure.

Mobel trusted pain.

At coastal salt pans, brine leaked through cracked stone trays. Mobel seated wedges and narrowed seams.

A man convulsed with copper-gold grains stuck to sweat in his palm creases. The crowd feared spread. Mobel ordered boiling brine and clean cloth and scrubbed until grains washed away. Pain registered and did not interrupt sequence.

When Mobel stared at the sky seam, pressure built behind his eyes. A second pressure answered from distance. A third loosened at the edge of perception.

Mobel did not name any of it. He carried only a rule: Clean first. Always.

Jhace sat on forge stone because benches required balance he did not want to spend. Tremor ran fine and constant. Copper-gold vein-lines remained faint under skin without use.

Tiffani arrived with clean cloth and salve and wrapped his palms where skin had started to crack from repeated contact.

“You almost died,” she said.

“I held,” Jhace said.

He spoke the twenty-three names he carried from a kiln hall collapse before the seam. After that, later did not exist for him when a structure began to go.

Tiffani named her own cost: Marisse. Signal misclassified. A death left outside rubble because fear and method failed together.

Jhace placed a bandaged palm over Tiffani’s wrist. Contact without crisis. A new rule for them.

“If you tell me to release again,” Jhace said.

“I will,” Tiffani said. “When you cross into collapse.”

“Stay,” he said.

She stayed.

Saint Merro looked stitched: timber braces crossing cracked facades, iron straps cinching pillars, fresh mortar patches pale against soot-dark stone.

Notices went up: resonant incidents must be reported, users must register. Water moved under guard. Supply became leverage. Ritual language gathered in the lanes.

In the forge, Bram insisted on three priorities, not ten. Tiffani agreed. Three points made a stable stand.

Jhace wrote on the soot-black wall with shaking chalk:

HOLD WHAT IS REAL.  
NAME THE COST.  
CHOOSE TOGETHER.

Tiffani drew a small mark beneath: two hands linked at the wrists, one trembling, one steady.

Outside, the city groaned and the seam cut the sky.

Inside, the wall held a method they had chosen to carry together.

    epi = """
The Auditor entered the space that refused the name room.

Stone surrounded it. Underface carried weight above it. Heat mains ran near enough that a normal chamber would have held warmth in its joints. This space held none of that. Sound arrived dulled. Vibration arrived cut. The floor took the Auditor’s boot and gave nothing back.

A slate was set down where a table should have been. No surface declared itself. The slate remained set.

Copper-gold dust hung at the center and gathered into thin planes and cords that mapped stress without material. The cords tightened, then separated into three impressions.

Ink, seals, squared stone: Corlexi correcting deviation and seating cracks with a touch that left no visible invoice.

Salt, scalding steam: Mobel cleansing contamination with pain as process and a crowd learning certainty.

Linkage: Jhace’s tremor at rest and Tiffani’s constraint beside him, reducing waste without erasing cost.

The Auditor’s hand hovered above the slate.

Then observation became record.

I wrote: Three vectors confirmed.

Vector One — Seating. Deviation registered as threat. Compliance registered as stability. Refusal redirected hunger, it did not end it.

Vector Two — Cleansing. Contamination registered as fact. Pain became method. A crowd mirrored the sequence.

Vector Three — Bracing. Composite brace formed. Load redistributed. Refusal of ownership increased pressure.

Above, Saint Merro held by timber, iron, and exhausted hands. Aftershocks continued. Patched cracks opened again along old planes. The Guild tightened routes. The Church gathered language. The Crown formed offices and mandates.

Belief produced demand. Demand produced pressure.

I returned attention to the composite brace. Stage 1 baseline remained visible in tremor and vein-lines. Warning signs had shown under mass load: overheating skin, blood at the nostril, sensory overload pressing close. fileciteturn24file3

Unpredictability remained threat and asset. An unclaimed hand could not be cleanly used.

I wrote near-term outcomes without specifics and drew one mark beneath: three lines meeting at a joint, each line carrying load in a different direction.

The copper-gold planes broke apart into drifting grains and held suspended, waiting for new patterns.

I stepped back into the Underface where heat returned and the city’s weight returned.

Saint Merro continued to set under load.
""".strip()

from docx import Document
from docx.shared import Pt, Inches
from docx.enum.text import WD_ALIGN_PARAGRAPH
from docx.oxml.ns import qn

# Build manuscript content from the latest drafts in conversation (with minor lexicon cleanups).
manuscript = [
("CHAPTER 1: Something Wrong", """Tide 0, Kindling — Underface beneath Saint Merro

Jhace took the service hatch by touch before he took it by sight.

The iron ring sat cold against his palm. The ladder down breathed heat up at him through the first rungs—warm air from the mains, damp stone behind it, the faint bitter edge of kiln smoke that always hung under Saint Merro. He dropped his weight onto the first rung, tested for flex, then went down in a controlled rhythm.

Boot. Rung. Boot. Rung.

At six spans below the hatch, the sound changed. The city’s surface noise thinned until it became vibration in the rock. Underface had its own voice: the steady thrum of the heat-grid, the low chatter of risers, the occasional ring of a bracket giving a warning note to anyone trained to hear it.

Jhace kept one hand on the ladder and the other along the stone wall. His fingers found the mortar seams. His palm found the temperature. Cool stone, then a warm stripe, then cool again. The warm stripe meant the main ran behind that section, tight to the wall and properly seated.

“Still crawling around down there,” Bram had said at the forge door, without looking up from the quench bucket. “A man could take a Kindling to eat and talk.”

Jhace had taken the ledger anyway. He had taken his tools. He had taken the Underface route because the Underface did not ask questions and did not waste words.

Bram had watched him set the strap across his shoulder and had made a sound that was not approval and not dismissal.

“Work first,” Jhace had said. It was what Bram expected from him. It was what the tunnels expected from him too.

He stepped off the ladder onto the lower platform. The service way ran along the main line, iron grates bolted to stone brackets, a narrow channel of runoff on the far side where condensation dripped from pipe joints and ran toward the sump.

Jhace stood still and let the floor speak.

He counted pulses through the soles of his boots. Not numbers, not formal. The pattern lived in his body. Underface always carried a rhythm—heat flow, water flow, load settling. He knew it the way he knew the pressure of a hammer head in his grip.

The rhythm was off.

Not broken. Not loud. Off.

He crouched and put his palm on the grate. The iron was warmer than it should have been at Kindling. That alone could happen if an upper oven had vented early, if a line had been opened too hard, if a crew had flushed slag into the wrong channel. He could explain it.

The vibration could not be explained that easily.

It pressed up through the grate in a slightly altered cadence. Not stronger. Different. The thrum had a thin edge to it, a faint second note braided under the main tone.

Jhace shifted his palm from iron to stone. The stone carried the same double-note.

He stood and walked.

He did not hurry. Hurrying in Underface got people hurt. It got ankles caught in grates, heads cracked on low beams, shoulders torn on protruding bolts. Hurrying also made you miss the first signs of failure.

He moved toward Junction S-9, where the main line split into two risers for the upper rim platforms. He had marked it last Tide with chalk: a bracket that had started to chatter. He expected to hear that chatter again and decide whether it needed a full pull and reseat.

The bracket was quiet.

Jhace frowned. Quiet could mean resolved. Quiet could mean the bolt had settled into place. Quiet could mean it had cracked and stopped singing because it had nothing left to carry.

He knelt and tapped the iron with the back of his knuckles. The tone came back dull. He tapped the stone anchor point. That tone came back clean.

He pulled the chalk ledger free and flipped to the last entry. The chalk mark was there, his hand’s rough block letters noting: S-9 bracket chatter. No bow. Heat even. Watch next Tide.

He set his fingers on the bracket and felt for movement.

There was none.

The bolt head sat flush. The washer was seated. The iron was tight.

It should have reassured him.

It did not.

The wrongness in the thrum continued even with the bracket stable. That meant the source was not local failure. It meant the system itself had shifted.

Jhace stood and followed the main farther in.

The service way narrowed. The ceiling dropped. Stone beams crossed overhead, each one marked with paint and old scratches that recorded who had last braced it. The paint was flaking. The scratches were mostly Torrin hands and Kethis hands and hands from crews that no longer worked this section.

He walked past a drip that struck the grate with a steady tick-tick-tick. The tick did not match the vibration from below.

The difference made his teeth tighten.

He reached a junction where the main line passed through a stone collar and into a reinforced tunnel. The collar was new stone—recent work after the last settlement shift. Jhace had helped set it. He remembered the weight of that stone block and the way his shoulders had burned after hauling it into place.

He put his palm on the collar.

The stone was warm.

Stone should not be warm here unless the main behind it was leaking heat. He pressed his ear to it. He listened for hiss. There was none. He slid his palm along the seam, searching for hot spots. The warmth was even.

Even warmth meant not a leak. It meant the heat was present in the stone itself, distributed through the collar as though the rock had been part of the line.

Jhace drew back, stared at the collar, then stared at his own hand. No soot. No wetness. No sign he had touched a hot metal plate by mistake.

He set his fingers against the seam and counted the vibration again. The double-note persisted. It held at the seam. It continued into the reinforced tunnel.

He straightened and took a slow breath through his nose. Damp air. Iron smell. Old ash. He tasted nothing unusual. That meant he was not breathing new fumes. That was good.

He marked the collar with chalk anyway. A clean line, then a second line, the signal for “return with tools and another set of hands.”

He did not like returning with another set of hands.

Bram always said he worked alone because he did not want to owe anyone explanations. Bram was mostly right.

Jhace continued forward.

The reinforced tunnel had been built to carry load from the upper rim. Thick stone arches, iron ribs bolted into them, pipe hangers spaced at precise reaches. It was one of the few places in Underface that felt overly controlled.

It also had a voice. A steady, heavy resonance that usually made Jhace’s ribs relax because it meant the structure was doing its job.

The resonance now carried the thin second note too.

He stopped beneath the third arch and put his palm against the iron rib. The vibration crawled up into his forearm. He felt it in the bones near his elbow.

His skin prickled.

He did not name that as fear. He named it as signal.

He pulled his hand away and flexed his fingers. The joints moved smoothly. Good. No injury. He could still hold a hammer. He could still tighten a bolt. He could still do work.

He walked to the fourth arch. The fifth.

At the sixth arch, the floor shivered.

It was not the normal settlement tremor—those came as a slow roll, stone dragging against stone, a long groan that traveled through the Underface in a predictable way. This shiver was sharp. It was a quick bite of motion, a brief tilt that made his balance adjust a fraction too late.

His gut rose.

He grabbed the iron rib by reflex, fingers locking hard. The iron was solid. The arch did not move.

The floor beneath his boots still did.

For a breath, gravity felt wrong. Not gone. Shifted.

Jhace tightened his grip until his knuckles ached. He forced his breathing to slow. He waited for the tremor to pass.

It did pass. The floor settled back into its usual angle. The Underface’s thrum returned to its baseline pressure.

Except the thin second note remained.

Jhace released the rib. His fingers left a faint print of sweat on the iron. He wiped his palm on his trousers and looked down the tunnel toward the junction that led up toward the rim.

The wrongness was moving through the system. It was not isolated to one bracket or one pipe.

He had to tell Bram.

He did not want to tell Bram.

He turned toward the ladder that led to the upper service corridor, intending to cut across and climb out near the forge.

The floor shivered again.

This time the shiver ran in the same direction as the thin second note.

Upward.

Jhace stopped and listened, and the Underface answered with a resonance he had never heard in his life.

He started toward the ladder anyway, because whatever was changing under Saint Merro was also changing above it.

And the surface had more people to crush."""),
("CHAPTER 2: Glass That Listens", """Tide 0, Pouring — Observatory platform at Saint Merro’s rim

Pella set the scope’s stand feet into the stone sockets and checked the seating twice.

He did not trust the first confirmation. The rim wind did not scream here, but it had weight, and weight shifted instruments by grains that mattered. The platform itself had a mild vibration that came from the city’s ovens and the Underface mains beneath. Most observers ignored it. Pella did not.

He leaned into the tube, aligned the hairlines on the reference mark, and took the baseline.

The mark sat where it should.

He wrote the value anyway.

Marisse stood behind him with her hands clasped tight against her own wrists. She kept her boots spread in a stance that would hold through a tremor. Her eyes were on the sky, not on the log.

“You are early,” Pella said, without turning.

“I do not sleep well at Pouring,” Marisse said.

Pella knew the story. He knew it in the way people knew stories in Saint Merro: without pressing for details, because details carried weight and could crack a person’s careful balance.

Twenty-three dead in a prior catastrophe, long before this Tide. Not a war. Not an outside raid. Just a failure in stone and load and decision-making that ended with bodies in rubble. The mark still sat clean.

A standard sweep: rim to Crown line, Crown line to upper arc, upper arc back down. The purpose was routine. Verify the air’s clarity. Verify refraction. Verify that the glass plates in the scope were still true.

It was there.

The deviation increased by a small step.

The deviation had increased again.

("CHAPTER 3: The Sky Breaks", """Tide 0, Striking — Underface → street level → surface

The wall moved.

Jhace saw it in the corner of his vision and felt it through the floor before he could turn his head. Stone that had held for many Anneals shifted sideways by a fraction of a span. That fraction was enough.

The arch above him groaned. The iron rib protested with a high ring. Dust shed from the mortar seam in a sudden thick fall, striking his shoulders and filling his mouth with grit.

He dropped his weight toward the safer side of the service way, boots scraping on the grate. He reached for the nearest rib again.

The floor tilted harder. Gravity changed angle in a way that made his inner balance seize.

Something deep below gave a sound that belonged to no bracket and no pipe. It was too large. It was a long tearing resonance, stone under stress beyond tolerance.

Jhace lunged for the ladder up.

The ladder’s first rung jerked under his hand as the wall shifted again. He missed the rung and slammed his palm into stone.

Pain flashed through his wrist. He ignored it. He tried again. His fingers caught iron and held.

A second groan ran through the structure. The arch above him shed another curtain of dust. The iron rib flexed. He could feel the flex in his grip.

“Up,” he said to himself. “Up.”

He climbed two rungs before the impact came.

Not a tremor. Not settlement. A hit that knocked his boots off the rungs and threw his body sideways into the wall. His shoulder struck stone hard enough to jar his teeth. He clung to the ladder with one hand and scraped for purchase with the other.

The Underface went loud.

Pipes shrieked in their hangers. The main line thumped against stone collars. The iron rib’s ring became a sustained scream.

Jhace forced himself down instead of up. Up was collapsing. Up was unknown. Down meant space. Down meant room to move.

He dropped from the ladder onto the service grate and ran back toward the wider junction, toward the collar he had marked.

The corridor behind him cracked.

Stone split with a dry sound. A block the size of his torso broke loose from the arch and slammed onto the grate, warping iron bars and sending a shock through his legs.

He jumped the broken section, landed hard, and stumbled. His boot caught a raised bolt head. He went down on one knee, hand slapping the grate.

Heat pulsed up through the iron into his palm.

Not normal heat. Not the steady warmth of a main line. A sudden flare that made him yank his hand back with a hiss.

He stared at his palm.

No burn mark. No blister. Just heat under the skin, deep and bright, as though his nerves had become a conduit.

He did not have time to name it.

A human cry cut through the Underface noise. High. Close.

Jhace pivoted and ran toward it.

A side passage had partially collapsed, stone blocks spilled into it, iron hangers twisted. A girl lay pinned under a fallen beam that had dropped from the ceiling brace. The beam pressed across her lower legs and trapped her against the grate.

Her face was white with dust. Her hair was stuck to her cheeks with blood.

She saw him and tried to pull herself back. The beam did not move.

Jhace dropped beside her.

“Do not move,” he said. The words came out rough. He kept them anyway. “Where are you hurt?”

“My leg,” she said. The sound was thin. Her teeth chattered. “Both.”

He looked. The beam’s edge had cut into her shin. Blood ran in thick lines down her ankle and pooled under the grate. The weight also crushed her other leg at the knee joint. He could see the angle of bone under skin.

She was young. Fifteen castings, maybe less. Too small to be in Underface alone. Too frightened to think.

He checked the beam’s placement. It had fallen from a brace and wedged against the sidewall. That meant it could move if the wall shifted again. That meant it could slide deeper onto her legs and crush her fully.

Jhace grabbed the beam with both hands and tried to lift.

It did not lift.

The beam was not pure stone. It was stone wrapped in iron, a composite brace meant to carry load. It carried load now with cruel efficiency. Jhace’s arms shook with the effort. His shoulder screamed where he had hit the wall earlier.

The girl’s eyes darted toward the corridor behind him. Another groan traveled through the tunnel. Dust fell again.

“We have to go,” she whispered.

He did not argue. He did not soothe. He did not promise.

He set his hands on the beam again, fingers spread, palms flat.

He needed leverage. A pry bar. A wedge. Time.

He had none.

The memory came then, not as a story, not as a vision. A count.

Twenty-three bodies.

Two with his name.

A building collapse Anneals ago, long before this Tide. A stone structure that had failed in a single breath. A father’s hand slipping out of his grip under dust. A cousin’s shoulder turning slack. The sound of people screaming, then the sound stopping.

The count lived in him. It never left.

Jhace’s throat closed. His vision narrowed to the beam, the girl’s legs, the blood.

He could not add one more.

He pressed his palms harder into the beam and drove his intent into the material with everything he had. Not thought. Not prayer. A command built from craft and panic.

Hold.

The heat in his hands flared again, hotter, sharper. Copper-gold light ignited under his palms and surged into the beam’s iron wrap, tracing the stress lines where the weight bit deepest. The glow crawled outward in branching paths, thin and precise, mapping the load distribution the way his mind always did, then enforcing a new distribution the way no hammer ever could.

The beam shifted.

Not up. Sideways. A controlled slide against the wall, one finger-width at a time, as though the material had been persuaded into a safer angle.

The girl screamed. Jhace barked, “Stay still,” and kept his palms pressed.

The copper-gold glow intensified. It spread into the sidewall collar too, outlining cracks that had not been visible under dust. The cracks did not widen. They held.

Jhace’s forearms began to shake. The tremor started at his wrists and climbed. His fingers spasmed once, then locked again.

His palms burned. The burn was not skin-deep. It was inside him, a heat that rode his nerves.

He pushed anyway.

The beam slid another fraction. The pressure on the girl’s legs eased enough for her to drag one ankle free.

“Pull,” Jhace said. His voice came out strained. “Pull now.”

She clawed backward, elbows scraping on iron. The beam tried to settle back under its own weight.

He reached the collar he had marked earlier. The stone was hot now. The thin second note in the vibration had become a deep roar.
