("CHAPTER 4: After the Shudder", """Tide 0, Quench — Saint Merro lower streets and triage wall

Jhace woke with his hands already shaking.

He sat up too fast and paid for it immediately. His head spun. His stomach clenched. Dust coated his tongue. He coughed once, then twice, and the cough scraped his throat raw.

He was on a low pallet in a corridor that smelled of wet stone and burned glass. People lay on either side of him—some groaning, some silent, some staring at the ceiling with eyes that did not blink.

A triage corridor. Not the forge. Not the Underface. Not safety.

His forearms were bare. The vein-lines were still there, dark and branching, running from wrist to elbow. They looked like bruises shaped by a careful hand.

Jhace held his arms away from his body and watched the tremor. Fine vibration in his fingers. Worse in his right hand. He tried to close his fist.

The fist closed. It did not close cleanly. The last two fingers lagged.

He forced them down anyway.

Work first.

He swung his legs off the pallet and stood. His knees threatened to fold. He steadied himself against the wall and counted breaths until the dizziness reduced enough to move.

A group of people surged past the corridor opening, carrying a child wrapped in cloth. Someone shouted for water. Someone else shouted for a brace crew. The air had the dense pressure of a city holding itself together by sheer refusal to fall.

Jhace left the pallet behind and walked toward the nearest open space. He found a half-collapsed stairwell leading to an alley. The alley’s stones were cracked, but the walls still stood.

He stepped outside.

The sky seam was still there.

The white line cut across the upper arc, permanent in its wrongness. Dust still drifted through the air in slow sheets. It settled on everything and turned Saint Merro’s stone a dull gray.

Jhace’s gaze dropped to the nearest crack in the alley wall. A simple fracture line running through mortar and stone. Not critical. Not load-bearing. A shallow crack that would be patched by any competent crew in a future Tide.

He crouched and put his palm on it.

He tried to summon the copper-gold glow.

He did not do it with desperation. He did it with intent, with craft knowledge, with the memory of how it had felt in the Underface.

Nothing happened.

No glow. No warmth. No shift in the crack.

Jhace pressed harder, jaw clenched, and tried again.

Still nothing.

Instead, a small pulse of heat flared in his palm and died immediately, leaving behind a sharp ache that ran up his forearm. The tremor spiked once. His fingers jerked.

He pulled his hand away and stared at it.

So it was not a tool he could pick up on command.

The stranger’s words returned: When it is real.

Jhace did not curse. He stood and flexed his hand until the tremor eased back to its baseline vibration.

He turned to go back inside and almost collided with someone stepping into the alley.

The stranger from the street. Close now, dust streaking her hair, a logbook held tight under one arm.

Her eyes went straight to the crack he had touched.

“You tested,” she said. Not a question. A conclusion.

Jhace kept his voice short. “It failed.”

“It should,” she said. “No immediate threat. No protective need. No trigger state.”

Jhace stared at her. “You speak as though you have written rules.”

“I speak as though I record what repeats,” she said. “Rules come later.”

She angled the logbook slightly toward him, then pulled it back again before he could read it. The motion was controlled, not teasing. Boundary.

“Where is the girl,” she asked.

“Inside,” Jhace said. “Triage corridor.”

He expected her to push past him. Instead she stayed in the alley and watched him.

“You shook in your sleep,” she said.

Jhace’s jaw tightened. “I do not—”

“You do,” she said. “Your hands. Your forearms. Your grip.”

Jhace looked down at his hands as if that would make them behave. The tremor continued. It did not care about pride.

The stranger wrote a short note in her logbook without looking away from him.

“What is your name,” she said.

“Jhace Torrin.”

A flicker crossed her face at the name. Not surprise. Recognition of a type: Underface worker, forge-hand, someone used to weight.

“And you,” he said, because the exchange required balance.

“Tiffani,” she said. “Observatory.”

That explained the way she watched. The way she turned disaster into measured categories.

A shout rose from the corridor behind them. A different shout this time—urgent, panicked.

“Wall!” someone cried. “The triage wall is going!”

Jhace’s body moved before his mind finished deciding. He ran back into the corridor with Tiffani at his side.

They reached the main triage space and saw the problem immediately.

A long retaining wall held back a slope of broken stone and debris from a collapsed upper tier. The wall had been cracked during Striking. It had been braced with hastily set timbers and iron straps. It was now bowing outward, slowly, under the weight of the rubble behind it.

People were clustered too close. Healers—hands only, no magic, just bandages and splints—worked on the injured along the wall’s base because it was the only open stretch of stone that had not flooded with runoff. If the wall failed, the debris would bury them all.

Jhace pushed through the crowd, shoulders braced, ignoring protests.

He put his hand on the wall.

The stone vibrated. The crack in the center seam widened by a grain with every pulse of load shifting behind it.

This was real.

This was immediate.

This was people about to be crushed.

Jhace’s palms heated at once. The copper-gold glow flared under his skin and spilled into the wall’s stress lines. It traced the crack, then moved outward in branching paths, seeking the load-bearing points and reinforcing them with a force that made his teeth grit.

The wall’s bow slowed.

Not stopped. Slowed.

Jhace pressed harder, fingers splayed, both palms flat on the stone. The glow brightened. The vein-lines in his forearms darkened and extended. The tremor climbed into his elbows.

A sharp pain stabbed behind his eyes. He swallowed it and drove his intent deeper.

Hold.

He felt the structure respond. The crack stopped widening. The wall’s outward creep arrested by a fraction, then another.

But the wall was too wide for one pair of hands.

“Tiffani,” Jhace forced out. His voice was strained now. “Where.”

She did not hesitate. She ran her hand along the wall’s surface, eyes tracking the crack paths and the brace points. She did not touch his hands. She did not touch the glow. She stayed just outside it and read the geometry.

“Left brace is wrong,” she said. “The iron strap is seated off-center. Your glow is feeding a weak joint.”

Jhace’s teeth clenched harder. “Fix it.”

“I cannot,” she said. “Your hands are occupied and the wall is moving.”

She grabbed a nearby timber wedge from a brace pile, shoved it under the off-center strap’s anchor point, and kicked it into place with her boot heel. The strap shifted a fraction and seated deeper into the stone groove.

“Now,” she said. “Feed the strap line. Not the crack. The strap.”

Jhace forced his focus to follow her instruction. He did not understand the mechanism. He understood load. He understood where force had to go.

He moved the copper-gold flow, guided by her words, into the iron strap and its anchor bolts.

The strap glowed. The bolts glowed. The glow ran down into the stone around them, thickening the load path.

The wall’s bow reduced by a grain.

The crowd noise changed. People noticed. They fell silent in the way people did when they saw a structure decide not to kill them yet.

Jhace’s hands shook violently. Sweat ran down his wrists. The heat in his palms became pain. His vision narrowed. He held anyway.

The wall steadied. It held.

Jhace released the stone and staggered back a step. His fingers refused to unclench at first. He forced them open and saw that his hands were trembling so badly he could not hold them steady.

A brace worker shoved a nail into his palm. “Pin that timber,” the worker said. “Quick, before it shifts.”

Jhace reached for the hammer.

His hand closed around the hammer handle and slipped.

He tried again. His fingers did not obey. The tremor made the handle jump against his palm. The hammer fell and clanged on stone.

The brace worker stared at him, then at the vein-lines on his forearms.

Jhace swallowed. The bitter taste of metal flooded his mouth.

Tiffani stepped between him and the brace worker without raising her voice.

“He cannot,” she said. “Not after that. You need another set of hands.”

The brace worker hesitated, then ran off shouting for help.

Jhace stood there, breathing hard, staring at his own useless grip.

The wall behind him held steady, the crack arrested, the bracing now seated properly.

His hands shook.

Tiffani looked at his forearms again, then at the wall, then at her logbook.

She wrote one line.

Jhace watched her pen move and understood the shape of the problem with a clarity that hurt more than the tremor:

He could hold stone together.

He could not hold himself steady enough to pick up a hammer afterward.

The wall held.

("CHAPTER 5: Lines That Don’t Lie", """Tide 2–3 — Saint Merro: Glass Quarter corridors and junction parapet

Tiffani’s logbook gained weight in her hand without gaining pages.

She kept it dry anyway. Habit. Oilcloth wrap, tie twice, tuck into the inner pocket of her field satchel. Even with Soulpulse dust in the air, even with mortar grit everywhere, even with the city’s breath turning coarse after the Piercing, paper still mattered. Paper held numbers. Numbers held memory without needing permission.

The Kiln Corridor ran crooked through the Glass Quarter, ovens on one side, cracked stone on the other. Firehouse vents still exhaled when they could. Some were dead. Some were choked. The ones that worked made the corridor warmer than the lanes that fed into it, and warmth made people gather. Warmth made it harder to keep clear passages. Warmth made a load path more complicated.

Tiffani stopped at the first hold site of Tide 2, Kindling. A small archway that had already been shored twice. The crew had used timber braces and iron straps. The straps were too thin and too long; the timber was warped. The arch carried weight from a broken upper walk and redirected it into a corner abutment that had lost its footing when the ground rotated during the Piercing.

She put her palm on the stone and did not pretend it was calm.

Her fingers felt micro-vibration, a fine pulse that traveled through her wrist and up into her forearm. She steadied herself with the other hand on the strap and counted the cadence. Not a sound. A repeatable rhythm. Repeatable meant real.

She opened the logbook to the current section and wrote:

Hold Site 1 — Kiln Corridor / West bend / Oven 14 vent line  
Structure: arch + corner abutment  
Material: stone + mortar, iron strap add-on  
State: creep present, low amplitude, constant  
Human traffic: high, uncontrolled  
Response: additional brace recommended; traffic control required  
Note: baseline vibration increased by one subdivision since Tide 1, Quench

She underlined traffic control twice. Not because it would happen, but because she wanted it documented that it should have happened.

She moved down the corridor in measured Strides, stopping at each shift in the paving where the stone had split. She marked deviations on her corridor map: hairline fissures, buckled joints, slumped grout. She checked the same points she had checked during the first Tides after the Piercing, and she compared. Some cracks were stable. Some had widened by less than a finger’s breadth. Two had opened enough for grit to pour through when she tapped them with her knuckle.

When she reached the second hold site, she did not have to ask whose work it was.

The copper-gold stain was faint but visible in the stone. Not a dye. Not paint. A residue in the surface where heat and light had passed through fractured material and left the cracks cleaner than the surrounding dust. Tiffani knelt, wiped away grit with the side of her hand, and traced the path with her fingertip.

Stress lines. Real ones. The route was not random. The glow had flowed along the fracture network that carried load.

She wrote again.

Hold Site 2 — Kiln Corridor / Junction to Underface stair  
Structure: stair throat + supporting lintel  
State pre-hold: recorded sag by two subdivisions, Tide 1 late Quench  
State post-hold: stable within half subdivision, Tide 2 Kindling  
Indicator: copper-gold residue along primary fractures  
Hypothesis: activation follows immediate need, follows load path, prefers existing fracture routes

Failures mattered too. Failures defined the boundary.

By Tide 2 Pouring she had six hold sites mapped and three confirmed sites with copper-gold residue. She chose an observation position above the Saint Merro junction approaches, where the main walk sloped down toward an intersection of arches, columns, and ramps that carried people between the Crown’s inner districts and the Glass Quarter camps.

She aligned her glass-sight with a known stone mark on the far arch and checked baseline. The arch carried slightly low. The deviation was within one subdivision, but the direction was consistent with creep she had logged at other sites.

She waited.

When a line of refugees pushed through and the load increased, she recorded the increased vibration. When an aftershock ran under the stone and the arch responded with a groan that made the lens tremble, she recorded that too.

The man she was tracking arrived from the Underface stair at the junction’s left side.

Jhace Torrin moved with the gait of someone who had spent most of his life under stone. He watched the joints first, then the crowd, then the shoring. He spoke to the crew chief with minimal words. He carried a hammer but did not swing it. His right hand held the handle too tightly, and his fingers did not settle into a stable grip.

His tremor showed even at distance. Small. Persistent.

He set his palm against the arch base. Skin to stone. Contact. Intent. Need.

The copper-gold light did not flare theatrically. It moved.

Tiffani watched it through the glass-sight as it ran along existing fractures and into a joint that had begun to separate. The arch shifted upward by half a subdivision. Then it held.

Jhace pulled his hand away quickly, as if the stone had burned. His shoulders tightened. He hid the tremor by clenching his hands into fists and putting them against his thighs. The veins in his forearms stood out darker than they should, with faint lines that followed no normal pattern. Copper-gold lines under skin.

Cost. Visible. Physical.

Tiffani recorded:

Hold Event — Junction arch A  
Trigger context: crowd load + aftershock + audible joint movement  
Method: palm contact at arch base; no tool grip during activation  
Effect: immediate stabilization + lift of 0.5 subdivision  
Residue: visible line along fracture network; copper-gold  
User condition: tremor increased post-use; vein-lines visible; grip avoidance observed

The Resonance Regulation Office arrived in pairs, coats too clean for the dust, and asked for her records. She refused. She recorded the approach.

The Water-Guild factor Tellis arrived with barrels and an offer to condemn districts for “realism.” She refused again. She recorded that too.

Marisse found her at a hold site and named what Tiffani had been avoiding: Jhace was becoming a single point of failure.

Tiffani wrote one final line and made a decision she could feel in her ribs before she said it aloud.

Decision — Engage. Anchor the work to method before Jhace Torrin becomes the next collapse.

She tied the oilcloth wrap around the book, tightened it, and went to find his forge."""),
("CHAPTER 6: Weight That Won’t Yield", """Tide 4–6 — Junction throat, then Underface stair

Jhace felt the junction fail before he saw it.

The stone under his boots changed tone. Not sound. Weight. A shift in the way vibration traveled through the paving, a deepening pulse that did not match the crowd’s footfall.

A woman with a boy on her hip stared at him. Her eyes were dry and fixed. The boy’s face was turned inward, pressed to her shoulder.

A man with mortar on his forearms nodded. “Me. We’re set. We’ll add another brace.”

“We don’t have clamps,” the man said.

Jhace’s hands shook. The tremor never stopped now. It lived in his wrists.

Jhace looked back at the arch—alive for now, because they had made a margin.

("INTERLUDE I: Corlexi — System vs Chaos", """Tide ~6–7 — Crown administrative chambers

Corlexi entered the Crown’s record tier and felt the pressure of too many edges.

The corridor was straight. The walls were plumb. The floor was laid in repeating squares. None of that helped. The air carried voices and paper-dust and wet ink. Sound arrived in stacked layers, each petition read aloud over another, each stamp striking over another, each boot scuffing in a different cadence. Corlexi’s skull held it all and did not filter.

RESONANCE REPORTS.

The knee’s tremor transferring into the table’s surface. The tremor traveling into the ink lines.

Corlexi’s teeth tightened. He stepped to the stool, tapped the short leg with his toe, then pulled a thin wedge from his sleeve and slid it beneath the leg. He pressed down until the wobble ceased.

The recorder looked up, startled. “What—”

“Continue,” Corlexi said.

The recorder continued, hand steadier now. The ink line became straight again.

The grains did not lie. They traveled stress lines.

Not a glow. Not a flare. A subtle alignment. Tiny cracks no longer drew his eye. Their edges seated closer together. The dust grains shifted, then stopped.

Corlexi looked at him. “No.”

The overseer blinked. “You can’t refuse. This is Crown work.”

Corlexi scanned the room. He saw the grid of tables. The doors. The lines of people waiting with papers that represented food, shelter, timber allocations, burial permissions.

The system was trying to form around a force it did not understand. It would squeeze until the force fit.

It would also squeeze the people inside it.

Corlexi spoke without raising his voice. “You are building a cage. You are calling it order.”

The policy man’s jaw tightened. “Order keeps the city from falling.”

Corlexi looked toward the windows. The sky seam cut the pale light into unequal halves. The seam was straight. The world beneath it was not.

Order could not be declared. It had to be seated, joint by joint, under load.

He lifted his hand and left without looking back, carrying a conclusion clean and hard:

If they tried to force the world into rectangles, the world would break their rectangles."""),

("INTERLUDE II: Mobel — Purity Rising", """Tide ~15–16 — Entry-side coast

Mobel trusted pain.

Pain stripped error from the body. It drew attention to the exact place where failure began.

He knelt at the salt pans where stone trays stepped down toward the sea. The Piercing’s far tremor had shifted the basins. Seams opened. Brine leaked into soil and left crusted white lines that spread in branching paths.

Impurity traveled through openings.

Mobel pressed his thumb into a crack until the sharp edge bit skin. He welcomed the bite.

Behind him, voices rose. Two men dragged a third across grit and dropped him near the pans. The man convulsed, foam at his lips, hands clawing, wrists shaking in short pulses.

“He touched the gold-dust,” someone said.

“He tried to hold a wall with his hands,” another said.

Mobel crouched beside him and watched for pattern. Tremor, cough, breath hitch, wrong heat.

The man rasped, “Help.”

Mobel asked, “What did you take in?”

“I… breathed it,” the man said. “I thought it would make me strong.”

Strength without method produced waste. Waste produced collapse.

Mobel stood and spoke to the ring of onlookers. “Bring boiling brine. Bring clean cloth. No reused rags.”

The brine arrived steaming. Mobel took the vessel without cloth. Heat licked his palms. Pain confirmed control.

He poured a thin stream over the man’s wrists and scrubbed until copper-gold grains washed away into sand. The man screamed and thrashed. Mobel did not stop when he begged. Begging did not change contamination.

When convulsions eased, Mobel rinsed his hands in seawater. Salt stung. He welcomed it.

He looked at the seam on the horizon and felt pressures behind it—alignment, binding, loosening—none of them named, all of them real.

He carried a rule out of the crowd’s hungry expectation:

Clean first. Always."""),
("CHAPTER 11: Holding Together", """Tide 17–18 — Jhace’s forge beneath Saint Merro

("EPILOGUE: The Auditor — The Not-Room", """Tide 20 — Saint Merro / Underface

The Auditor entered the space that refused the name room.

Stone surrounded it. Underface carried weight above it. Heat mains ran near enough that a normal chamber would have held warmth in its joints. This space held none of that. Sound arrived dulled. Vibration arrived cut. The floor took the Auditor’s boot and gave nothing back.

A slate appeared in the Auditor’s hands and was set down where a table should have been. No surface declared itself. The slate remained set.

Three slates followed, placed in a line, edges aligned by fingertip distance. The alignment held. The place permitted record, not structure.

Copper-gold dust hung at the center. It did not fall. It did not rise. It gathered into thin planes and cords that mapped stress without material. The cords tightened, then separated into three impressions.

The first impression formed around ink, seals, and squared stone.

Corlexi moved through Crown chambers where tables sat in grids and stools sat wrong. The impression sharpened on the moments he corrected: a wobbling leg wedged, a ledger squared to an inlay, a doorframe touched. The touch shifted a crack’s edge tighter by a fraction and left no visible cost on skin. No tremor followed. No heat climbed. The act still changed the load behavior.

The second impression formed around salt, scalding steam, and hands that did not flinch.

Mobel stood at a coastal pan where stone trays had opened seams. He seated wedges with controlled taps. He poured boiling brine over a convulsing man’s wrists and scrubbed until copper-gold grains washed away. Pain registered in the body and did not interrupt the sequence. The crowd’s fear aligned behind him, reshaping into appetite for rule.

The third impression did not begin with place. It began with linkage.

Jhace Torrin’s hands trembled at rest. The tremor ran through fingers and into wrists, present even with empty palms. Faint copper-gold vein-lines remained visible under skin without contact. His body carried the invoice forward.

Beside him, Tiffani’s presence acted as constraint. She reduced waste. She narrowed his holds to true failure paths. She interrupted the caretaker compulsion that pushed him past safe limits. She did not erase cost. She made cost survivable.

The Auditor’s hand hovered above the first slate.

Then the voice changed, not as announcement, but as the natural point where observation became record.

I wrote the first line clean and short: Three vectors confirmed.

I kept the next lines anchored to what the world had shown, not what the world might mean.

Vector One — Seating. Corlexi’s mind sought hierarchy in joints and grids. He treated deviation as threat. He treated compliance as stability. He refused overt command once, and the refusal did not end the system’s hunger. It redirected it toward finding a more compliant hand.

Vector Two — Cleansing. Mobel’s mind sought correction through removal. He treated contamination as moral fact. He used pain as process control. A crowd accepted the sequence and began to mirror it. When fear becomes instruction, instruction becomes authority.

Vector Three — Bracing. Jhace and Tiffani formed a composite brace. Load redistributed between them. Their method reduced waste under activation and delayed failure. Their bond remained functional, not ornamental. Their refusal of ownership increased external pressure.

Above, Saint Merro held by timber and iron and exhausted hands. Aftershocks continued. Cracks that had been patched opened again along old planes.

The Guild tightened route control. Water moved under guard. Tools moved with stamped permission. Supply became leverage.

The Church gathered language. Language became ritual. Ritual became claim.

The Crown formed new offices with new doors and new seals. Lists became mandates. Mandates reached for bodies that could be named, tracked, contained.

None of those structures cared about the true mechanic behind copper-gold holds. They cared that the holds worked often enough to be believed. Belief produced demand. Demand produced pressure.

I returned my attention to the third impression.

Jhace’s trajectory had already bent. Stage 1 baseline remained visible in tremor and vein-lines. Warning signs had appeared during mass-load events: overheating skin, blood at the nostril, sensory overload edging in when scale exceeded method.

Tiffani would slow that bend. She would not stop it. The cost model did not allow clean victory. It allowed only management, triage, and refusal to waste.

Unpredictability remained threat and asset.

A registered hand could be scheduled. A leased hand could be deployed. A feared hand could be erased.

An unclaimed hand could not be cleanly used.

I wrote the last section as prediction without specifics, staying inside the gate.

Near-term outcomes:
- Increased attempts to capture method through policy, supply, and theology.
- Increased untrained grasping by civilians, driven by rumor and fear.
- Increased pressure on the composite brace to submit, relocate, or vanish.
- Increased probability of cost escalation events when mass-load crises recur.

Before I left, I drew one mark beneath the text: three lines meeting at a joint, each line carrying load in a different direction.

The copper-gold dust planes broke apart into drifting grains. The grains remained suspended, waiting for new patterns to map.

I stepped back into the Underface where heat returned, where vibration returned, where the city’s weight returned all at once.

Saint Merro continued to set under load."""),
])

# Set default font
style = doc.styles['Normal']
style.font.name = 'Garamond'
style._element.rPr.rFonts.set(qn('w:eastAsia'), 'Garamond')
style.font.size = Pt(11)

# Title page
title = doc.add_paragraph("CONFLUENCE CHRONICLES")
title.runs[0].bold = True
title.runs[0].font.size = Pt(20)
title.alignment = WD_ALIGN_PARAGRAPH.CENTER

subtitle = doc.add_paragraph("Novella N01 — Draft Manuscript")
subtitle.runs[0].italic = True
subtitle.runs[0].font.size = Pt(14)
subtitle.alignment = WD_ALIGN_PARAGRAPH.CENTER

doc.add_paragraph("Te’Oga — Saint Merro").alignment = WD_ALIGN_PARAGRAPH.CENTER

doc.add_page_break()

# Helper to add section
def add_section(doc, heading, body):
    h = doc.add_paragraph(heading)
    h.style = doc.styles['Heading 1']
    for para in body.split("\n\n"):
        p = doc.add_paragraph(para.strip())
        p.style = doc.styles['Normal']

for heading, body in manuscript:
    add_section(doc, heading, body)
    doc.add_page_break()

out_path = "/mnt/data/N01_Draft_Manuscript.docx"
doc.save(out_path)

out_path

[ASSISTANT]
import re
from pathlib import Path

# rebuild without filecite markers
def strip_cites(s):
    return re.sub(r"filecite[^]+", "", s)

# We'll load the doc creation function but adjust relevant strings by stripping cite markers at the end.
# Easiest: open the file we created and just inform user? Better to regenerate clean.
from docx import Document
from docx.shared import Pt
from docx.enum.text import WD_ALIGN_PARAGRAPH
from docx.oxml.ns import qn

def build_clean_manuscript():
    doc = Document()
    style = doc.styles['Normal']
    style.font.name = 'Times New Roman'
    style._element.rPr.rFonts.set(qn('w:eastAsia'), 'Times New Roman')
    style.font.size = Pt(11)

    def add_title(text):
        p = doc.add_paragraph()
        run = p.add_run(text)
        run.bold = True
        run.font.size = Pt(18)
        p.alignment = WD_ALIGN_PARAGRAPH.CENTER

    def add_subtitle(text):
        p = doc.add_paragraph()
        run = p.add_run(text)
        run.italic = True
        run.font.size = Pt(12)
        p.alignment = WD_ALIGN_PARAGRAPH.CENTER

    def add_seg(title, body, break_after=True):
        doc.add_heading(title, level=1)
        body = strip_cites(body)
        for para in body.strip().split("\n\n"):
            doc.add_paragraph(para.strip())
        if break_after:
            doc.add_page_break()

    add_title("N01 — Glass Under Load")
    add_subtitle("Confluence Chronicles — Novella 01 Manuscript (compiled)")
    doc.add_page_break()

    # reuse the same text from previous function by re-importing from the global namespace via build_manuscript? Not accessible.
    # We'll quickly read the strings out of the previous .docx? That's hard.
    # Instead, we will re-run the same build_manuscript code block content by copying from notebook history is not available.
    # We'll take the pragmatic route: duplicate by calling build_manuscript() and then stripping cites from paragraphs is easier.
    return doc

# We'll modify the existing FULL doc by removing citation markers from runs.
src = Path("/mnt/data/N01_Manuscript_Compiled_FULL.docx")
doc = Document(src)
pattern = re.compile(r"filecite[^]+")
for para in doc.paragraphs:
    if pattern.search(para.text):
        # Replace text in whole paragraph (simple)
        para.text = pattern.sub("", para.text).replace("  ", " ").strip()
out = Path("/mnt/data/N01_Manuscript_Compiled_FULL_CLEAN.docx")
doc.save(out)
out.exists(), out.stat().st_size

from pathlib import Path
import re, textwrap

full_text = """# Confluence Chronicles — N01 (GLASS)
## Draft Manuscript (Compiled)

---

## CHAPTER 1: Something Wrong

*Tide 0, Kindling — Underface beneath Saint Merro*

Jhace took the service hatch by touch before he took it by sight.

The iron ring sat cold against his palm. The ladder down breathed heat up at him through the first rungs—warm air from the mains, damp stone behind it, the faint bitter edge of kiln smoke that always hung under Saint Merro. He dropped his weight onto the first rung, tested for flex, then went down in a controlled rhythm.

Boot. Rung. Boot. Rung.

At six spans below the hatch, the sound changed. The city’s surface noise thinned until it became vibration in the rock. Underface had its own voice: the steady thrum of the heat-grid, the low chatter of risers, the occasional ring of a bracket giving a warning note to anyone trained to hear it.

Jhace kept one hand on the ladder and the other along the stone wall. His fingers found the mortar seams. His palm found the temperature. Cool stone, then a warm stripe, then cool again. The warm stripe meant the main ran behind that section, tight to the wall and properly seated.

“Still crawling around down there,” Bram had said at the forge door, without looking up from the quench bucket. “A man could take a Kindling to eat and talk.”

Jhace had taken the ledger anyway. He had taken his tools. He had taken the Underface route because the Underface did not ask questions and did not waste words.

Bram had watched him set the strap across his shoulder and had made a sound that was not approval and not dismissal.

“Work first,” Jhace had said. It was what Bram expected from him. It was what the tunnels expected from him too.

He stepped off the ladder onto the lower platform. The service way ran along the main line, iron grates bolted to stone brackets, a narrow channel of runoff on the far side where condensation dripped from pipe joints and ran toward the sump.

Jhace stood still and let the floor speak.

He counted pulses through the soles of his boots. Not numbers, not formal. The pattern lived in his body. Underface always carried a rhythm—heat flow, water flow, load settling. He knew it the way he knew the pressure of a hammer head in his grip.

The rhythm was off.

Not broken. Not loud. Off.

He crouched and put his palm on the grate. The iron was warmer than it should have been at Kindling. That alone could happen if an upper oven had vented early, if a line had been opened too hard, if a crew had flushed slag into the wrong channel. He could explain it.

The vibration could not be explained that easily.

It pressed up through the grate in a slightly altered cadence. Not stronger. Different. The thrum had a thin edge to it, a faint second note braided under the main tone.

Jhace shifted his palm from iron to stone. The stone carried the same double-note.

He stood and walked.

He did not hurry. Hurrying in Underface got people hurt. It got ankles caught in grates, heads cracked on low beams, shoulders torn on protruding bolts. Hurrying also made you miss the first signs of failure.

He moved toward Junction S-9, where the main line split into two risers for the upper rim platforms. He had marked it last Tide with chalk: a bracket that had started to chatter. He expected to hear that chatter again and decide whether it needed a full pull and reseat.

The bracket was quiet.

Jhace frowned. Quiet could mean resolved. Quiet could mean the bolt had settled into place. Quiet could mean it had cracked and stopped singing because it had nothing left to carry.

He knelt and tapped the iron with the back of his knuckles. The tone came back dull. He tapped the stone anchor point. That tone came back clean.

He pulled the chalk ledger free and flipped to the last entry. The chalk mark was there, his hand’s rough block letters noting: S-9 bracket chatter. No bow. Heat even. Watch next Tide.

He set his fingers on the bracket and felt for movement.

There was none.

The bolt head sat flush. The washer was seated. The iron was tight.

It should have reassured him.

It did not.

The wrongness in the thrum continued even with the bracket stable. That meant the source was not local failure. It meant the system itself had shifted.

Jhace stood and followed the main farther in.

The service way narrowed. The ceiling dropped. Stone beams crossed overhead, each one marked with paint and old scratches that recorded who had last braced it. The paint was flaking. The scratches were mostly Torrin hands and Kethis hands and hands from crews that no longer worked this section.

He walked past a drip that struck the grate with a steady tick-tick-tick. The tick did not match the vibration from below.

The difference made his teeth tighten.

He reached a junction where the main line passed through a stone collar and into a reinforced tunnel. The collar was new stone—recent work after the last settlement shift. Jhace had helped set it. He remembered the weight of that stone block and the way his shoulders had burned after hauling it into place.

He put his palm on the collar.

The stone was warm.

Stone should not be warm here unless the main behind it was leaking heat. He pressed his ear to it. He listened for hiss. There was none. He slid his palm along the seam, searching for hot spots. The warmth was even.

Even warmth meant not a leak. It meant the heat was present in the stone itself, distributed through the collar as though the rock had been part of the line.

Jhace drew back, stared at the collar, then stared at his own hand. No soot. No wetness. No sign he had touched a hot metal plate by mistake.

He set his fingers against the seam and counted the vibration again. The double-note persisted. It held at the seam. It continued into the reinforced tunnel.

He straightened and took a slow breath through his nose. Damp air. Iron smell. Old ash. He tasted nothing unusual. That meant he was not breathing new fumes. That was good.

He marked the collar with chalk anyway. A clean line, then a second line, the signal for “return with tools and another set of hands.”

He did not like returning with another set of hands.

Bram always said he worked alone because he did not want to owe anyone explanations. Bram was mostly right.

Jhace continued forward.

The reinforced tunnel had been built to carry load from the upper rim. Thick stone arches, iron ribs bolted into them, pipe hangers spaced at precise reaches. It was one of the few places in Underface that felt overly controlled.

It also had a voice. A steady, heavy resonance that usually made Jhace’s ribs relax because it meant the structure was doing its job.

The resonance now carried the thin second note too.

He stopped beneath the third arch and put his palm against the iron rib. The vibration crawled up into his forearm. He felt it in the bones near his elbow.

His skin prickled.

He did not name that as fear. He named it as signal.

He pulled his hand away and flexed his fingers. The joints moved smoothly. Good. No injury. He could still hold a hammer. He could still tighten a bolt. He could still do work.

He walked to the fourth arch. The fifth.

At the sixth arch, the floor shivered.

It was not the normal settlement tremor—those came as a slow roll, stone dragging against stone, a long groan that traveled through the Underface in a predictable way. This shiver was sharp. It was a quick bite of motion, a brief tilt that made his balance adjust a fraction too late.

His gut rose.

He grabbed the iron rib by reflex, fingers locking hard. The iron was solid. The arch did not move.

The floor beneath his boots still did.

For a breath, gravity felt wrong. Not gone. Shifted.

Jhace tightened his grip until his knuckles ached. He forced his breathing to slow. He waited for the tremor to pass.

It did pass. The floor settled back into its usual angle. The Underface’s thrum returned to its baseline pressure.

Except the thin second note remained.

Jhace released the rib. His fingers left a faint print of sweat on the iron. He wiped his palm on his trousers and looked down the tunnel toward the junction that led up toward the rim.

The wrongness was moving through the system. It was not isolated to one bracket or one pipe.

He had to tell Bram.

He did not want to tell Bram.

He turned toward the ladder that led to the upper service corridor, intending to cut across and climb out near the forge.

The floor shivered again.

This time the shiver ran in the same direction as the thin second note.

Upward.

Jhace stopped and listened, and the Underface answered with a resonance he had never heard in his life.

He started toward the ladder anyway, because whatever was changing under Saint Merro was also changing above it.

And the surface had more people to crush.

---

## CHAPTER 2: Glass That Listens

*Tide 0, Pouring — Observatory platform at Saint Merro’s rim*

Pella set the scope’s stand feet into the stone sockets and checked the seating twice.

He did not trust the first confirmation. The rim wind did not scream here, but it had weight, and weight shifted instruments by grains that mattered. The platform itself had a mild vibration that came from the city’s ovens and the Underface mains beneath. Most observers ignored it. Pella did not.

He leaned into the tube, aligned the hairlines on the reference mark, and took the baseline.

The mark sat where it should.

He wrote the value anyway.

Marisse stood behind him with her hands clasped tight against her own wrists. She kept her boots spread in a stance that would hold through a tremor. Her eyes were on the sky, not on the log.

“You are early,” Pella said, without turning.

“I do not sleep well at Pouring,” Marisse said.

Pella knew the story. He knew it in the way people knew stories in Saint Merro: without pressing for details, because details carried weight and could crack a person’s careful balance.

Twenty-three dead in a prior catastrophe, long before this Tide. Not a war. Not an outside raid. Just a failure in stone and load and decision-making that ended with bodies in rubble. Marisse had survived. That survival had sharpened her until she could hear a fault line in someone’s footsteps.

Pella adjusted the scope by a small turn and took the second baseline.

The mark still sat clean.

He recorded it. He turned the page and began the sequence.

A standard sweep: rim to Crown line, Crown line to upper arc, upper arc back down. The purpose was routine. Verify the air’s clarity. Verify refraction. Verify that the glass plates in the scope were still true.

The first sweep brought him to a smear.

Not in the scope glass. Not a scratch. A distortion in the sky itself, faint and pale, a thin band that cut across the upper arc.

Pella blinked. He lifted his face from the tube and looked with naked eyes.

The smear was not visible that way.

He put his eye back to the tube and checked the smear again.

It was there.

He loosened the tube’s clamp, nudged the angle, then retightened it. He took the reading again.

The smear remained. It did not shift with the instrument.

“Mark?” Marisse asked.

Pella kept his voice level. “A deviation in refraction.”

“Noise,” Marisse said, but the word came out flat. It did not sound like dismissal. It sounded like a test.

Pella wrote the number. He wrote a second number beside it, the baseline plus the deviation. He wrote a third note: persistent?

He ran the sweep again.

The smear appeared in the same position. The deviation held at the same range across the same arc.

Not noise.

Pella’s tongue tasted faintly of metal. He did not react outwardly. He made the small internal adjustment that always came with risk: widen the set of possibilities.

He looked along the platform’s edge at the other equipment: mirror stands, glass plates set in frames, a string of small signal lenses used to direct light down to the lower chambers. The signal lenses had begun to vibrate.

A fine vibration. Not enough to rattle. Enough to make the lens edges whisper against their frames.

Pella walked to the nearest lens and set his fingers against the frame. The vibration traveled into his hand.

He returned to the scope and took another measurement.

The deviation increased by a small step.

He wrote it down.

Marisse moved closer. She kept her stance wide. Her eyes were on the sky again.

“It is growing,” she said.

Pella did not say yes yet. He took the sweep again. The deviation held at the higher value. Repeatable.

He did not call it noise now. He did not even call it anomaly. He called new state and wrote the phrase in the margin.

Marisse’s breath was controlled. Pella could hear it because the platform was quiet. That quiet did not feel stable. It felt thin.

A tremor passed through the rim. Sharp enough to make the scope’s tube twitch in its clamp.

Pella caught the stand with one hand and steadied the tube with the other. He waited for the shiver to stop, then checked alignment again.

The hairline mark had drifted by a fraction.

He tightened the clamp. He re-aligned. He took the reading again.

The deviation had increased again.

Marisse’s hands flexed at her wrists, fingers spreading and closing once, then locking again.

“I have seen this pattern,” she said. “Small shifts first. Then a break.”

Pella did not look away from the scope. “This is not stone.”

“No,” Marisse said. “But it is still load. It is still pressure. It is still people trusting a baseline.”

Pella wrote the number. He wrote the number twice. He underlined it.

He tried a different check: he rotated the scope’s glass plate, changed the internal alignment, then took the sweep again.

The smear did not rotate.

Marisse stepped to the platform edge and looked outward over Saint Merro. Everything looked normal from this height.

Everything felt wrong through the instruments.

Pella finished the sweep and wrote a final note at the bottom of the page: cause external.

He capped his pen and held still for a breath.

He heard a sound then, faint and thin, from the signal lenses.

A hum.

Glass humming against its frame. Not from wind.

Pella walked to the lens frame and put his palm against the glass.

The hum rose into his skin. It was steady. It was patterned. It carried the same thin second note he had seen in the sky.

He took his hand away and looked up.

The smear had become a line.

A pale line stretched across the upper arc, clean and sharp, a white seam cut into the air itself.

The platform shuddered again, stronger, and Pella’s logbook slid across the stone.

Marisse said one word, low and certain.

“Now.”

---

## CHAPTER 3: The Sky Breaks

*Tide 0, Striking — Underface → street level → surface*

The wall moved.

Jhace saw it in the corner of his vision and felt it through the floor before he could turn his head. Stone that had held for many Anneals shifted sideways by a fraction of a span. That fraction was enough.

The arch above him groaned. The iron rib protested with a high ring. Dust shed from the mortar seam in a sudden thick fall, striking his shoulders and filling his mouth with grit.

He dropped his weight toward the safer side of the service way. He reached for the nearest rib.

The floor tilted harder. Gravity changed angle in a way that made his inner balance seize.

Something deep below gave a sound that belonged to no bracket and no pipe. It was too large. It was a long tearing resonance, stone under stress beyond tolerance.

Jhace lunged for the ladder up.

The ladder’s first rung jerked under his hand as the wall shifted again. He missed the rung and slammed his palm into stone.

Pain flashed through his wrist. He ignored it. He tried again. His fingers caught iron and held.

A second groan ran through the structure. The arch overhead shed another curtain of dust. The iron rib flexed. He could feel the flex in his grip.

“Up,” he said to himself. “Up.”

He climbed two rungs before the impact came.

A hit that knocked his boots off the rungs and threw his body sideways into the wall. His shoulder struck stone hard enough to jar his teeth. He clung to the ladder with one hand and scraped for purchase with the other.

The Underface went loud.

Pipes shrieked. The main line thumped against stone collars. The iron rib’s ring became a sustained scream.

Jhace forced himself down instead of up. Down meant space.

He dropped from the ladder onto the service grate and ran back toward the wider junction, toward the collar he had marked.

The corridor behind him cracked.

A block the size of his torso broke loose from the arch and slammed onto the grate, warping iron bars and sending a shock through his legs.

He jumped the broken section, landed hard, and stumbled. His boot caught a raised bolt head. He went down on one knee, hand slapping the grate.

Heat pulsed up through the iron into his palm.

Not normal heat. A sudden flare that made him yank his hand back with a hiss.

He stared at his palm.

No burn mark. Just heat under the skin, deep and bright, as though his nerves had become a conduit.

A human cry cut through the Underface noise. High. Close.

Jhace pivoted and ran toward it.

A side passage had partially collapsed. A girl lay pinned under a fallen beam that had dropped from the ceiling brace. The beam pressed across her lower legs and trapped her against the grate.

Her face was white with dust. Her hair was stuck to her cheeks with blood.

She saw him and tried to pull herself back. The beam did not move.

Jhace dropped beside her.

“Do not move,” he said. “Where are you hurt?”

“My leg,” she said. “Both.”

Blood ran in thick lines down her ankle. The weight crushed her other leg at the knee joint. He could see the angle of bone under skin.

The beam had wedged against the sidewall. Another wall shift could slide it deeper onto her legs and crush her fully.

Jhace grabbed the beam with both hands and tried to lift.

It did not lift.

He needed a pry bar. A wedge. Time.

He had none.

The count came: twenty-three bodies. Two with his name.

He pressed his palms harder into the beam and drove intent into the material with everything he had.

Hold.

Heat in his hands flared. Copper-gold light ignited under his palms and surged into the beam’s iron wrap, tracing stress lines where weight bit deepest. The glow crawled outward in branching paths, mapping load distribution, then enforcing a new distribution.

The beam shifted.

Sideways. A controlled slide against the wall, one finger-width at a time.

The girl screamed. Jhace barked, “Stay still,” and kept his palms pressed.

The glow intensified. It spread into the sidewall collar too, outlining cracks under dust. The cracks did not widen. They held.

Jhace’s forearms began to shake. The tremor started at his wrists and climbed. His fingers spasmed once, then locked again.

His palms burned from the inside.

He pushed anyway.

The beam slid another fraction. Pressure eased enough for the girl to drag one ankle free.

“Pull,” Jhace said. “Pull now.”

She clawed backward.

The beam tried to settle back under its own weight.

Jhace drove the glow harder into the stress lines. The grate beneath stiffened. The beam’s iron wrap tightened.

The girl’s second leg came free with a wet sound that made Jhace’s stomach lurch.

He grabbed her under the shoulders and hauled her away from the collapse zone, dragging her across the grate toward the wider junction.

Behind them, the brace groaned and settled. It did not fall fully. Not yet.

Jhace’s hands shook violently now. Vein-lines lit faintly along his forearms, following the same branching pattern the glow had traced in the iron.

The tunnel began to fail. Sidewall bulged. Arch cracked. A slab dropped and smashed through the grate.

Jhace kept dragging the girl.

He reached the ladder and shoved the girl onto the first rungs. She could not climb. He climbed behind and pushed her upward, one rung at a time.

They reached the hatch. Surface light hit his face. Air full of dust and smoke. Stone breaking everywhere. Glass panes shattering.

Jhace dragged the girl into the open street space between two supports, the safest place he could reach.

He looked up.

A white line split the sky.

It cut across the air in a clean seam, bright enough to hurt his eyes. Light warped around it.

Footsteps behind him—fast, controlled. Not panicked.

A person crouched beside the girl, looked at her legs, then at Jhace’s hands.

Her gaze fixed on the vein-lines on his forearms. Copper-gold residue still glimmered faintly at his palms.

“Did you do that,” she said, not accusing—classifying.

Jhace tried to answer. His mouth did not form sound.

The stranger’s eyes flicked to a cracked support wall and back to his hands.

“Then you can do it again,” she said. “Not everywhere. Here. With intent. When it is real.”

The street shuddered.

Jhace swallowed grit and forced air into his lungs.

He looked at the failing wall. He looked at the bleeding girl. He looked at the stranger who had already turned his collapse into a question.

---

## CHAPTER 4: After the Shudder

*Tide 0, Quench — Saint Merro lower streets and triage wall*

Jhace woke with his hands already shaking.

He sat up too fast and paid for it immediately. His head spun. His stomach clenched. Dust coated his tongue. He coughed until his throat went raw.

He was on a low pallet in a corridor that smelled of wet stone and burned glass. People lay on either side of him—some groaning, some silent, some staring at the ceiling with eyes that did not blink.

A triage corridor. Not the forge. Not safety.

His forearms were bare. The vein-lines were still there, dark and branching, running from wrist to elbow.

He watched the tremor. Fine vibration in his fingers. Worse in his right hand. He tried to close his fist. The last two fingers lagged. He forced them down anyway.

Work first.

He stood. His knees threatened to fold. He steadied against the wall and counted breaths until the dizziness reduced enough to move.

Outside, the sky seam was still there, permanent in its wrongness.

Jhace crouched by a shallow crack in an alley wall and put his palm on it. He tried to summon the copper-gold glow.

Nothing happened.

He tried again. A small pulse of heat flared and died, leaving a sharp ache up his forearm. The tremor spiked.

So it was not a tool he could pick up on command.

The stranger’s words returned: When it is real.

He turned and nearly collided with her.

She held a logbook tight under one arm. Her eyes went to the crack he had touched.

“You tested,” she said.

“It failed,” Jhace said.

“It should,” she replied. “No immediate threat. No protective need. No trigger state.”

He stared. “You speak as though you have written rules.”

“I record what repeats,” she said. “Rules come later.”

“Where is the girl?”

“Inside. Triage corridor.”

A shout rose behind them: “Wall! The triage wall is going!”

Jhace ran with Tiffani at his side.

A long retaining wall held back a slope of broken stone. It had been cracked during Striking and braced hastily. It was now bowing outward under rubble weight. Healers worked along its base. If it failed, debris would bury them.

Jhace pushed through the crowd and put his hands on the wall.

This was real. Immediate. People about to be crushed.

Copper-gold glow spilled into the wall’s stress lines. It traced the crack, then branched outward. The bow slowed.

Jhace pressed harder. The glow brightened. Vein-lines darkened and extended. Tremor climbed into his elbows. Pain stabbed behind his eyes. He held anyway.

The wall steadied, but it was too wide for one pair of hands.

Tiffani ran her hand along the wall, reading geometry.

“Left brace is wrong,” she said. “Your glow is feeding a weak joint.”

“Fix it.”

“I cannot,” she said. “Your hands are occupied.”

She shoved a timber wedge under the off-center strap’s anchor point and kicked it into place. The strap shifted and seated deeper.

“Now,” she said. “Feed the strap line. Not the crack. The strap.”

Jhace forced the flow into the iron strap and anchor bolts. Strap and bolts glowed. Load path thickened.

The wall’s bow reduced by a grain.

Jhace released and staggered back. His fingers refused to unclench at first. He forced them open and saw his hands trembling too badly to hold steady.

A brace worker shoved a nail into his palm. “Pin that timber.”

Jhace reached for the hammer. His hand closed and slipped. The hammer fell.

Tiffani stepped in. “He cannot. Not after that. You need another set of hands.”

The brace worker ran for help.

Jhace stared at his useless grip.

The wall held.

His grip did not.

ch5 = r"""
---

## CHAPTER 5: Lines That Don’t Lie  
*Tide 2–3 — Saint Merro: Glass Quarter corridors and junction parapet*

Tiffani’s logbook gained weight in her hand without gaining pages.

She kept it dry anyway. Habit. Oilcloth wrap, tie twice, tuck into the inner pocket of her field satchel. Even with Soulpulse dust in the air, even with mortar grit everywhere, even with the city’s breath turning coarse after the Piercing, paper still mattered. Paper held numbers. Numbers held memory without needing permission.

The Kiln Corridor ran crooked through the Glass Quarter, ovens on one side, cracked stone on the other. Firehouse vents still exhaled when they could. Some were dead. Some were choked. The ones that worked made the corridor warmer than the lanes that fed into it, and warmth made people gather. Warmth made it harder to keep clear passages. Warmth made a load path more complicated.

Tiffani stopped at the first hold site of Tide 2, Kindling. A small archway that had already been shored twice. The crew had used timber braces and iron straps. The straps were too thin and too long; the timber was warped. The arch carried weight from a broken upper walk and redirected it into a corner abutment that had lost its footing when the ground rotated during the Piercing.

She put her palm on the stone and did not pretend it was calm.

Her fingers felt micro-vibration, a fine pulse that traveled through her wrist and up into her forearm. She steadied herself with the other hand on the strap and counted the cadence. Not a sound. A repeatable rhythm. Repeatable meant real.

She opened the logbook to the current section and wrote:

Hold Site 1 — Kiln Corridor / West bend / Oven 14 vent line  
Structure: arch + corner abutment  
Material: stone + mortar, iron strap add-on  
State: creep present, low amplitude, constant  
Human traffic: high, uncontrolled  
Response: additional brace recommended; traffic control required  
Note: baseline vibration increased by one subdivision since Tide 1, Quench

She underlined traffic control twice. Not because it would happen, but because she wanted it documented that it should have happened.

She moved down the corridor in measured Strides, stopping at each shift in the paving where the stone had split. She marked deviations on her corridor map: hairline fissures, buckled joints, slumped grout. She checked the same points she had checked during the first Tides after the Piercing, and she compared. Some cracks were stable. Some had widened by less than a finger’s breadth. Two had opened enough for grit to pour through when she tapped them with her knuckle.

When she reached the second hold site, she did not have to ask whose work it was.

The copper-gold stain was faint but visible in the stone. Not a dye. Not paint. A residue in the surface where heat and light had passed through fractured material and left the cracks cleaner than the surrounding dust. Tiffani knelt, wiped away grit with the side of her hand, and traced the path with her fingertip.

Stress lines. Real ones. The route was not random. The glow had flowed along the fracture network that carried load.

She wrote again.

Hold Site 2 — Kiln Corridor / Junction to Underface stair  
Structure: stair throat + supporting lintel  
State pre-hold: recorded sag by two subdivisions, Tide 1 late Quench  
State post-hold: stable within half subdivision, Tide 2 Kindling  
Indicator: copper-gold residue along primary fractures  
Hypothesis: activation follows immediate need, follows load path, prefers existing fracture routes

Failures mattered too. Failures defined the boundary.

By Tide 2 Pouring she had six hold sites mapped and three confirmed sites with copper-gold residue. She chose an observation position above the Saint Merro junction approaches, where the main walk sloped down toward an intersection of arches, columns, and ramps that carried people between the Crown’s inner districts and the Glass Quarter camps.

She aligned her glass-sight with a known stone mark on the far arch and checked baseline. The arch carried slightly low. The deviation was within one subdivision, but the direction was consistent with creep she had logged at other sites.

She waited.

When a line of refugees pushed through and the load increased, she recorded the increased vibration. When an aftershock ran under the stone and the arch responded with a groan that made the lens tremble, she recorded that too.

The man she was tracking arrived from the Underface stair at the junction’s left side.

Jhace Torrin moved with the gait of someone who had spent most of his life under stone. He watched the joints first, then the crowd, then the shoring. He spoke to the crew chief with minimal words. He carried a hammer but did not swing it. His right hand held the handle too tightly, and his fingers did not settle into a stable grip.

His tremor showed even at distance. Small. Persistent.

He set his palm against the arch base. Skin to stone. Contact. Intent. Need.

The copper-gold light did not flare theatrically. It moved.

Tiffani watched it through the glass-sight as it ran along existing fractures and into a joint that had begun to separate. The arch shifted upward by half a subdivision. Then it held.

Jhace pulled his hand away quickly, as if the stone had burned. His shoulders tightened. He hid the tremor by clenching his hands into fists and putting them against his thighs. The veins in his forearms stood out darker than they should, with faint lines that followed no normal pattern. Copper-gold lines under skin.

Cost. Visible. Physical.

Tiffani recorded:

Hold Event — Junction arch A  
Trigger context: crowd load + aftershock + audible joint movement  
Method: palm contact at arch base; no tool grip during activation  
Effect: immediate stabilization + lift of 0.5 subdivision  
Residue: visible line along fracture network; copper-gold  
User condition: tremor increased post-use; vein-lines visible; grip avoidance observed

The Resonance Regulation Office arrived in pairs, coats too clean for the dust, and asked for her records.

“You are the observatory recorder,” the taller one said, speaking as though that title meant obligation. “We require copies.”

Tiffani did not close her logbook. She held it against her ribs. “You can require anything,” she said. “You cannot have it.”

Their eyes went to Jhace as he leaned against the parapet, forearms held close, hands flexing as if he could shake the tremor out. His gaze stayed on the arch. He did not look at the officers.

“You are interfering with Crown safety,” the officer said.

“I am collecting data,” Tiffani replied. “Safety without understanding is theatre.”

The other officer’s jaw tightened. “We have an Office now,” he said. “We regulate resonance incidents.”

Tiffani wrote their names in her margin without asking. The taller officer noticed and flinched. He hated being recorded.

“We will return,” he said.

“Do,” Tiffani said, and watched them leave with brittle discipline on their faces.

When they were gone, Jhace spoke without turning his head. “You made them angry.”

“They were already angry,” Tiffani said. “I just gave them a target.”

Jhace’s hands shook. He tried to pick up his hammer. His grip failed and the handle slid a fraction. He caught it with his left hand, then clenched both fists as if fists were safer than tools.

Tiffani watched the motion and wrote a note: post-use fine motor failure. Stage 1 baseline?

She did not show him the words.

She moved her glass-sight to the sky seam and took a reading. The seam bent light in a way that made her stomach tighten. She wrote the deviation and underlined it. A line in the sky that behaved like a material boundary.

“Marisse,” Pella had said earlier when the first readings spiked. “She’d call it load.”

Tiffani had laughed then, once, sharp. “Everything is load,” she’d said.

Now she believed it. Now she believed the sky itself was part of the structure.

She packed her instruments and followed Jhace down the ramp. She kept to his pace. She did not offer to help him carry the hammer. He would have refused.

At the foot of the Underface stair, she finally said what her notes demanded.

“You can’t keep doing it like this,” she said. “Your cost curve is accelerating.”

Jhace looked at her then, eyes raw from dust and strain. “People die if I don’t.”

“People die if you do,” Tiffani said, and hated how true it sounded.

He stared past her at the junction—crowd movement, brace crews, cracks waiting. “Then what,” he asked.

Tiffani tightened her grip on her satchel strap. She made the decision her logbook had already written.

“Then we turn your instincts into method,” she said. “Before the city spends you.”
"""
out.write_text(out.read_text(encoding="utf-8") + ch5, encoding="utf-8")
out.stat().st_size

ch6 = r"""
---

## CHAPTER 6: Weight That Won’t Yield  
*Tide 4–6 — Junction throat, then Underface stair*

Jhace felt the junction fail before he saw it.

The stone under his boots changed tone. Not sound. Weight. A shift in the way vibration traveled through the paving, a deepening pulse that did not match the crowd’s footfall.

A runner grabbed his sleeve near the Underface stair throat. “Torrin. Arch A. It’s moving.”

Jhace didn’t ask which arch. He pushed through bodies, kept his shoulder low, and moved toward the main intersection where arches met ramps and the crowd funneled into a single passage.

He saw it then. The arch’s crown had dropped. Not far. Less than a hand’s breadth. Enough to change the load path.

A crew had already thrown up timber shoring. Two vertical posts, one diagonal brace, wedges driven hard. It was the right shape for the wrong situation. The timber bowed under compressive load it wasn’t sized for. The wedges had begun to spit out dust.

“Back,” Jhace said to the closest cluster of refugees.

No one moved.

Jhace lifted his hammer and pointed at the base of the arch. “That joint goes, this whole throat goes. Move.”

A woman with a boy on her hip stared at him. Her eyes were dry and fixed. The boy’s face was turned inward, pressed to her shoulder.

Jhace swallowed and stepped past her, closer to the shoring crew.

“Who’s lead?” he asked.

A man with mortar on his forearms nodded. “Me. We’re set. We’ll add another brace.”

“Your braces are too long,” Jhace said. “They’ll bow and kick out. You need shorter spans under the crown and a clamp at the base.”

“We don’t have clamps,” the man said.

Traditional shoring was not going to outrun the failure.

Jhace’s hands shook. The tremor never stopped now. It lived in his wrists.

He set the hammer down because he did not trust his grip.

“Do the thing,” the runner said. “Do the glow.”

Jhace hated that the city had already made it into a request.

He stepped toward the arch base and reached out.

“Wait.”

The voice cut through the junction noise with precision, not volume.

Tiffani moved down the ramp with measured Strides, stopped near the base, and knelt. She snapped chalk lines along a visible crack, then tapped the opening joint.

“Primary fracture network,” she said. “The load is not centered. It is traveling along this route.”

She looked up at him. “If you spread across the whole base, you waste. You need to target the joint that’s opening. Here.”

Jhace’s heart hammered once. He did not have time for pride.

“Mark,” he said.

Tiffani placed her left hand flat against the stone just below the opening joint. “Not there,” she said, shifting half a hand-width. “Here. You need the shortest path.”

Jhace set his palm over hers, skin to skin and skin to stone. The contact made the work shared. He hated that. He needed it.

The arch shuddered. An aftershock ran under the junction. The timber brace bowed. The wedge spit out grit.

Protect. Hold.

Heat flared. Copper-gold light ran into the crack network that carried load, following the line Tiffani had marked.

The crown stopped dropping.

Tiffani traced the glow’s route with chalk. “Left abutment next if it slips.”

Jhace shifted without releasing contact and pushed again. The joint tightened. Stabilized.

When he released, his right hand shook hard enough he couldn’t close his fingers. Vein-lines climbed higher than before. He tasted iron.

Tiffani watched his hands with the same expression she used on cracks in stone. “Your tremor is worse than Tide 1,” she said.

“I know,” Jhace said.

“You’re using too broad a push when you act alone,” she said. “You increase cost.”

“Stop,” Jhace said.

“If you want to keep holding structures,” Tiffani replied, “you will need to stop pretending you can do it without method.”

The crew chief stepped closer, eyes fixed on Jhace’s forearms. “Is he—”

“A worker,” Jhace snapped. “A worker doing your job because your braces are wrong.”

The chief flinched. Pride braced against need.

A second aftershock rolled under them, longer this time. The arch groaned, and a hairline crack opened along the crown. People screamed and surged. The crowd’s movement became its own load hazard.

Tiffani’s voice cut through the surge. “Clear the throat! Side run only! Move now!”

A brace worker shouted back, “They won’t listen!”

Tiffani grabbed a chalk line and snapped it across the paving, making a bright boundary. “Then give them a line,” she said. “Crowds follow lines.”

Jhace stared at the chalk mark. A simple move. Effective. A traffic control tool he never would have thought to use.

The crowd shifted, some obeying the line out of instinct, some out of superstition. It thinned the throat by a fraction, enough to reduce the load on the arch for a breath.

Jhace felt the arch’s vibration ease. He hated that he felt relief at a fraction.

The chief said, “If the Office finds you—”

“Then let them,” Tiffani said, not looking away from the arch. “They can’t brace stone with stamps.”

Jhace picked up his hammer again. This time his grip held long enough to point. “Your wedges,” he told the chief. “Drive them deeper on the right post. Your left post is off-angle. Seat it.”

The chief hesitated, then obeyed. When the wedges bit, the arch’s groan reduced again.

Tiffani moved closer to Jhace, voice low. “We need to see your failure boundary,” she said. “Not in crisis. After. Where does the tremor settle? How long does grip loss last?”

Jhace’s jaw tightened. “I don’t have time to be measured.”

“You don’t have time not to,” she replied.

He glared at her, then looked at his own hand, still shaking. The anger did not erase the truth.

When the immediate crisis eased, they moved toward the Underface stair throat. The crowd noise followed them like a wake.

In the stairwell shadow, Tiffani finally looked directly at his forearms. “The vein-lines are extending,” she said. “Your baseline is not returning to zero.”

Jhace swallowed. “What does that mean.”

“It means your cost is accumulating,” Tiffani said. “And the city will keep asking until you don’t come back from it.”

Jhace looked at the stone steps leading down into Underface. He could almost taste the tunnel dust. The Underface was familiar. The surface was hungry.

“My forge is down there,” he said, more statement than invitation.

Tiffani adjusted her satchel strap. “I know,” she said. “That’s why I’m coming.”
"""

ch7 = r"""
---

## CHAPTER 7: Quiet Calculations  
*Tide 6–7 — Observatory data chamber*

Tiffani spread her maps across the table and did not let herself look at the sky seam through the high slit windows.

The Observatory’s data chamber was built to feel detached from the city’s dirt. Smooth stone. Clean tables. Shelves for glass plates and reference charts. Even now, dust had found its way in. Dust always did.

Pella sat across from her with his ledger open. Ink stained his fingers. Fatigue deepened at the corner of his mouth. He had been awake since the first deviation readings. He looked older in a way that did not belong to age.

“How many now?” he asked.

Tiffani didn’t answer with a guess. She slid her logbook across the table and pointed to the summary column.

“Seventeen logged,” she said. “Nine direct stabilizations. Three partial holds. Five failures under non-critical conditions.”

Pella read the line twice. His eyes went to the cost column.

“Your curve,” he said quietly.

Tiffani flipped to the graph page. She had drawn it in ink with a steady hand. The line started low. It rose. Then it rose faster.

“It accelerates,” she said. “Not just per use. Per sequence. Close activations compound.”

Pella’s throat worked. “That means a few more uses and he loses function entirely.”

“He is already losing function,” Tiffani said.

She tapped her notes on the junction parapet event. “Post-use grip loss. Tremor persistence. Baseline not returning to zero.”

Pella stared at the page as if numbers could be argued out of existence. “So what are you doing,” he asked.

Tiffani’s answer came before her fear could soften it. “Method,” she said.

Pella blinked. “Method doesn’t change what it is.”

“It changes waste,” Tiffani said. “When he targets with marks, he spends less contact area. The effect is cleaner. The cost appears lower. Not erased. Lower.”

“That’s a hope,” Pella said.

“It’s a hypothesis,” Tiffani corrected. “Hypotheses can be tested.”

She turned a page to her chalk-cord notes. “Trigger conditions are consistent: immediate threat, protective need, contact. But his response when acting alone is broad. He floods the structure. It works. It also spends him.”

Pella’s eyes narrowed. “And you think you can teach him restraint.”

“I can give him references,” Tiffani said. “Lines. Targets. Timing. I can make him wait for the real trigger.”

Pella exhaled, sharp. “And he will accept that.”

“He will if the alternative is his hands becoming dead weight,” Tiffani said.

A silence sat between them, filled by the faint hum of the Observatory’s glass plates in their frames. The hum was new since the seam appeared. Pella pretended not to hear it. Tiffani did not have that luxury.

Pella finally said, “The Office came again.”

Tiffani’s jaw tightened. “I know.”

“They’re calling it Resonance Regulation,” Pella said. “They’re building a registry.”

Tiffani’s fingers tightened on the edge of her map. A registry meant ownership disguised as safety.

“And Tellis,” Pella added. “Water-Guild. He’s been at the camps. Offering barrels where people sign.”

Tiffani didn’t look up. “So the city is already carving claim-lines.”

“Yes,” Pella said. “And you’re walking toward the one person they want most.”

Tiffani closed her logbook. The sound was soft but final. “Before they get him first,” she said.

Pella leaned forward. “Tiff. Be careful.”

She almost smiled at the nickname. It was old, from before the Piercing, from before everything became cost curves and fracture maps.

“I will be accurate,” she said.

Pella’s mouth tightened. “Accuracy won’t protect you.”

“No,” Tiffani agreed. “But it will protect him long enough for us to learn what we’re dealing with.”

She gathered her maps, rolled them tight, and tied them. She packed chalk cord, wedges, her glass-sight. She left the data chamber without glancing at the seam.

In the stairwell, the city’s vibration rose into her bones again. She felt the weight of Saint Merro above. She felt the hunger of its institutions taking shape.

She whispered one phrase, not prayer, not comfort. An operational constraint.

“Hold what is real.”

Then she descended to Underface and found the forge door, heat seeping through its cracks. She steadied her pulse, then knocked.
"""

out.write_text(out.read_text(encoding="utf-8") + ch6 + ch7, encoding="utf-8")
