("INTERLUDE I: Corlexi — System vs Chaos", """Tide ~6–7 — Crown administrative chambers

Corlexi entered the Crown’s record tier and felt the pressure of too many edges.

The corridor was straight. The walls were plumb. The floor was laid in repeating squares. None of that helped. The air carried voices and paper-dust and wet ink. Sound arrived in stacked layers, each petition read aloud over another, each stamp striking over another, each boot scuffing in a different cadence. Corlexi’s skull held it all and did not filter.

He pressed two fingers to the inside of his wrist and counted pulses until the count steadied. His skin was cool. His breath stayed even. The overload was not in his body. It sat behind his eyes.

A clerk brushed past with a bundle of forms. The bundle’s corners were misaligned by less than a span. The misalignment pulled at Corlexi’s attention until he turned and watched the clerk disappear through a door marked with fresh paint:

RESONANCE REPORTS.

New letters. New authority. New boxes to fit unknown things into.

Corlexi moved deeper. He passed a row of tables where recorders sat shoulder to shoulder, each one hunched over slate or paper, each one tracing lines meant to become law. The tables were set in parallel. The stools were not. Small deviations. A stool leg shorter than the others. A recorder bracing a knee against it. The knee’s tremor transferring into the table’s surface. The tremor traveling into the ink lines.

Corlexi’s teeth tightened. He stepped to the stool, tapped the short leg with his toe, then pulled a thin wedge from his sleeve and slid it beneath the leg. He pressed down until the wobble ceased.

The recorder looked up, startled. “What—”

“Continue,” Corlexi said.

The recorder continued, hand steadier now. The ink line became straight again.

At the central desk, an overseer argued with two guild men. A third figure stood behind the overseer, quieter, cleaner, holding a new ledger that still smelled of glue.

A runner arrived and laid a pouch on the desk. Copper-gold dust sifted from the seam when it hit the wood. A few grains hovered longer than they should have, then settled along scratches, following grooves made by Anneals of writing.

Corlexi stared at the grains until his vision sharpened.

The grains did not lie. They traveled stress lines.

He reached forward before deciding to. His fingertip touched the desk near the dust. A pulse ran through his finger and into the wood.

Not a glow. Not a flare. A subtle alignment. Tiny cracks no longer drew his eye. Their edges seated closer together. The dust grains shifted, then stopped.

The runner gasped. The overseer went still. The policy man’s gaze snapped to Corlexi’s hand.

Corlexi pulled his finger back. His skin remained unmarked. No tremor followed. No heat climbed his forearm. The act left no visible invoice on him, only a sensation of rightness behind his ribs.

The policy man said, softly, “Do it again.”

Corlexi looked at him. “No.”

The overseer blinked. “You can’t refuse. This is Crown work.”

Corlexi scanned the room. He saw the grid of tables. The doors. The lines of people waiting with papers that represented food, shelter, timber allocations, burial permissions.

The system was trying to form around a force it did not understand. It would squeeze until the force fit.

It would also squeeze the people inside it.

Corlexi spoke without raising his voice. “You are building a cage. You are calling it order.”

The policy man’s jaw tightened. “Order keeps the city from falling.”

Corlexi looked toward the windows. The sky seam cut the pale light into unequal halves. The seam was straight. The world beneath it was not.

Order could not be declared. It had to be seated, joint by joint, under load.

Corlexi stepped away from the desk and stopped before the door marked RESONANCE REPORTS. He put his palm against the doorframe. Stone carried a fine vibration, constant since the Piercing.

He lifted his hand and left without looking back, carrying a conclusion clean and hard:

If they tried to force the world into rectangles, the world would break their rectangles."""),
("CHAPTER 8: Proof and Resistance", """Tide 7–8 — Jhace’s forge, then a joint inspection site

Jhace kept his hands busy because stillness made the tremor feel louder.

The forge’s heat had settled into a steady breath. The bellows line held. The quench trough sat half-full, water dulled by ash and grit. He had reshaped three brackets since Kindling and each one came out with the same faint warp—small, tolerable, proof that his grip was no longer his alone.

He set a rivet on the anvil block, aligned it by touch, and brought the hammer down.

The hammer head struck true. His wrist did not.

The rivet skittered off the block and clicked against the stone. The sound was small. The shame was not.

Tiffani arrived with chalk cord and proof instead of comfort. Bram insisted on going with them.

They went to a covered corridor that connected two refugee blocks. The corridor’s roof had been patched with salvaged sheet and glass panels. The supports were timber braces married to stone posts that were not designed for wood.

The brace nearest the midpoint bowed a hair under load.

Tiffani snapped chalk cord across the seam and traced the visible stress line. “To you,” she said. “Contact here. Not on the whole face. On the seam.”

Jhace pressed his palm to the seam between post and brace and waited for trigger. Tiffani made him wait until the post slipped and the roof crackled.

Then copper-gold heat flooded his palm. The glow ran into the seam in narrow channels, following the failure path Tiffani had marked. The brace stopped bowing. The post stopped slipping.

Jhace pulled his hand back.

The tremor rose in his wrist, then held. It did not climb into his forearm. He could still curl his fingers.

“Visible,” Tiffani said, looking at his vein-lines. “But contained.”

Proof. Repeatable. A small margin carved out of a system that wanted to spend him fast.

On the walk back, Jhace said, “Next crisis. You mark. I hold. We see if it repeats.”

“It will repeat,” Tiffani said. “If you let it.”"""),
("CHAPTER 9: Ash and Claims", """Tide 10–12 — Glass Quarter refugee camp

The Glass Quarter had a smell that never fully left: ash, wet grit, old heat trapped in brick.

Now the camp sat in that smell. Tents and patched canopies filled the spaces where ovens once stood. Broken panes were stacked into crude windbreaks. People moved through the lanes in slow Strides, careful with weight because the ground still shifted in small aftershocks that made the debris talk.

Jhace hated camps. They were structure without design. Load without plan.

Tiffani moved beside him, eyes always tracking supports, brace angles, rope ties. Her logbook was strapped tight against her ribs.

They reached a distribution line where water cups were being filled from a barrel.

Tellis stood near the barrel with two of his people, clean coats that looked wrong in this place. Corvin stood with him, coat dusted, posture composed, ledger in hand.

“We can stabilize this district,” Corvin said. “We can bring timber. Iron. Skilled crews. We can redirect water routes. We can enforce load limits.”

“And you,” Tellis added, “can stop crawling through rubble for free.”

The offer had strings: registration, scheduled deployment, authority, reporting.

Jhace and Tiffani stepped aside to consult. He didn’t want a leash. She didn’t want the method owned.

They returned to the makeshift table.

“I’m not registering,” Jhace said.

“We decline,” Tiffani said.

Corvin rolled his map back up. “Then you are choosing chaos,” he said.

Jhace held his gaze. “I’m choosing to keep my hands mine.”

They walked away with enemies behind them and ash under their boots—line drawn, pressure rising."""),
("CHAPTER 10: Near Collapse", """Tide 14–15 — Saint Merro main support structure crisis zone

The main support failed with a sound that did not belong to stone.

It began as a low grind through the pavement under Jhace’s boots, then a sharp crack that ran up the nearest column. Dust burst from a joint high overhead. A brace plate popped free and clattered down the steps.

People screamed.

Jhace saw the structure in pieces at once: viaduct span packed with evacuees, column cluster holding the upper terrace, abutment patched twice since the Piercing. A load path had shifted. The shift was running.

Tiffani was already moving. Chalk in hand. “Clear the run!” she shouted. “Off the center lane now!”

Jhace ran to the first column, pressed his palm to the opening crack, and waited for trigger.

Trigger arrived immediately. The viaduct above shifted. A child slid toward an edge.

Protective need hit Jhace’s chest like a hammer.

Copper-gold heat flooded his palm. He pushed into the seam, narrow and targeted. The joint locked long enough for the span to settle and a stranger to haul the child back.

“Next,” Tiffani said close.

They moved joint to joint. Mark. Contact. Hold. Release.

The structure kept failing anyway.

A secondary crack opened along the abutment. The upper terrace began to drop by a span.

Jhace ran to the main column cluster and slammed both palms onto stone.

Copper-gold surged into both arms. He tried to hold the entire terrace alone.

Tiffani saw the spread climb toward his elbow. Blood appeared at his nostril. Sensory overload edged in.

“Release in pulses,” she said. “Two breaths. Then off.”

He snarled, “No.”

She grabbed his wrist, forced a fractional break in contact. The surge stuttered. He gasped.

She counted anyway. She redirected brace crews to side supports, drove wedges into a lower bed seam, and bought a new margin.

“Off. Now. Full release,” she said.

Jhace pulled away. The terrace held on new braces, groaning but not dropping.

He swayed. Full-body tremor. Vein-lines pulsed at rest.

“You made me let go,” he said.

“Yes,” Tiffani replied. “Because you will not be a martyr for your own compulsion.”

Fury stayed between them, but so did method. They went back into the dust to keep directing bodies away from seams that wanted to fail."""),
("INTERLUDE II: Mobel — Purity Rising", """Tide ~15–16 — Entry-side coast

Mobel trusted pain.

Pain stripped error from the body. It drew attention to the exact place where failure began.

He knelt at the salt pans where stone trays stepped down toward the sea. The Piercing’s far tremor had shifted the basins. Seams opened. Brine leaked into soil and left crusted white lines that spread in branching paths.

Impurity traveled through openings.

Mobel pressed his thumb into a crack until the sharp edge bit skin. He welcomed the bite.

Behind him, voices rose. Two men dragged a third across grit and dropped him near the pans. The man convulsed, foam at his lips, hands clawing, wrists shaking in short pulses.

“He touched the gold-dust,” someone said.

“He tried to hold a wall with his hands,” another said.

Mobel crouched beside him and watched for pattern. Tremor, cough, breath hitch, wrong heat.

The man rasped, “Help.”

Mobel asked, “What did you take in?”

“I… breathed it,” the man said. “I thought it would make me strong.”

Strength without method produced waste. Waste produced collapse.

Mobel stood and spoke to the ring of onlookers. “Bring boiling brine. Bring clean cloth. No reused rags.”

The brine arrived steaming. Mobel took the vessel without cloth. Heat licked his palms. Pain confirmed control.

He poured a thin stream over the man’s wrists and scrubbed until copper-gold grains washed away into sand. The man screamed and thrashed. Mobel did not stop when he begged. Begging did not change contamination.

When convulsions eased, Mobel rinsed his hands in seawater. Salt stung. He welcomed it.

He looked at the seam on the horizon and felt pressures behind it—alignment, binding, loosening—none of them named, all of them real.

He carried a rule out of the crowd’s hungry expectation:

Clean first. Always."""),
("CHAPTER 11: Holding Together", """Tide 17–18 — Jhace’s forge beneath Saint Merro

The forge had never been quiet, but it knew the difference between noise and threat.

This Quench it was only noise: the low breath of the heat-grid, the soft tick of cooling iron, the faint drip from a pipe seam Bram refused to replace because “it still holds.”

Jhace sat on the floor with his back against the forge wall because benches required balance and he did not have enough of that left to waste. His hands lay in his lap, palms up, as if he were waiting for someone to place a tool there.

Nothing was placed.

The tremor ran anyway. Constant. A fine shiver at the edges of his fingers.

Bram slept on the bench with his boots still on. Jhace hated that he checked Bram’s breath.

The door creaked. Measured Stride.

Tiffani stepped inside, carrying clean cloth, salve, and a wax-wrapped needle.

Jhace’s jaw tightened. “I’m not bleeding.”

“You were,” Tiffani said. “Your skin is too hot.”

“Hands,” she said.

Jhace lifted them. The tremor made the movement sloppy.

Tiffani wrapped his palms and wrists, calm and competent. Calm made it worse. Calm meant she expected him to survive long enough for this to matter.

“What are you doing here?” Jhace asked.

“You almost died,” she said.

He glared. “I didn’t.”

“You tried,” she replied.

Jhace finally spoke the count he carried: names from the kiln hall fall, Anneals ago. Twenty-three. Bram’s brother among them.

“I don’t let ‘later’ exist,” Jhace said. “When something starts to go, I hold. Because I won’t add another name.”

Tiffani answered with her own: Marisse. Wrongly dismissed. Heard under rubble. Left because Tiffani was afraid the structure would take her too.

“I keep her name,” Tiffani said. “Because I left her.”

Silence sat heavy in the forge.

Jhace shifted closer and placed his bandaged palm over Tiffani’s wrist—lightly. Contact, non-crisis. A test of trust.

Tiffani curled her fingers around his wrist. Her grip was steady. It held his tremor without pretending it wasn’t there.

“I don’t know how to stop holding,” Jhace admitted.

“Then don’t,” Tiffani said. “Just stop holding alone.”

“Stay,” Jhace said.

Tiffani stayed.

When sleep took him, it wasn’t because fear was gone.

It was because, for the first time since the kiln hall fall, his weight was not held by his hands alone."""),
("CHAPTER 12: Choosing the Weight", """Tide 19–20 — Saint Merro lanes, then the forge wall

Saint Merro looked stitched.

Timber braces crossed cracked facades like crude bandage. Iron straps cinched pillars that had no business still standing. Fresh mortar gleamed pale against old soot-dark stone. The city held itself together by sheer density of effort.

Tiffani walked with Jhace along a lane that used to be a market run. Now it was a supply corridor.

Clean-coated watchers moved at their pace. Office hands, tracking.

A posted notice hung from a brace post with a fresh seal:

ALL RESONANT INCIDENTS MUST BE REPORTED. ALL USERS MUST REGISTER.

Across the lane, water barrels moved under guard into secured courtyards. Allocation wearing polite coats.

Tiffani and Jhace climbed to a small overlook above the forge district. From there the seam in the sky was visible without trying—straight, pale, indifferent.

“We don’t have control,” Tiffani said quietly.

“We never did,” Jhace replied.

“But we can choose what we hold,” she said.

“And what we don’t,” Jhace answered.

Back at the forge, people gathered without ceremony: Bram, Pella, brace workers, the girl Jhace had dragged from Underface.

Tiffani handed Jhace a chunk of chalk. His fingers shook. Chalk dust fell.

Bram said, “Three. Not two. Not ten. Three holds.”

Jhace wrote, letters rough from tremor:

HOLD WHAT IS REAL.  
NAME THE COST.  
CHOOSE TOGETHER.

Tiffani added a simple mark beneath: two hands linked at the wrists, one trembling, one steady.

A brace worker touched the wall lightly. The other followed. The rescued girl placed her fingertips on the lowest line, then pulled back like she’d touched heat.

Bram grunted. “Good.”

Jhace took Tiffani’s hand. She held his tremor without trying to erase it.

Outside, the city still groaned. The Office still posted. The Guild still carried water where it chose.

Inside, three lines on soot-black stone made a structure they could stand under.

Together, facing what came next."""),
("EPILOGUE: The Auditor — The Not-Room", """Tide 20 — Saint Merro / Underface

The Auditor entered the space that refused the name room.

Stone surrounded it. Underface carried weight above it. Heat mains ran near enough that a normal chamber would have held warmth in its joints. This space held none of that. Sound arrived dulled. Vibration arrived cut. The floor took the Auditor’s boot and gave nothing back.

A slate appeared in the Auditor’s hands and was set down where a table should have been. No surface declared itself. The slate remained set.

Three slates followed, placed in a line, edges aligned by fingertip distance. The alignment held. The place permitted record, not structure.

Copper-gold dust hung at the center. It did not fall. It did not rise. It gathered into thin planes and cords that mapped stress without material. The cords tightened, then separated into three impressions.

The first impression formed around ink, seals, and squared stone.

Corlexi moved through Crown chambers where tables sat in grids and stools sat wrong. The impression sharpened on the moments he corrected: a wobbling leg wedged, a ledger squared to an inlay, a doorframe touched. The touch shifted a crack’s edge tighter by a fraction and left no visible cost on skin. No tremor followed. No heat climbed. The act still changed the load behavior.

The second impression formed around salt, scalding steam, and hands that did not flinch.

Mobel stood at a coastal pan where stone trays had opened seams. He seated wedges with controlled taps. He poured boiling brine over a convulsing man’s wrists and scrubbed until copper-gold grains washed away. Pain registered in the body and did not interrupt the sequence. The crowd’s fear aligned behind him, reshaping into appetite for rule.

The third impression did not begin with place. It began with linkage.

Jhace Torrin’s hands trembled at rest. The tremor ran through fingers and into wrists, present even with empty palms. Faint copper-gold vein-lines remained visible under skin without contact. His body carried the invoice forward.

Beside him, Tiffani’s presence acted as constraint. She reduced waste. She narrowed his holds to true failure paths. She interrupted the caretaker compulsion that pushed him past safe limits. She did not erase cost. She made cost survivable.

The Auditor’s hand hovered above the first slate.

Then the voice changed, not as announcement, but as the natural point where observation became record.

I wrote the first line clean and short: Three vectors confirmed.

I kept the next lines anchored to what the world had shown, not what the world might mean.

Vector One — Seating. Corlexi’s mind sought hierarchy in joints and grids. He treated deviation as threat. He treated compliance as stability. He refused overt command once, and the refusal did not end the system’s hunger. It redirected it toward finding a more compliant hand.

Vector Two — Cleansing. Mobel’s mind sought correction through removal. He treated contamination as moral fact. He used pain as process control. A crowd accepted the sequence and began to mirror it. When fear becomes instruction, instruction becomes authority.

Vector Three — Bracing. Jhace and Tiffani formed a composite brace. Load redistributed between them. Their method reduced waste under activation and delayed failure. Their bond remained functional, not ornamental. Their refusal of ownership increased external pressure.

Above, Saint Merro held by timber and iron and exhausted hands. Aftershocks continued. Cracks that had been patched opened again along old planes.

The Guild tightened route control. Water moved under guard. Tools moved with stamped permission. Supply became leverage.

The Church gathered language. Language became ritual. Ritual became claim.

The Crown formed new offices with new doors and new seals. Lists became mandates. Mandates reached for bodies that could be named, tracked, contained.

None of those structures cared about the true mechanic behind copper-gold holds. They cared that the holds worked often enough to be believed. Belief produced demand. Demand produced pressure.

I returned my attention to the third impression.

Jhace’s trajectory had already bent. Stage 1 baseline remained visible in tremor and vein-lines. Warning signs had appeared during mass-load events: overheating skin, blood at the nostril, sensory overload edging in when scale exceeded method.

Tiffani would slow that bend. She would not stop it. The cost model did not allow clean victory. It allowed only management, triage, and refusal to waste.

Unpredictability remained threat and asset.

A registered hand could be scheduled. A leased hand could be deployed. A feared hand could be erased.

An unclaimed hand could not be cleanly used.

I wrote the last section as prediction without specifics, staying inside the gate.

Near-term outcomes:
- Increased attempts to capture method through policy, supply, and theology.
- Increased untrained grasping by civilians, driven by rumor and fear.
- Increased pressure on the composite brace to submit, relocate, or vanish.
- Increased probability of cost escalation events when mass-load crises recur.

Before I left, I drew one mark beneath the text: three lines meeting at a joint, each line carrying load in a different direction.

The copper-gold dust planes broke apart into drifting grains. The grains remained suspended, waiting for new patterns to map.

I stepped back into the Underface where heat returned, where vibration returned, where the city’s weight returned all at once.

Saint Merro continued to set under load."""),
]

# Build full manuscript list
manuscript = [
("CHAPTER 1: Something Wrong", ch1),
("CHAPTER 2: Glass That Listens", ch2),
("CHAPTER 3: The Sky Breaks", ch3),
("CHAPTER 4: After the Shudder", ch4),
]
manuscript.extend(rest_sections)

# Create DOCX
doc = Document()
style = doc.styles['Normal']
style.font.name = 'Garamond'
style._element.rPr.rFonts.set(qn('w:eastAsia'), 'Garamond')
style.font.size = Pt(11)

title = doc.add_paragraph("CONFLUENCE CHRONICLES")
title.runs[0].bold = True
title.runs[0].font.size = Pt(20)
title.alignment = WD_ALIGN_PARAGRAPH.CENTER

subtitle = doc.add_paragraph("Novella N01 — Manuscript (Piercing-Consistent Pass)")
subtitle.runs[0].italic = True
subtitle.runs[0].font.size = Pt(14)
subtitle.alignment = WD_ALIGN_PARAGRAPH.CENTER

doc.add_paragraph("Te’Oga — Saint Merro").alignment = WD_ALIGN_PARAGRAPH.CENTER
doc.add_page_break()

def add_section(heading, body):
    h = doc.add_paragraph(heading)
    h.style = doc.styles['Heading 1']
    for para in body.split("\n\n"):
        p = doc.add_paragraph(para)
        p.style = doc.styles['Normal']

for heading, body in manuscript:
    add_section(heading, body)
    doc.add_page_break()

docx_path = "/mnt/data/N01_Manuscript_PiercingPass.docx"
doc.save(docx_path)

# Create Markdown
md_lines = ["# CONFLUENCE CHRONICLES", "_Novella N01 — Manuscript (Piercing-Consistent Pass)_", "Te’Oga — Saint Merro", ""]
for heading, body in manuscript:
    md_lines.append(f"# {heading}")
    md_lines.append("")
    md_lines.append(body)
    md_lines.append("")
md_path = "/mnt/data/N01_Manuscript_PiercingPass.md"
with open(md_path, "w", encoding="utf-8") as f:
    f.write("\n".join(md_lines))

(docx_path, md_path)
